> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above use the alphanumeric prefixing convention (e.g., `nodeA`, `p1`, `step1`) to comply with KTU diagram rendering standards and avoid reserved-keyword conflicts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define the Triple Bottom Line. Mention its three pillars. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) is a sustainability framework proposed by **John Elkington (1994)** that measures an organization's performance across three dimensions: **People (Social)**, **Planet (Environmental)**, and **Profit (Economic)**. Unlike traditional accounting, which reports only financial profit, TBL asserts that true corporate success must also include social equity and environmental stewardship. The three pillars are:

- **People (Social):** Labor welfare, human rights, community development.
- **Planet (Environmental):** Resource use, emissions, waste, ecosystem impact.
- **Profit (Economic):** Financial returns, market competitiveness, economic value creation.

(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark three pillars + 1 mark brief expansion)

---

**Q2. List any six key indicators used to measure the Social Pillar of TBL in a manufacturing firm. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

Six Social (People) indicators in manufacturing are:

1. **Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)** — Worker safety
2. **Average Training Hours per Employee** — Skill development
3. **Percentage of Female Workforce** — Diversity and inclusion
4. **Wage-to-Living-Wage Ratio** — Fair compensation
5. **Employee Turnover Rate** — Workforce stability
6. **Community Investment as % of Profit** — Social contribution

(3 marks: 6 × 0.5 = 3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the Triple Bottom Line concept in detail with reference to sustainable manufacturing. Discuss the role of each of the three pillars with suitable manufacturing examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit emits 800 kg of particulate matter annually against a regulatory benchmark of 1000 kg. The firm's social index is 0.65 (weight 0.3) and economic index is 0.85 (weight 0.4). Calculate the composite TBL score if the environmental weight is 0.3. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Triple Bottom Line (TBL), coined by **John Elkington (1994)**, is a holistic sustainability framework that evaluates a manufacturing firm on three pillars:

**1. People (Social Pillar):** Focuses on labor practices, occupational health and safety, fair wages, diversity, and community welfare. *Manufacturing Example:* A factory implementing ISO 45001 certification ensures worker safety, reducing LTIFR by 40%.

**2. Planet (Environmental Pillar):** Addresses resource consumption, energy use, emissions, waste, and ecological footprint. *Manufacturing Example:* A textile mill adopting zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD) systems reuses 90% of process water, reducing freshwater intake.

**3. Profit (Economic Pillar):** Captures financial returns, cost efficiency, and market competitiveness. *Manufacturing Example:* A car manufacturer using lightweight recycled aluminum reduces material cost by 15% while improving fuel efficiency.

The integration of these three pillars enables a manufacturing firm to achieve **long-term resilience**, **regulatory compliance**, and **stakeholder trust**, forming the backbone of **Sustainable Manufacturing**.

(Marks: 1+1+1 for definitions, 3 × 1 for examples, 1 for integration summary = 7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Compute the environmental index $P_{env}$.**

$$
P_{env} = 1 - \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{benchmark}} = 1 - \frac{800}{1000} = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2
$$

**Step 2: State the given values.**

- $P_{econ} = 0.85$, $w_1 = 0.4$
- $P_{soc} = 0.65$, $w_2 = 0.3$
- $P_{env} = 0.20$, $w_3 = 0.3$

**Step 3: Compute the composite TBL score.**

$$
S_{TBL} = w_1 P_{econ} + w_2 P_{soc} + w_3 P_{env}
$$

$$
S_{TBL} = (0.4)(0.85) + (0.3)(0.65) + (0.3)(0.20)
$$

$$
S_{TBL} = 0.340 + 0.195 + 0.060
$$

$$
S_{TBL} = 0.595
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

The composite TBL score is **0.595**, indicating **moderate sustainability**. The environmental pillar is critically weak ($P_{env} = 0.20$) and requires immediate investment in emission-control technologies such as electrostatic precipitators or cyclone separators.

(Marks: 2 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks)

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Compare the Traditional Bottom Line approach with the Triple Bottom Line approach. Discuss at least four key differences with manufacturing-specific examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm has the following TBL data: Profit = ₹50 Lakhs, CO\textsubscript{2} Emission = 200 tons/year, Workforce = 500. Normalize the three pillars to a 0–1 scale using benchmarks: ₹80 Lakhs, 100 tons/year, 800 employees. Compute the weighted TBL score using weights 0.5, 0.3, 0.2 for economic, environmental, and social respectively. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Aspect | Traditional Bottom Line | Triple Bottom Line |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Focus** | Profit maximization only | People, Planet, Profit balance |
| **Stakeholders** | Shareholders | Shareholders, workers, community, environment |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term quarterly gains | Long-term intergenerational equity |
| **Reporting** | Financial statements only | Integrated sustainability reports (GRI, ESG) |
| **Risk View** | Market and credit risk only | Includes environmental, social, and reputational risk |
| **Example** | Cutting safety budget to boost profit | Investing in safety to retain workforce and reduce liability |

*Manufacturing Examples:*
- **Traditional:** A factory outsources production to a low-wage country purely for cost.
- **TBL:** A factory balances cost with fair-labor audits and carbon-neutral logistics.

(Marks: 4 differences × 1.5 = 6 marks + 1 mark for overall conclusion = 7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Normalize the Economic Index.**

$$
P_{econ} = \frac{\text{Actual Profit}}{\text{Benchmark Profit}} = \frac{50}{80} = 0.625
$$

**Step 2: Normalize the Environmental Index.**

For emissions, lower is better, so invert the ratio:

$$
P_{env} = 1 - \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{benchmark}} = 1 - \frac{200}{100} = 1 - 2.0 = -1.0
$$

Since negative scores are not valid, we cap at 0:

$$
P_{env} = \max\left(0,\ 1 - \frac{200}{100}\right) = 0
$$

**Step 3: Normalize the Social Index.**

For workforce capacity, more employees may indicate social contribution, but for normalization we use a fair-employment ratio. Assume workforce meets 500/800 of social benchmark:

$$
P_{soc} = \frac{500}{800} = 0.625
$$

**Step 4: Apply weights.**

- $w_1 = 0.5$ (Economic), $w_2 = 0.3$ (Environmental), $w_3 = 0.2$ (Social)

**Step 5: Compute $S_{TBL}$.**

$$
S_{TBL} = (0.5)(0.625) + (0.3)(0) + (0.2)(0.625)
$$

$$
S_{TBL} = 0.3125 + 0 + 0.125
$$

$$
S_{TBL} = 0.4375
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation.**

The TBL score is **0.4375** (below average). The firm is **doubly over-emitting** ($P_{env} = 0$), signaling urgent need for carbon capture or process electrification.

(Marks: 6 × 1 = 6 marks + 1 mark for interpretation = 7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TBL with CSR:** CSR is a *subset* activity; TBL is a *full reporting framework* covering all operations.
> 2. **Forgetting Weight Constraint:** Always verify $\sum w_i = 1$; partial marks are cut for violations.
> 3. **Inverting Environmental Metrics Incorrectly:** For "lower-is-better" metrics like emissions, use $1 - \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{benchmark}}$, not the direct ratio.
> 4. **Ignoring Negative Scores:** When actual emission exceeds benchmark, cap the index at 0, not leave it negative.
> 5. **Forgetting Examples:** KTU examiners allocate ~30% of marks for **manufacturing-specific examples** in 14-mark answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TBL = People + Planet + Profit** (Elkington, 1994)
- **People (Social):** Worker welfare, community, fair labor
- **Planet (Environmental):** Energy, emissions, waste, resources
- **Profit (Economic):** Cost, revenue, market value, ROI
- **Composite TBL formula:** $S_{TBL} = \sum w_i P_i$ subject to $\sum w_i = 1$
- **Environmental normalization for "lower-is-better" metrics:** $P_{env} = 1 - \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{benchmark}}$ with $\max(0, \cdot)$ capping
- **Three-leg stool analogy** is a high-yield exam illustration
- **TBL ≠ CSR** — TBL is broader and integrated
- **Minimum weight safeguard:** $w_i \geq 0.1$ ensures no pillar is ignored
- **Real-world TBL wins:** Lean manufacturing, green supply chain, Industry 4.0
- **Standard industrial indicators:** LTIFR, SEC, CF, RR, EVA, GWP
- **Always normalize KPIs to 0–1 scale** before aggregation
- **Always state interpretation** after numerical computation in KTU answers
- **Mapping to SDGs:** TBL aligns with UN Sustainable Development Goals (especially SDG 8, 12, 13)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental, Economic and Social Dimensions of Sustainability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environmental, Economic and Social Dimensions of Sustainability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Formal Definition of Sustainability

**Sustainability** is formally defined (per the **Brundtland Commission Report, 1987 - "Our Common Future"**) as:

> *"Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*

In the context of **Sustainable Manufacturing**, this definition expands to a production philosophy that seeks to create manufactured products using **processes that are non-polluting, conserve energy and natural resources, and are economically viable, safe for employees, communities, and consumers**, while remaining socially and ethically responsible throughout the entire product life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The three dimensions — **Environmental**, **Economic**, and **Social** — together form the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** or **People–Planet–Profit (3P) Framework**, originally proposed by **John Elkington (1994)**. This is the most frequently asked concept in KTU ESE Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Three-Legged Stool

Imagine **sustainability as a three-legged wooden stool**:
- **Leg 1 (Environmental / Planet)**: Represents ecological integrity — clean air, water, biodiversity, and responsible resource use.
- **Leg 2 (Economic / Profit)**: Represents financial viability — profitable operations, market competitiveness, and long-term economic resilience.
- **Leg 3 (Social / People)**: Represents human well-being — fair labor, community health, equity, and safety.

> If **even one leg breaks or becomes shorter**, the stool collapses. A factory may generate massive revenue (strong economic leg) but if it pollutes a river (weak environmental leg) and exploits workers (weak social leg), the system is fundamentally **unsustainable** — it will fail over time, even if short-term profits look attractive.

## 1.3 The Three Dimensions — Definitions

### A. Environmental Dimension (Planet / Ecology)
The environmental dimension deals with the **impact of manufacturing activities on natural ecosystems**. It focuses on conserving raw materials, energy, and water; minimizing emissions, effluents, and waste; and protecting biodiversity. Core metrics include **Carbon Footprint (kg CO₂-eq)**, **Water Footprint (litres/unit)**, **Energy Intensity (MJ/kg)**, and **E-waste generation (tonnes/year)**.

### B. Economic Dimension (Profit / Prosperity)
The economic dimension ensures that sustainable practices are **financially viable** for businesses, customers, and society. It covers cost reduction through efficiency, green innovation, circular economy revenues, ROI on sustainable investments, and long-term competitiveness. Key indicators include **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)**, **Return on Sustainability Investment (ROSI)**, and **Green Revenue Share (%)**.

### C. Social Dimension (People / Equity)
The social dimension addresses the **impact of manufacturing on people** — workers, local communities, consumers, and society at large. It encompasses occupational health and safety (OHS), fair wages, gender equity, human rights, community development, and product responsibility. Indicators include **Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)**, **Employee Satisfaction Index**, and **Community Investment Ratio**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong vs. Weak Sustainability**: KTU students often confuse these. **Weak Sustainability** assumes that natural capital can be substituted by man-made capital (e.g., a deforested area replaced by a steel factory is acceptable). **Strong Sustainability** insists that natural capital cannot be wholly substituted and must be preserved. Most modern sustainability frameworks (including the UN SDGs) lean toward **Strong Sustainability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Framework

The TBL framework evaluates sustainability performance across **three interconnected pillars**. A sustainable manufacturing system must achieve an **acceptable balance** across all three dimensions simultaneously.

| Pillar | Core Question | Engineering Concern | Sample Metric |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Environmental (Planet)** | Are we operating within planetary boundaries? | Resource depletion, emissions, waste | kg CO₂-eq per unit |
| **Economic (Profit)** | Is the operation financially viable long-term? | Cost, revenue, ROI, market share | LCC in ₹/unit |
| **Social (People)** | Are stakeholders (workers, community) better off? | Safety, wages, equity, community | LTIFR per million hours |

## 2.2 Pillars Explained — Engineering Logic

### Environmental Dimension (Why and How)
- **Why**: Earth has finite resources. Manufacturing is the largest single consumer of materials and energy (~50% of global CO₂ emissions).
- **How**: Implement cleaner production, renewable energy, closed-loop material cycles, eco-design, and life-cycle assessment (LCA).
- **Sub-domains**: 
  - *Climate action* (GHG reduction)
  - *Circular resource use* (recycling, remanufacturing)
  - *Pollution prevention* (air, water, soil)
  - *Biodiversity protection*

### Economic Dimension (Why and How)
- **Why**: A sustainable system that is not profitable will collapse financially and be abandoned.
- **How**: Adopt lean manufacturing, green procurement, energy-efficient technologies, and circular business models (Product-as-a-Service).
- **Sub-domains**:
  - *Cost optimization*
  - *Green innovation and patents*
  - *Long-term value creation*
  - *Risk mitigation*

### Social Dimension (Why and How)
- **Why**: Manufacturing employs ~14% of global workforce. Unsafe or exploitative practices cause social unrest, litigation, and brand damage.
- **How**: Implement OHS standards (ISO 45001), fair labor practices (ILO conventions), CSR activities, and stakeholder engagement.
- **Sub-domains**:
  - *Worker safety and health*
  - *Human rights and labor equity*
  - *Community welfare*
  - *Consumer protection*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated mathematical toolkit for this module. Every formula here is examinable in either Part A (formula recall) or Part B (numerical application).

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Unit | Engineering Application |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{output}}}$ | kWh/kg or MJ/kg | Energy benchmarking in factories |
| 2 | **Carbon Footprint (CF)** | $\text{CF} = \sum_{i} (m_i \times \text{EF}_i)$ | kg CO₂-eq | GHG inventory (Scope 1+2+3) |
| 3 | **Water Footprint (WF)** | $\text{WF} = \text{WF}_{\text{blue}} + \text{WF}_{\text{green}} + \text{WF}_{\text{grey}}$ | litres/unit | Total water accountability |
| 4 | **Material Efficiency (ME)** | $\text{ME} = \dfrac{M_{\text{useful product}}}{M_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | % | Lean / zero-waste target |
| 5 | **Recycling Rate (RR)** | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{recycled}}}{M_{\text{total waste}}} \times 100$ | % | Circularity KPI |
| 6 | **Return on Sustainability Investment (ROSI)** | $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Savings} - \text{Investment}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Justifying green capex |
| 7 | **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** | $\text{LCC} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹ | Long-term project economics |
| 8 | **LTIFR (Safety)** | $\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{\text{Lost-time injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total hours worked}}$ | per million hours | OHS performance |
| 9 | **Sustainability Index (Composite)** | $\text{SI} = w_E E_n + w_P P_n + w_S S_n$ | dimensionless (0–1) | TBL aggregation |
| 10 | **Ecological Footprint (EF)** | $\text{EF} = \text{Population} \times \dfrac{\text{Resource use}}{\text{Biocapacity}}$ | global hectares (gha) | Planetary boundary check |

Where:
- $m_i$ = mass of GHG $i$, $\text{EF}_i$ = emission factor of GHG $i$
- $E_n, P_n, S_n$ = normalized scores; $w_E + w_P + w_S = 1$ (weightages)
- $C_t$ = cost in year $t$, $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = life in years

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Environmental KPIs** feed directly into **Environmental Management Systems (ISO 14001)** and **Carbon Disclosure Reports**.
- **Economic KPIs** are integrated into **Capital Budgeting** and **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) ratings** used by investors.
- **Social KPIs** align with **GRI Standards (Global Reporting Initiative)** and **SA8000** certifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment**: This module directly satisfies **CO1** of the OESFT612 syllabus — *"Illustrate the concepts and principles of sustainable manufacturing."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Normalized Sustainability Index (SI) Using TBL

The **Sustainability Index (SI)** is a composite score used to benchmark a manufacturing unit's overall sustainability performance by combining all three dimensions.

### Step 1: Define Raw Scores for Each Dimension
Let the raw performance values be:
- Environmental score $E_r$ (e.g., kg CO₂-eq/unit — lower is better)
- Economic score $P_r$ (e.g., ₹/unit profit — higher is better)
- Social score $S_r$ (e.g., LTIFR — lower is better)

### Step 2: Normalize Each Score to a 0–1 Scale (Min–Max Normalization)
For indicators where **higher is better** (e.g., profit):
$$
E_n = \dfrac{E_r - E_{\min}}{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}
$$

For indicators where **lower is better** (e.g., emissions):
$$
P_n = \dfrac{P_{\max} - P_r}{P_{\max} - P_{\min}}
$$

The same rule applies to the social dimension:
$$
S_n = \dfrac{S_{\max} - S_r}{S_{\max} - S_{\min}}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Stakeholder Weightages
The aggregated Sustainability Index is the weighted sum:
$$
\text{SI} = w_E \cdot E_n + w_P \cdot P_n + w_S \cdot S_n
$$

Subject to the constraint:
$$
w_E + w_P + w_S = 1
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation
- $\text{SI} \to 1$: Excellent sustainability
- $\text{SI} \in [0.5, 0.8]$: Acceptable / improving
- $\text{SI} < 0.5$: Critical — redesign required

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A manufacturing unit reports the following monthly KPIs. Compute the composite Sustainability Index.

| Dimension | Raw Value | Industry Best | Industry Worst | Weight |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Environmental (kg CO₂-eq/unit) ↓ better | 4.0 | 2.0 | 8.0 | 0.4 |
| Economic (₹ profit/unit) ↑ better | 120 | 200 | 50 | 0.35 |
| Social (LTIFR per M-hr) ↓ better | 2.5 | 1.0 | 5.0 | 0.25 |

### Step 1: Environmental Normalization (lower is better)
$$
E_n = \dfrac{8.0 - 4.0}{8.0 - 2.0} = \dfrac{4.0}{6.0} = 0.6667
$$

*Logic*: Subtract current value from max, divide by range. Higher $E_n$ = greener performance.

### Step 2: Economic Normalization (higher is better)
$$
P_n = \dfrac{120 - 50}{200 - 50} = \dfrac{70}{150} = 0.4667
$$

### Step 3: Social Normalization (lower is better)
$$
S_n = \dfrac{5.0 - 2.5}{5.0 - 1.0} = \dfrac{2.5}{4.0} = 0.625
$$

### Step 4: Composite SI
$$
\text{SI} = (0.4 \times 0.6667) + (0.35 \times 0.4667) + (0.25 \times 0.625)
$$

$$
\text{SI} = 0.2667 + 0.1633 + 0.1563
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SI} = 0.5863 \approx 0.59}
$$

**Interpretation**: The unit is in the **"Acceptable / Improving"** band. Environmental performance is good, but **economic profitability** is the weakest dimension and warrants managerial attention.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Break-Even on Sustainability Investment

To justify an investment in green technology, the **Annual Net Savings (A)** from reduced energy/waste must outweigh the **Annualized Capital Cost (C)**.

$$
\text{Net Annual Benefit} = A - C
$$

Where:
$$
C = \dfrac{I \cdot r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}
$$

This is the standard **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**. Here, $I$ = initial investment, $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = project life.

The **Payback Period (PP)** for sustainability investments is:
$$
\text{PP} = \dfrac{I}{A}
$$

A project is acceptable if $\text{PP} < \text{Target payback period (usually 3–5 years)}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs Between Dimensions

| Scenario | Environmental Impact | Economic Impact | Social Impact | Net Verdict |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Switching to solar power | $\downarrow$ emissions | $\uparrow$ capex, $\downarrow$ long-run cost | $\uparrow$ green jobs | Sustainable |
| Outsourcing to low-wage country | $\uparrow$ transport emissions | $\downarrow$ labor cost | $\downarrow$ local jobs, possible labor rights issues | Mixed / Risky |
| Using recycled raw material | $\downarrow$ extraction impact | $\downarrow$ input cost (often) | $\uparrow$ recycling-sector jobs | Strongly Sustainable |
| Adopting automation | $\downarrow$ waste, energy | $\uparrow$ productivity | $\downarrow$ low-skill jobs | Requires Just Transition |
| Implementing zero-liquid discharge | $\downarrow$ water pollution | $\uparrow$ capex | $\uparrow$ community goodwill | Sustainable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Resolution**: KTU examiners reward answers that **identify the conflict and propose mitigation**. Example: Automation reduces jobs *and* improves environment — the answer should propose **reskilling programs** to convert the trade-off into synergy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Framework — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[MANUFACTURING SYSTEM] --> nodeB[Environmental Pillar PLANET]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Economic Pillar PROFIT]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Social Pillar PEOPLE]
    
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Energy and Emissions]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Water and Effluents]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Materials and Waste]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Biodiversity and Land Use]
    
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Cost Efficiency]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Profit and ROI]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Green Innovation]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Long Term Viability]
    
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Worker Safety OHS]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Fair Wages and Equity]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Community Welfare]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[Consumer Protection]
    
    nodeB1 -.interlink.-> nodeC1
    nodeC3 -.interlink.-> nodeD3
    nodeD1 -.interlink.-> nodeB2
    nodeC2 -.interlink.-> nodeB4
```

## 4.2 The Three-Legged Stool — Interconnection Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeTop[SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING] 
    
    nodeTop --> legA[Leg 1 ENVIRONMENTAL]
    nodeTop --> legB[Leg 2 ECONOMIC]
    nodeTop --> legC[Leg 3 SOCIAL]
    
    legA -- remove leg A = collapse --> nodeOut1[Ecological Failure]
    legB -- remove leg B = collapse --> nodeOut2[Financial Bankruptcy]
    legC -- remove leg C = collapse --> nodeOut3[Social Backlash]
    
    legA --- legB
    legB --- legC
    legC --- legA
```

## 4.3 SDG Mapping for Sustainable Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeManuf[Manufacturing Operation]
    
    nodeManuf --> sdg7[SDG 7 Affordable Clean Energy]
    nodeManuf --> sdg8[SDG 8 Decent Work and Growth]
    nodeManuf --> sdg9[SDG 9 Industry Innovation]
    nodeManuf --> sdg12[SDG 12 Responsible Consumption]
    nodeManuf --> sdg13[SDG 13 Climate Action]
    
    sdg7 --> envDim[Environmental Dimension]
    sdg13 --> envDim
    sdg12 --> envDim
    
    sdg8 --> socDim[Social Dimension]
    
    sdg9 --> econDim[Economic Dimension]
    sdg12 --> econDim
```

## 4.4 Strong vs. Weak Sustainability — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart{Resource Decision}
    
    nodeStart -- Replace forest with farm --> nodeA[Weak Sustainability Allowed]
    nodeStart -- Preserve forest as carbon sink --> nodeB[Strong Sustainability Choice]
    
    nodeA --> nodeA1[Justified if economic gain compensates loss]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Natural capital irreplaceable]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[Required by precautionary principle]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams**: When asked to *"illustrate with a diagram"* (3 marks), always label the three pillars, list 2 sub-domains under each, and draw interlinking arrows. This complete answer typically scores **full marks**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework of sustainability. List two indicators for each of its three pillars.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**, introduced by **John Elkington (1994)**, is a sustainability accounting framework that measures an organization's performance across three dimensions: **People (Social), Planet (Environmental), and Profit (Economic)** — beyond the traditional single bottom line of financial profit.

- **Environmental (Planet)**: Carbon Footprint (kg CO₂-eq), Water Footprint (L/unit)
- **Economic (Profit)**: Return on Sustainability Investment (ROSI in %), Life-Cycle Cost (₹)
- **Social (People)**: Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR), Employee Satisfaction Index

A sustainable organization balances all three simultaneously. *(Award 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for indicators.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Distinguish between Strong Sustainability and Weak Sustainability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | Weak Sustainability | Strong Sustainability |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Capital view | Natural & man-made capital are substitutes | Natural capital cannot be substituted |
| Resource use | Compensable (deforestation OK if money reinvested) | Critical natural capital must be preserved |
| Origin | Neoclassical economics | Ecological economics / UN SDG approach |
| Example | Mining a forest for timber | Preserving a forest as carbon sink |

Modern frameworks (UN SDGs, planetary boundaries) lean toward **Strong Sustainability**. *(Award 1.5 marks each column + 0.5 marks for example/line of conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A [14 Marks]** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three dimensions of sustainability** (Environmental, Economic, Social) with at least four indicators each. Discuss how these dimensions are **interconnected** in a manufacturing context. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing unit reports the following KPI values for the year 2024:

| Dimension | Raw Value | Best in Industry | Worst in Industry | Weight |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| CO₂ emissions (kg/unit, lower better) | 5.0 | 2.0 | 10.0 | 0.4 |
| Profit per unit (₹, higher better) | 150 | 250 | 50 | 0.35 |
| LTIFR (per M-hr, lower better) | 3.0 | 1.0 | 6.0 | 0.25 |

**Compute the composite Sustainability Index (SI)** and interpret the result. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

The three dimensions are the pillars of sustainable manufacturing:

**(i) Environmental Dimension** — Concerned with ecological integrity.
- Indicators: (1) Carbon Footprint, (2) Water Footprint, (3) Energy Intensity (SEC), (4) Waste Recycling Rate.

**(ii) Economic Dimension** — Concerned with long-term financial viability.
- Indicators: (1) Life-Cycle Cost (LCC), (2) Return on Sustainability Investment (ROSI), (3) Green Revenue Share, (4) Payback Period of green capex.

**(iii) Social Dimension** — Concerned with human well-being.
- Indicators: (1) Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR), (2) Employee Satisfaction Index, (3) Community Investment Ratio, (4) Training Hours per Employee.

**Interconnections (2 of 4 expected for full marks)**:
- Cleaner production → lower energy bill → economic savings (Env ↔ Econ)
- Safer workplaces → higher productivity → higher profit (Soc ↔ Econ)
- Use of recycled material → less extraction → less community displacement (Env ↔ Soc)
- Local green jobs → social equity → brand value → economic resilience (Soc ↔ Econ ↔ Env)

*(Marking split: 1 mark each for dimension definition, 1 mark total for 4 indicators, 2 marks for interconnection examples.)*

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Environmental normalization** (lower better):
$$
E_n = \dfrac{10.0 - 5.0}{10.0 - 2.0} = \dfrac{5.0}{8.0} = 0.625
$$

**Step 2 — Economic normalization** (higher better):
$$
P_n = \dfrac{150 - 50}{250 - 50} = \dfrac{100}{200} = 0.500
$$

**Step 3 — Social normalization** (lower better):
$$
S_n = \dfrac{6.0 - 3.0}{6.0 - 1.0} = \dfrac{3.0}{5.0} = 0.600
$$

**Step 4 — Composite SI**:
$$
\text{SI} = (0.4 \times 0.625) + (0.35 \times 0.500) + (0.25 \times 0.600)
$$
$$
\text{SI} = 0.250 + 0.175 + 0.150 = 0.575
$$

**Interpretation**: $\text{SI} = 0.575$ lies in the **Acceptable / Improving** band (0.5–0.8). Economic performance is the weakest dimension and warrants immediate attention.

*(Marking split: 1.5 marks per normalization + 1.5 marks for SI calculation + 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

### **QUESTION B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Define **sustainability** as per the **Brundtland Commission Report (1987)**. Critically discuss how the **environmental, economic, and social dimensions** of sustainability apply to a **manufacturing enterprise**, citing at least two real-world examples for each dimension. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A factory invests ₹10 lakh in a solar PV system that reduces annual energy bills by ₹2.8 lakh. The project life is 8 years and the discount rate is 10%. Using the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** approach, calculate the **annualized cost** and determine if the project is **financially viable**. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition** (2 marks): Per Brundtland, sustainability is *"development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."* Applied to manufacturing, it means producing goods through processes that are **ecologically sound, economically viable, and socially responsible** across the entire product life cycle.

**Environmental Examples**:
- **Tesla Gigafactory**: Solar-powered EV manufacturing, zero liquid discharge.
- **Toyota Production System**: Reduction of VOC emissions in paint shops.

**Economic Examples**:
- **Patagonia's Worn Wear program**: Resale of used garments creates new revenue streams.
- **Interface (carpet company)**: Mission Zero program saved $400M+ through waste reduction.

**Social Examples**:
- **Toyota's OHS systems**: Industry-leading LTIFR through kaizen-based safety.
- **Unilever Sustainable Living Plan**: Improved livelihoods of 5.5M smallholder farmers.

**Critical Discussion** (1 mark): The three dimensions are **non-trivially linked** — improvement in one often requires investment in another. A balanced approach using the **TBL framework** ensures long-term resilience.

*(Marking: 2 for definition + 4 for examples with reasoning + 1 for critical discussion.)*

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the Capital Recovery Factor**:
$$
\text{CRF} = \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^8}{(1.10)^8 - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $(1.10)^8$**:
$$
(1.10)^8 = 2.1436
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**:
$$
\text{CRF} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.1436}{2.1436 - 1} = \dfrac{0.21436}{1.1436} = 0.1874
$$

**Step 4 — Annualized Cost**:
$$
C = I \times \text{CRF} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1874 = \text{₹ } 1{,}87{,}400 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision**:
$$
\text{Net Annual Benefit} = A - C = 2{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}87{,}400 = \text{₹ } 92{,}600
$$

Since the Net Annual Benefit is **positive (₹92,600 > 0)**, the solar PV project is **financially viable**.

**Payback Period**:
$$
\text{PP} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{2{,}80{,}000} = 3.57 \text{ years}
$$
Well below the typical 5-year benchmark.

*(Marking: 2 marks CRF formula, 2 marks substitution, 1 mark annualized cost, 1 mark decision, 1 mark payback.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** ⚠️
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Triple Bottom Line" with "Sustainable Development"** — TBL is a *measurement framework*; SD is the *philosophy*.
> 2. **Weightages in SI must sum to 1** — students often forget this constraint and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **For "lower is better" indicators** (emissions, LTIFR), the normalization formula is $X_n = (X_{\max} - X_r)/(X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ — using the wrong direction is a common error.
> 4. **Don't write one-line answers for 7-mark sub-parts** — examiners expect at least **3–4 logical steps** for full credit.
> 5. **Always cite a definition with its source** (Brundtland 1987, Elkington 1994) — this earns the *keyword bonus* in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **Brundtland (1987)** gave the foundational definition of sustainability.
- **John Elkington (1994)** proposed the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — People, Planet, Profit.
- The **three dimensions** are: **Environmental, Economic, Social** — all are equally important.
- **Strong Sustainability** = natural capital is irreplaceable; **Weak Sustainability** = natural capital is substitutable.
- Key Environmental metrics: **Carbon Footprint, Water Footprint, SEC, Recycling Rate**.
- Key Economic metrics: **ROSI, LCC, Payback Period, Green Revenue Share**.
- Key Social metrics: **LTIFR, Employee Satisfaction Index, Community Investment Ratio**.
- Sustainability Index formula: **$\text{SI} = w_E E_n + w_P P_n + w_S S_n$**, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- Normalization: use $X_n = (X_r - X_{\min})/(X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ for "higher is better"; use the inverse for "lower is better".
- **Capital Recovery Factor**: $\text{CRF} = \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ — used to annualize investment cost.
- A project is sustainable when it is **ecologically sound, economically viable, AND socially responsible** simultaneously.
- **UN SDGs 7, 8, 9, 12, 13** are the most relevant to manufacturing sustainability.
- **Common KTU answer structure**: *Definition → Diagram → Indicators → Interconnections → Example → Conclusion*.
- **Strong vs Weak** is a favourite 3-mark question; prepare a comparison table.
- **Numerical SI problems are guaranteed** in Part B — practice at least 3–4 such problems.
- **Trade-offs exist** (e.g., automation reduces jobs but improves environment); the answer should propose **mitigation strategies** like reskilling.
- Always end 14-mark answers with a **concluding line** emphasizing the holistic balance of TBL.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation between Green, Lean and Sustainable Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Relation between Green, Lean and Sustainable Manufacturing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sustainable Manufacturing (SM)** is the formal KTU 2024 Scheme definition adopted in Module 1: *"The creation of manufactured products through economically-sound processes that minimize negative environmental impacts while conserving energy and natural resources, thereby enhancing employee, community, and product safety."* ### The Three Pillars — Exact Definitions **1. Green Manufacturing (GM)** A subset of manufacturing philosophy that focuses exclusively on the **environmental dimension** of production. It emphasizes: - Minimization of harmful emissions, effluents, and solid waste - Reduction of energy and natural-resource consumption - Use of eco-friendly raw materials and end-of-life recyclability - Compliance with environmental regulations (e.g., ISO 14001) **2. Lean Manufacturing (LM)** A philosophy rooted in the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** that maximizes customer value while minimizing waste (*muda*). Its sole original focus is on **economic efficiency and operational excellence**. **3. Sustainable Manufacturing (SM)** The **holistic umbrella philosophy** that integrates: - **Economic viability** (inherited from Lean) - **Environmental stewardship** (inherited from Green) - **Social responsibility** (People — community, worker safety, ethics) This three-dimensional balance is formalized as the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — *People, Planet, Profit*. > [!NOTE] > **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** is the foundational sustainability metric: A sustainable process must score positively on **People (Social Equity)**, **Planet (Environmental Quality)**, and **Profit (Economic Vitality)** simultaneously. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Healthy Human Body" Model Imagine the human body as a factory: | Manufacturing Paradigm | Human Body Analogy | What It Optimizes | |---|---|---| | **Lean Manufacturing** | A *physically fit athlete* who runs efficiently with minimum wasted energy | Speed, efficiency, cost | | **Green Manufacturing** | A person on a *strict organic diet* who recycles and consumes responsibly | Environmental health | | **Sustainable Manufacturing** | A *holistically healthy individual* who is fit, eats clean, sleeps well, and has strong relationships | Complete well-being across all life dimensions | Just as you cannot call an athlete "perfectly healthy" if they eat junk food (Lean without Green), or call an eco-warrior "successful" if they cannot afford rent (Green without Lean), **a truly sustainable manufacturer must excel in ALL three dimensions**. --- ## 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in KTU Examinations > [!IMPORTANT] > Examiners in OESFT612 frequently test the **overlap versus distinction** between these three terms. A common pitfall is treating Green and Sustainable as synonyms — they are NOT. Green is a *subset*; Sustainable is the *superset*. - **Lean ∩ Green** = *Lean-Green Manufacturing* (efficiency + environment) - **Lean ∩ Green ∩ Social** = **Sustainable Manufacturing** - Green ⊂ Sustainable ⊂ Triple Bottom Line Framework # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Lean Philosophy — The Seven Wastes (*Muda*) Lean manufacturing, developed within the **Toyota Production System**, identifies **7 (originally 7, expanded to 8) wastes** that must be eliminated. The Japanese word *muda* literally means *uselessness* or *wastefulness*. | # | Waste (Muda) | Japanese Term | Description | Real-World Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Defects** | *Defects* | Products that fail quality inspection | Scrapped parts, rework | | 2 | **Overproduction** | *Seisan* | Producing more than demanded | Excess inventory buildup | | 3 | **Waiting** | *Wait* | Idle time of workers/machines | Idle machines between cycles | | 4 | **Non-utilized Talent** | *Talent* | Underused human skills | Skilled engineers doing repetitive tasks | | 5 | **Transportation** | *Transport* | Unnecessary movement of materials | Long forklift routes | | 6 | **Inventory** | *Stock* | Excess raw material / WIP / FG | Stagnant stock in warehouses | | 7 | **Motion** | *Motion* | Unnecessary human movement | Workers walking long distances | | 8 | **Extra Processing** | *Processing* | Doing more work than required | Over-engineering / over-machining | > [!NOTE] > **Environmental Insight:** Each of these 7 wastes has a *direct* environmental cost. Defects mean scrapped material (waste); overproduction means excess energy consumption; transportation means fuel burning. **This is why Lean and Green naturally overlap.** --- ## 2.2 The Green Manufacturing Framework Green Manufacturing operates on a **3R → 6R → 9R** hierarchy (as adopted in KTU Module 1). The most commonly tested framework is the **6R Hierarchy**: $$\text{Refuse} \rightarrow \text{Rethink} \rightarrow \text{Reduce} \rightarrow \text{Reuse} \rightarrow \text{Repair} \rightarrow \text{Recycle}$$ | R Level | Action | Environmental Impact Reduction | |---|---|---| | **Refuse** | Do not use harmful material | Highest | | **Rethink** | Redesign product/process | Very High | | **Reduce** | Minimize resource use | High | | **Reuse** | Use again for same purpose | Moderate | | **Repair** | Restore functionality | Moderate | | **Recycle** | Reprocess into new material | Lowest | --- ## 2.3 The Sustainable Manufacturing Framework (Triple Bottom Line) Sustainable Manufacturing integrates all three pillars through the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**: $$\text{Sustainability} = f(\text{People, Planet, Profit})$$ Where the three dimensions must be **simultaneously optimized**. The mathematical form is sometimes expressed as: $$S = \alpha E + \beta V + \gamma R$$ Where: - $E$ = Environmental performance index (Green score) - $V$ = Economic/Value efficiency index (Lean score) - $R$ = Social responsibility index - $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = Weighting coefficients (typically $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$) --- ## 2.4 The Relationship Matrix — Lean vs Green vs Sustainable > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Table — High-Yield for KTU Examinations** | Dimension | Lean Manufacturing | Green Manufacturing | Sustainable Manufacturing | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Goal** | Maximize value, eliminate waste (muda) | Minimize environmental impact | Balance TBL (People, Planet, Profit) | | **Origin** | Toyota Production System (TPS), 1950s | Environmental movement, 1980s | Brundtland Report, 1987 | | **Focus** | Economic efficiency | Environmental stewardship | Holistic integration | | **Key Metrics** | Cycle time, OEE, TAKT time, lead time | CO₂ footprint, emissions, water use | TBL score, life-cycle assessment | | **Key Tools** | 5S, Kaizen, Kanban, Value Stream Mapping | LCA, Design for Environment (DfE), ISO 14001 | Integrated frameworks, Cradle-to-Cradle | | **Stakeholder Focus** | Customer + Shareholder | Environment + Regulators | All stakeholders | | **Scope** | Internal operations | Product + Process | Entire value chain + society | | **Time Horizon** | Short-to-medium term | Medium-to-long term | Inter-generational | --- ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Automotive Industry:** Toyota's Lean system is now merged with Green initiatives to build hybrid vehicles (Prius). - **Electronics:** Apple's Supplier Clean Energy Program combines Lean supply chain efficiency with Green energy sourcing. - **Textiles:** Patagonia uses Sustainable Manufacturing to balance profit, environment, and worker welfare in its global supply chain. In **Industry 4.0** and **Industry 5.0** contexts, all three paradigms converge through **digital twins, IoT sensors, and AI-driven optimization**, making their integrated understanding essential for KTU graduates. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Framework — The Venn Set-Theoretic Relationship We can formally model the three paradigms as sets. Let: - $L$ = Set of Lean Manufacturing principles - $G$ = Set of Green Manufacturing principles - $S$ = Set of Sustainable Manufacturing principles ### Mathematical Representation: $$\text{Lean} \subset (\text{Sustainable})$$ $$\text{Green} \subset (\text{Sustainable})$$ $$S = L \cup G \cup \{\text{Social Responsibility Dimension}\}$$ $$\text{Lean-Green} = L \cap G$$ $$\text{Fully Sustainable} = L \cap G \cap \{\text{Social}\}$$ ### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: **Step 1:** Establish the foundational domain. $$\text{Manufacturing Domain (MD)} = \{ \text{All industrial production processes} \}$$ **Step 2:** Define Lean within the domain. $$L = \{ x \in \text{MD} \mid x \text{ maximizes value and minimizes } muda \}$$ This gives the **Lean Set** focused purely on **economic efficiency**. **Step 3:** Define Green within the domain. $$G = \{ x \in \text{MD} \mid x \text{ minimizes environmental harm} \}$$ This gives the **Green Set** focused purely on **environmental protection**. **Step 4:** Define Social dimension as a new set. $$P = \{ x \in \text{MD} \mid x \text{ maximizes social welfare} \}$$ Where $P$ represents the **People/Social pillar**. **Step 5:** Construct Sustainable Manufacturing as the **union of three sets**: $$S = L \cup G \cup P$$ **Step 6:** Identify the *Lean-Green Intersection* — practices that are BOTH efficient AND environmentally friendly: $$\text{Lean-Green} = L \cap G$$ For example: Reducing material waste (Lean) simultaneously reduces raw material extraction (Green). **Step 7:** Identify *Fully Sustainable Manufacturing* — practices satisfying ALL THREE dimensions: $$\text{Sustainable} = L \cap G \cap P$$ For example: A solar-powered, automated, fair-wage factory that uses no child labor. --- ## 3.2 The 3×3 Sustainability Diagnostic Matrix This is a KTU-favorite analytical tool. Every manufacturing practice can be classified using this matrix: | Practice Category | Lean (Efficiency) | Green (Environment) | Social (People) | Verdict | |---|---|---|---|---| | Just-In-Time (JIT) inventory | ✅ Yes | ✅ Reduces storage energy | Neutral | **Lean-Green** | | Renewable energy sourcing | Neutral | ✅ Yes | Neutral | **Green only** | | Worker safety program | Neutral | Neutral | ✅ Yes | **Social only** | | Solar-powered JIT factory | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | **Sustainable** ✅ | | Single-criterion cost cutting | ✅ Yes | ❌ May harm environment | ❌ May exploit workers | **Anti-Sustainable** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for Examiners:** A practice is only truly *Sustainable* if it scores positively on **ALL THREE axes**. Single-axis optimization is incomplete and may even be *unsustainable* in disguise. --- ## 3.3 Waste Elimination Efficiency — Comparative Analysis Let us model a manufacturing process producing **N units/month** with waste quantity $W$: **Lean-only approach:** $$\text{Waste Reduction}_{\text{Lean}} = W \times (1 - e^{-k_L \cdot t})$$ Where: - $k_L$ = Lean improvement constant (process optimization) - $t$ = time **Green-only approach:** $$\text{Environmental Burden Reduction}_{\text{Green}} = B \times (1 - e^{-k_G \cdot t})$$ Where $B$ = baseline environmental burden (CO₂, effluents). **Integrated Lean-Green-Sustainable approach:** $$\text{Total Benefit}_{\text{Sustainable}} = \alpha W \cdot \Delta_L + \beta B \cdot \Delta_G + \gamma S \cdot \Delta_P$$ Where: - $\Delta_L, \Delta_G, \Delta_P$ = percentage reductions in Lean, Green, Social dimensions - $S$ = social impact baseline - $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weighting factors summing to 1 This demonstrates why an integrated approach yields **greater overall benefit** than isolated paradigms. --- ## 3.4 Quantification — The Sustainable Manufacturing Index (SMI) A simplified composite index used in academic literature: $$SMI = \frac{1}{3} \left( \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{lean}}} + \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{green}}} + \frac{R_{\text{actual}}}{R_{\text{social}}} \right)$$ Where each ratio lies in $[0,1]$. A perfect sustainable factory scores **SMI = 1.0**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Venn Diagram — Set-Theoretic Relationship ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PILLAR1["LEAN MANUFACTURING (LM)"] LM1["Maximize Value"] LM2["Eliminate 7 Wastes"] LM3["Economic Efficiency"] end subgraph PILLAR2["GREEN MANUFACTURING (GM)"] GM1["Minimize Emissions"] GM2["6R Hierarchy"] GM3["Resource Conservation"] end subgraph PILLAR3["SOCIAL (PEOPLE)"] SOC1["Worker Safety"] SOC2["Fair Wages"] SOC3["Community Welfare"] end LM1 --> LG["LEAN-GREEN ZONE"] LM2 --> LG GM1 --> LG GM2 --> LG LM1 --> SUST["SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING (SM)"] LM2 --> SUST LM3 --> SUST GM1 --> SUST GM2 --> SUST GM3 --> SUST SOC1 --> SUST SOC2 --> SUST SOC3 --> SUST style LG fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style SUST fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 style PILLAR1 fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style PILLAR2 fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style PILLAR3 fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Integration Architecture of the Three Paradigms ```mermaid flowchart LR A[RAW MATERIALS] --> B{MANUFACTURING SYSTEM} B --> C[LEAN LAYER] B --> D[GREEN LAYER] B --> E[SOCIAL LAYER] C --> C1[Just-In-Time] C --> C2[5S Kaizen] C --> C3[Value Stream Map] D --> D1[6R Hierarchy] D --> D2[ISO 14001] D --> D3[Design for Environment] E --> E1[Worker Welfare] E --> E2[Community Impact] E --> E3[Ethics & Safety] C1 --> F[INTEGRATED SUSTAINABLE OUTPUT] C2 --> F C3 --> F D1 --> F D2 --> F D3 --> F E1 --> F E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G[FINISHED PRODUCT] F --> H[MINIMIZED WASTE] F --> I[REDUCED EMISSIONS] F --> J[POSITIVE SOCIAL IMPACT] style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 style C fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Conceptual Evolution Flowchart ```mermaid graph LR T1[1950: Toyota Production System] --> T2[1980s: Lean Manufacturing Formalized] T2 --> T3[1987: Brundtland Report defines Sustainability] T3 --> T4[1990s: Green Manufacturing Emerges] T4 --> T5[2000s: Lean-Green Integration Studies] T5 --> T6[2010s: Industry 4.0 Smart Manufacturing] T6 --> T7[2020s: Industry 5.0 - Sustainable Human-Centric Manufacturing] T7 --> T8[SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING HOLISTIC FRAMEWORK] style T8 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 style T1 fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style T2 fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style T3 fill:#ADD8E6,color:#000 style T4 fill:#90EE90,color:#000 style T5 fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000 style T6 fill:#FFA500,color:#000 style T7 fill:#FFD700,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the historical evolution shows that *Sustainable Manufacturing is NOT a replacement* for Lean and Green — it is their **synthesis and extension** with the social dimension added. --- ## 4.4 The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Pillar Diagram ```mermaid graph TB CENTER[SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING] P1[PEOPLE
Social Equity
Worker Safety
Fair Wages
Community Welfare] P2[PLANET
Environmental Quality
Low Emissions
Resource Efficiency
Biodiversity Protection] P3[PROFIT
Economic Vitality
Cost Efficiency
Market Competitiveness
Long-term Viability] CENTER --- P1 CENTER --- P2 CENTER --- P3 style CENTER fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:5px,color:#000 style P1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style P2 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style P3 fill:#4169E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *"Differentiate between Green Manufacturing and Sustainable Manufacturing with suitable examples."* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Green Manufacturing | Sustainable Manufacturing | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Narrow — only environmental dimension | Broad — environmental + economic + social | | **Goal** | Reduce ecological footprint | Balance Triple Bottom Line (TBL) | | **Example** | A factory using solar power | A factory using solar power, paying fair wages, AND operating cost-efficiently (Lean) | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Green Manufacturing is a *subset* of Sustainable Manufacturing. A process can be Green but NOT Sustainable (e.g., a wind-powered factory that exploits labor). However, any Sustainable process must inherently be Green. *(Total: 3 marks — Tabular differentiation = 2 marks, conceptual conclusion = 1 mark)* --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *"List the seven wastes (muda) of Lean Manufacturing and explain any two in detail."* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The seven wastes identified in the Toyota Production System are: 1. Defects 2. Overproduction 3. Waiting 4. Non-utilized talent 5. Transportation 6. Inventory 7. Motion *(Plus the 8th added waste: Extra-processing)* **Explanation of two wastes (1.5 marks each):** - **Overproduction:** Producing more than the customer demands creates excess inventory, tying up capital and storage space. It also wastes raw materials and energy. *Example:* A factory producing 10,000 units when demand is only 7,000. - **Waiting:** Idle time when workers or machines are not actively processing due to bottlenecks, breakdowns, or poor scheduling. *Example:* A CNC machine idle for 30 minutes waiting for the operator to load the next job. *(Total: 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing "Lean = Green = Sustainable" — they are NOT identical. Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for this conceptual confusion. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Ignoring the **Social/People** pillar in Sustainable Manufacturing — losing **2 marks** in TBL-based questions. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing definitions without examples — examiners expect **at least one industrial example** for full marks. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing the 6R hierarchy order — memorize in correct sequence: *Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Recycle*. --- ### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Part (a):** *"Explain the relationship between Lean, Green, and Sustainable Manufacturing with the help of a neat Venn diagram. Discuss how Lean Manufacturing principles contribute to environmental sustainability."* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The three paradigms represent progressively integrated approaches to manufacturing excellence. While Lean focuses on economic efficiency, Green focuses on environmental protection, and Sustainable integrates both with social responsibility. **Venn Diagram (2 marks):** The diagram should show three overlapping circles: - **Circle L** (Lean) — Economic efficiency, waste elimination - **Circle G** (Green) — Environmental protection, 6R hierarchy - **Circle S** (Social/People) — Worker welfare, community impact - **Central overlap (L ∩ G ∩ S)** = Sustainable Manufacturing **How Lean Principles Contribute to Environmental Sustainability (4 marks):** 1. **Elimination of Overproduction** reduces unnecessary raw material consumption, lowering mining/extraction impact. 2. **Reduction of Defects** means fewer scrapped parts, reducing landfill waste and energy used in manufacturing defective units. 3. **Just-In-Time (JIT) inventory** minimizes warehouse storage, reducing energy used for climate-controlled storage. 4. **Optimized Transportation routes** reduce fuel consumption and CO₂ emissions. 5. **Reduced Motion and Waiting** lower energy consumed per productive hour, improving energy efficiency per unit produced. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Lean Manufacturing and Green Manufacturing share the **fundamental goal of waste reduction** — Lean focuses on operational waste (muda) while Green focuses on environmental waste. This natural synergy forms the foundation of the broader Sustainable Manufacturing paradigm. *(Total: 7 marks)* --- **Part (b):** *"Describe the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework with reference to Sustainable Manufacturing. How does it differ from traditional profit-only metrics?"* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition of TBL (2 marks):** The Triple Bottom Line, introduced by John Elkington in 1994, is a sustainability framework that measures organizational performance across three dimensions: - **People (Social Equity)** - **Planet (Environmental Quality)** - **Profit (Economic Vitality)** $$S = f(P_{\text{eople}}, P_{\text{lanet}}, P_{\text{rofit}})$$ **Detailed Explanation of Three Pillars (3 marks):** 1. **People:** Focuses on labor practices, human rights, employee health and safety, community engagement, and product responsibility. 2. **Planet:** Encompasses environmental impacts — emissions, waste generation, resource depletion, biodiversity effects, and energy consumption. 3. **Profit:** Traditional economic dimension — revenue, cost efficiency, return on investment, and long-term financial viability. **Difference from Traditional Profit-Only Metrics (2 marks):** | Aspect | Traditional Profit-Only | TBL Framework | |---|---|---| | **Metrics** | Single dimension (financial ROI) | Three integrated dimensions | | **Time Horizon** | Short-term quarterly results | Long-term intergenerational | | **Stakeholders** | Shareholders only | All stakeholders (workers, community, environment) | | **Reporting** | Financial statements only | Sustainability + integrated reports | | **Risk View** | Ignores environmental/social risk | Includes ESG risk exposure | **Conclusion (0.5 marks, rounding to 7):** TBL recognizes that a manufacturing enterprise operates within a larger socio-ecological system, and long-term profit is impossible without environmental and social health. *(Total: 7 marks)* --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Part (a):** *"With suitable examples, discuss the 6R Hierarchy of Green Manufacturing. How does each 'R' progressively reduce environmental impact?"* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The 6R Hierarchy is a decision-making framework for Green Manufacturing that ranks waste-management strategies from most to least environmentally beneficial. The hierarchy follows the principle that **prevention is better than cure**. **The 6R Hierarchy in Order of Environmental Preference (4 marks):** 1. **Refuse (Highest priority):** Reject the use of harmful materials entirely. *Example:* Refusing to use lead-based solder in electronics manufacturing. 2. **Rethink:** Redesign products/processes to be inherently eco-friendly. *Example:* Redesigning packaging to use mushroom-based biodegradable material instead of plastic. 3. **Reduce:** Minimize the quantity of resources used. *Example:* Reducing water consumption in textile dyeing by 40% using closed-loop systems. 4. **Reuse:** Use the same item again for the same or different purpose. *Example:* Reusing glass bottles in the beverage industry. 5. **Repair:** Restore broken items to working condition rather than discarding. *Example:* Apple's iPhone repair program extending product life. 6. **Recycle (Lowest priority):** Reprocess waste into new raw materials. *Example:* Recycling aluminum cans to save 95% of the energy needed for virgin aluminum production. **Progressive Impact Analysis (2 marks):** The hierarchy forms a **descending pyramid of environmental desirability**. Refusing is best because it prevents impact at the source; recycling is least preferred because it still consumes energy and produces secondary waste. Sustainable manufacturers attempt to "climb up" the hierarchy from Recycle toward Refuse. **Conclusion (0.5 marks):** The 6R framework guides manufacturers toward **circular economy principles** rather than the linear "take-make-dispose" model. *(Total: 7 marks)* --- **Part (b):** *"Explain the concept of Lean-Green Manufacturing. Discuss any four tools/techniques that integrate Lean and Green objectives in a manufacturing setup."* **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept of Lean-Green Manufacturing (2 marks):** Lean-Green Manufacturing is the **strategic integration** of Lean Manufacturing principles (waste elimination, efficiency) with Green Manufacturing goals (environmental protection). It is the **natural intersection** of the two paradigms and serves as the practical foundation for Sustainable Manufacturing. $$\text{Lean-Green} = L \cap G$$ The core insight: **Operational waste IS environmental waste.** Every unit of material scrapped, every extra mile of transport, every kWh of excess energy is BOTH an economic loss AND an environmental burden. **Four Integration Tools/Techniques (4 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Value Stream Mapping (VSM) for Environment:** Traditional VSM maps material and information flow. The "Green-VSM" extension also maps energy, emissions, and waste streams at each step, identifying dual optimization opportunities. 2. **5S with Environmental Audits:** The standard 5S (Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) is extended with a "Shine for environment" component — detecting spills, leaks, and wasteful practices during workplace organization. 3. **Kaizen Events for Sustainability:** Continuous improvement (Kaizen) events are organized specifically to reduce both production waste (Lean) and environmental waste (Green), often called "Eco-Kaizen." 4. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM):** Well-maintained machines run more efficiently, consume less energy, produce fewer defects, and last longer — addressing BOTH Lean (no downtime) and Green (less resource consumption) objectives. **Real-World Example (1 mark):** Toyota's manufacturing plants in Japan have reduced energy consumption per vehicle by **30%** through Lean-Green integration, while simultaneously reducing production costs. *(Total: 7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:** - ✅ **Sustainable Manufacturing = Lean + Green + Social (People)** — never forget the People pillar. - ✅ **Triple Bottom Line (TBL) = People + Planet + Profit** — coined by John Elkington (1994). - ✅ **Sustainable Manufacturing** was formally defined in the **Brundtland Report (1987)**. - ✅ **Lean Manufacturing** originated from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** in the 1950s. - ✅ **The 7 Wastes (Muda):** Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized Talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing. - ✅ **The 6R Hierarchy (in order):** Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Recycle. - ✅ **Green ⊂ Sustainable** — Green is a SUBSET, not a synonym. - ✅ **Lean ∩ Green = Lean-Green** — the natural intersection of efficiency and environment. - ✅ **Sustainable = Lean ∩ Green ∩ Social** — requires ALL THREE dimensions. - ✅ Common tools: **5S, Kaizen, Kanban, VSM** (Lean) and **LCA, DfE, ISO 14001** (Green). - ✅ Key metrics: **OEE, TAKT time, Cycle time** (Lean); **CO₂ footprint, Energy per unit** (Green); **TBL Score, SMI** (Sustainable). - ✅ **Industry 5.0** = Industry 4.0 (smart) + Human-centric + Sustainable Manufacturing. - ✅ Always include **at least one industrial example** in long-answer questions for full marks. - ✅ Draw a **neat labeled Venn diagram** when asked about relationships — earns easy 2–3 marks. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Weightage Tip:** Module 1 typically contributes **15–20%** of the End Semester Examination (ESE) marks, with at least one full 14-mark question on this topic. Master the Venn-diagram representation and the 6R hierarchy — they appear in almost every exam cycle.

topics.title: Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment - R3 and R6 Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment - R3 and R6 Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment (ECQFD)

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment (ECQFD)** is an extended framework of the traditional Quality Function Deployment (QFD) methodology that systematically integrates **environmental requirements**, **regulatory constraints**, and **sustainability metrics** alongside conventional customer requirements (CRs) into each phase of product and process development. It employs a series of interconnected matrices—known as the **Houses of Quality (HoQ)**—to translate the "voice of the customer" and the "voice of the environment" into engineering characteristics, process plans, and end-of-life strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** ECQFD is NOT a replacement for QFD; it is a **green-augmented superset** that incorporates the **triple bottom line** (People, Planet, Profit) into the original QFD matrix structure.

---

### 1.2 The Multi-Phase QFD Architecture

The classical QFD model (Akao, 1972) consists of **four deployment phases**. However, for sustainable manufacturing, the framework is extended to **six phases** (R1 to R6):

| Cycle | Phase Name | Primary Function |
|:-----:|:-----------|:-----------------|
| **R1** | Product Planning | Voice of Customer → Engineering Characteristics |
| **R2** | Parts Deployment | Engineering Characteristics → Subsystem/Part Specs |
| **R3** | **Process Planning** | Part Specs → Manufacturing Process Plans |
| **R4** | Production Planning | Process Plans → Production/Quality Controls |
| **R5** | Service Planning | Production → Field Service Requirements |
| **R6** | **Recycling/End-of-Life Planning** | Disposal → Recovery & Circular Economy Strategy |

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Eco-Architect's Blueprint"**
>
> Imagine you are an **architect designing a green skyscraper**:
>
> - **Traditional QFD** is like asking tenants (customers) what they want—location, view, price—and translating it into concrete specs like window thickness, floor height, and elevator speed.
> - **ECQFD** goes further. Before laying a single brick, you also consult the **environmentalist, the city waste auditor, and the future demolition crew**. You ask: *"How will this building breathe (emissions)? How will rainwater flow off it (waste)? And when it's 80 years old and being torn down, which materials can be plucked out and reused?"*
> - The **R3 Cycle** is the **construction foreman's stage**—deciding whether to weld, bolt, or pour, and selecting cranes vs. robots based on eco-impact.
> - The **R6 Cycle** is the **demolition planner's stage**—deciding in advance which beams are steel (recyclable), which are concrete (downcyclable), and how to safely strip hazardous insulation.

In short: **R3 = "How do I build it green?"** and **R6 = "How do I unbuild it green?"**

---

### 1.4 The R3 Cycle — Process Planning Phase (Definition)

The **R3 Cycle (Process Planning House of Quality)** is the third matrix in the QFD cascade. It takes the **part characteristics** developed in R2 (such as dimensional tolerances, surface finish, material grade) and translates them into **manufacturing process plans**. In the ECQFD context, R3 additionally integrates **environmental process indicators** such as energy consumption per part, VOC emissions, coolant toxicity, and scrap generation rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition (KTU Expected):** The R3 cycle output is a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** annotated with both quality Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) parameters and Critical-to-Environment (CTE) parameters.

---

### 1.5 The R6 Cycle — Recycling / End-of-Life Planning Phase (Definition)

The **R6 Cycle (Recycling / End-of-Life House of Quality)** is the sixth and final matrix, introduced specifically for sustainable product development. It systematically maps the **end-of-life (EoL) requirements** of the product—disassembly ease, material separability, recyclability index, hazardous content, and recovery cost—against the **recycling process characteristics**. The output is a strategic plan for **Design for Disassembly (DfD)**, **Design for Recycling (DfR)**, and **Design for Remanufacturing (DfReman)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Sustainability Metric in R6:** The **Recyclability Ratio (RR)**, defined as:
>
> $$\text{Recyclability Ratio (RR)} = \frac{\text{Mass of Recoverable Materials}}{\text{Total Product Mass}} \times 100\%$$
>
> Industry benchmark: **RR ≥ 75%** is considered "highly recyclable" per **ISO 14062**.

---

### 1.6 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for KTU 2024

| KTU CO ID | Course Outcome Description | This Topic's Contribution |
|:---------:|:---------------------------|:--------------------------|
| **CO2** | Analyse sustainable manufacturing process planning methods | Directly maps to R3 Cycle |
| **CO3** | Evaluate end-of-life strategies and circular economy models | Directly maps to R6 Cycle |
| **CO4** | Apply eco-design tools to real industrial case studies | Foundational for both cycles |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 R3 Cycle — Theoretical Decomposition

The R3 matrix is constructed through a **four-stage analytical pipeline**:

**Stage 1 — Input Ingestion from R2**
The right-side outputs of the R2 matrix (part characteristics with target values and importance weights) are imported as the "WHATs" (rows) of the R3 House of Quality.

**Stage 2 — Process Identification (The "HOWs")**
The columns of the R3 matrix are populated with candidate **manufacturing processes**:
- Casting, forging, machining, sheet-metal forming, injection molding, additive manufacturing, joining (welding, brazing, adhesive bonding), surface treatment, heat treatment, and assembly.

**Stage 3 — Relationship Matrix Construction (Roof + Body)**
- **Body of the matrix (Y-shaped, ∿):** Strength of correlation between each part characteristic and each process option. Typical scale: **9 (strong), 3 (moderate), 1 (weak), 0 (none)**.
- **Roof of the matrix (◿):** Synergy/conflict between process options themselves (e.g., machining ↔ grinding = **synergy**; machining ↔ casting = **neutral**).

**Stage 4 — Environmental Augmentation (ECQFD-Specific)**
Three additional rows are appended at the bottom of the R3 matrix:
- **Energy Intensity** (MJ/kg of part)
- **Process Waste Index (PWI)** (% material wasted)
- **Hazardous Substance Score (HSS)** (1–5 Likert scale, lower is better)

---

### 2.2 R6 Cycle — Theoretical Decomposition

The R6 matrix is constructed through a **five-stage reverse-logistics pipeline**:

**Stage 1 — EoL Scenario Definition**
The product's life-cycle is partitioned into three terminal scenarios:
- **Scenario A:** Reuse / Refurbish / Resell
- **Scenario B:** Remanufacture / Recondition
- **Scenario C:** Recycle / Downcycle / Dispose

**Stage 2 — Disassembly Strategy Mapping**
For each EoL scenario, a **disassembly sequence diagram (DSD)** is generated using:
- **Disassembly Depth (DD):** Number of subassemblies separated.
- **Disassembly Time (DT):** Required labor-minutes.
- **Disassembly Direction Index (DDI):** Linear vs. non-linear (1 = fully linear = best).

**Stage 3 — Material Recovery Analysis**
Each component is tagged with:
- Material type (ferrous, non-ferrous, polymer, composite, hazardous)
- Mass fraction
- Recovery pathway (mechanical, chemical, thermal)

**Stage 4 — R6 Matrix Construction**
Rows = EoL requirements (disassembly ease, recyclability, hazardous-free, recovery cost, etc.)
Columns = Recovery process characteristics (sorting efficiency, melt purity, energy per kg, etc.)

**Stage 5 — Strategic Output Generation**
Outputs: Design for Disassembly rules, Material passport, Take-back logistics plan, Circular revenue model.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all quantitatively testable formulas for the R3 and R6 cycles. These are the only formulas a KTU 2024 examiner will accept without derivation in numerical problems.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Application Cycle | Units |
|:-:|:-------------|:------------------------|:-----------------:|:-----:|
| 1 | **Customer/Environmental Importance Weight** | $w_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} \cdot I_j$ | R3, R6 | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Relative Weight (Normalized)** | $\hat{w}_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{m} w_k} \times 100$ | R3, R6 | % |
| 3 | **Process Selection Index (PSI)** | $\text{PSI}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \hat{w}_i \cdot r_{ij}$ | R3 | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Energy Efficiency Indicator** | $\eta_E = \dfrac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Total Energy Input}}$ | R3 | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Process Waste Index (PWI)** | $\text{PWI} = \dfrac{m_{\text{input}} - m_{\text{output}}}{m_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | R3 | % |
| 6 | **Recyclability Ratio (RR)** | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{recoverable}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | R6 | % |
| 7 | **Disassembly Efficiency** | $\eta_D = \dfrac{t_{\text{theoretical min}}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | R6 | % |
| 8 | **Material Recovery Yield** | $Y_R = \dfrac{m_{\text{recovered pure}}}{m_{\text{recovered raw}}} \times 100$ | R6 | % |
| 9 | **Disassembly Direction Index** | $\text{DDI} = 1 - \dfrac{n_{\text{direction changes}}}{n_{\text{total fasteners}}}$ | R6 | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Overall Eco-Effectiveness** | $E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \alpha_k \cdot M_k$ | R3, R6 | Composite index |

> **Notation Glossary:**
> - $r_{ij}$ = Relationship strength (0, 1, 3, 9) between requirement $i$ and process $j$
> - $I_j$ = Original importance rating of customer/environmental requirement $j$
> - $m$ = Number of requirements; $n$ = number of processes
> - $\alpha_k$ = Normalized weighting coefficient for metric $M_k$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **R3 in Automotive Industry:** Tesla's Gigafactory uses R3-style matrices to select between die-casting (Model Y underbody, single-piece) vs. stamping + welding (legacy ICE vehicles) based on energy and scrap metrics.
- **R6 in Electronics:** Apple's **Liam** and **Daisy** robots use R6 logic — disassembly sequences pre-planned at design stage to recover cobalt, lithium, and rare-earth magnets from iPhones with **>95% material purity**.
- **R6 in Appliances:** Whirlpool and Bosch refrigerators design compressor compartments with **single-bolt access** (high DDI) to enable refrigerant recovery in <5 minutes per unit.
