## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: R3 Cycle Process Selection (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem Statement:**
A precision gear manufacturer is evaluating **three manufacturing processes** (CNC Hobbing, Gear Skiving, Powder Metallurgy) for a planetary gear set. Customer and environmental requirements with their importance ratings are:

| Req. ID | Requirement | Importance $I_j$ |
|:-------:|:------------|:----------------:|
| CR1 | High surface hardness | 9 |
| CR2 | Tight dimensional tolerance (±0.02 mm) | 8 |
| CR3 | Low production cost | 6 |
| ER1 | Low energy consumption | 7 |
| ER2 | Minimal process waste | 8 |

The relationship matrix (scale 0/1/3/9) between requirements and processes is given below:

| Req. ID | P1: CNC Hobbing | P2: Gear Skiving | P3: Powder Metallurgy |
|:-------:|:--------------:|:----------------:|:---------------------:|
| CR1 | 9 | 9 | 3 |
| CR2 | 9 | 9 | 3 |
| CR3 | 3 | 3 | 9 |
| ER1 | 3 | 3 | 9 |
| ER2 | 1 | 3 | 9 |

**Required:** Compute the **Absolute Importance Weights** $w_j$ for each process, the **Normalized Weights** $\hat{w}_j$, and recommend the optimal process.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the Absolute Importance Weight Formula**

$$w_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} r_{ij} \cdot I_i$$

We compute this for each of the three processes.

**For Process P1 (CNC Hobbing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{P1} &= (r_{CR1,P1} \cdot I_{CR1}) + (r_{CR2,P1} \cdot I_{CR2}) + (r_{CR3,P1} \cdot I_{CR3}) + (r_{ER1,P1} \cdot I_{ER1}) + (r_{ER2,P1} \cdot I_{ER2}) \\
&= (9 \times 9) + (9 \times 8) + (3 \times 6) + (3 \times 7) + (1 \times 8) \\
&= 81 + 72 + 18 + 21 + 8 \\
&= 200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Each requirement's importance rating is multiplied by its relationship strength with the process, and the results are summed across all requirements.

**For Process P2 (Gear Skiving):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{P2} &= (9 \times 9) + (9 \times 8) + (3 \times 6) + (3 \times 7) + (3 \times 8) \\
&= 81 + 72 + 18 + 21 + 24 \\
&= 216
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Identical to P1 for the first four requirements; the only difference is in ER2 (process waste), where skiving scores a 3 instead of 1, yielding an additional 16 points.

**For Process P3 (Powder Metallurgy):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{P3} &= (3 \times 9) + (3 \times 8) + (9 \times 6) + (9 \times 7) + (9 \times 8) \\
&= 27 + 24 + 54 + 63 + 72 \\
&= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Powder metallurgy scores low on quality requirements (CR1, CR2) but excels on cost and environmental requirements (CR3, ER1, ER2), giving it the highest cumulative score.

---

**Step 2: Compute the Normalized Weights**

$$\hat{w}_j = \frac{w_j}{\sum_{k=1}^{3} w_k} \times 100$$

**Total Weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_k &= w_{P1} + w_{P2} + w_{P3} \\
&= 200 + 216 + 240 \\
&= 656
\end{aligned}
$$

**Normalized weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{w}_{P1} &= \frac{200}{656} \times 100 = 30.49\% \\
\hat{w}_{P2} &= \frac{216}{656} \times 100 = 32.93\% \\
\hat{w}_{P3} &= \frac{240}{656} \times 100 = 36.59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The normalization transforms absolute scores into relative percentages that sum to 100%, enabling direct comparison.

---

**Step 3: Recommendation**

| Process | Absolute Weight | Normalized Weight | Rank |
|:-------:|:---------------:|:-----------------:|:----:|
| CNC Hobbing | 200 | 30.49% | 3 |
| Gear Skiving | 216 | 32.93% | 2 |
| Powder Metallurgy | 240 | **36.59%** | **1** |

> **Recommendation:** **Powder Metallurgy (P3)** is the most suitable process when environmental and cost factors are given equal weight to quality. However, the small margin (≈3.7%) over skiving suggests the company should verify whether the **±0.02 mm tolerance** is technically achievable with PM. If not, **Gear Skiving (P2)** is the next-best eco-conscious choice.

---

### 3.3 R6 Cycle Worked Example: Recyclability Ratio Computation

**Problem Statement:** A washing machine has the following component mass breakdown:

| Component | Material | Mass (kg) | Recovery Pathway | Recovery Yield $Y_R$ |
|:---------:|:---------|:---------:|:-----------------|:--------------------:|
| Outer drum | Stainless steel | 30 | Magnetic separation + melt | 95% |
| Motor | Copper + steel | 8 | Shredding + density sort | 90% |
| Plastic tub | PP polymer | 15 | Mechanical regrind | 80% |
| Control PCB | FR-4 + Cu + hazardous Pb | 2 | Chemical leaching | 70% |
| Concrete counterweight | Concrete | 25 | Downcycle (aggregate) | 100% |
| Cables & insulation | PVC + Cu | 3 | Thermal + mechanical | 60% |
| **Total** | — | **83** | — | — |

**Required:** Compute the **Recyclability Ratio (RR)** and the **Overall Material Recovery Yield (OMRY)**.

---

**Step 4: Compute Mass of Recoverable Material**

$$m_{\text{recoverable}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = 30 + 8 + 15 + 2 + 25 + 3 = 83 \text{ kg}$$

In this design, **all** components are routed to some recovery pathway, so $m_{\text{recoverable}} = m_{\text{total}}$.

**Step 5: Compute the Recyclability Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RR} &= \frac{m_{\text{recoverable}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{83}{83} \times 100 \\
&= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Mass-Average Recovery Yield (OMRY)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OMRY} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i \times Y_{R,i})}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(30 \times 0.95) + (8 \times 0.90) + (15 \times 0.80) + (2 \times 0.70) + (25 \times 1.00) + (3 \times 0.60)}{83} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{28.5 + 7.2 + 12.0 + 1.4 + 25.0 + 1.8}{83} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{75.9}{83} \times 100 \\
&= 91.45\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Although the machine is **100% recyclable** by routing, only **91.45% of the mass is effectively recovered** as usable secondary raw material. The bottleneck is the **PVC cables (60% yield)** and **PCB (70% yield)**, which are the strongest candidates for **redesign priority** in the next product generation.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 QFD/ECQFD Six-Phase Cascade (Master Flow)

The following Mermaid block renders the complete six-phase ECQFD cascade, showing how customer/environmental voice propagates from R1 to R6.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Voice of Customer + Voice of Environment] --> B[R1: Product Planning HOQ]
    B --> C[R2: Parts Deployment HOQ]
    C --> D[R3: Process Planning HOQ]
    D --> E[R4: Production Planning HOQ]
    E --> F[R5: Service Planning HOQ]
    F --> G[R6: Recycling EoL Planning HOQ]
    G --> H[Closed Loop Circular Economy]

    B -. CRs, ERs .-> B
    C -. Engineering Specs .-> C
    D -. Process Plans + Energy + Waste .-> D
    E -. QC, SPC, TPM .-> E
    F -. Service, Maintenance, Upgrade .-> F
    G -. Disassembly, Recovery, Reuse .-> G
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** represent forward flow of design information.
- **Dashed self-loops** highlight the unique focus/output of each House of Quality.
- The **R3 node** is the keystone for manufacturing sustainability; the **R6 node** is the keystone for circularity.

---

### 4.2 R3 House of Quality — Internal Block Architecture

This block diagram details the **anatomy of the R3 matrix**, which is the most heavily tested structure in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R3Top["R2 Part Characteristics Imported as WHATs"]
    R3Body["Relationship Matrix body 9 3 1 0"]
    R3Roof["Roof Correlation Matrix Process Synergies vs Conflicts"]
    R3Weight["Importance Weights wj and Normalized hat wj"]
    R3Env["Environmental Rows Energy PWI HSS"]
    R3Out["Output: Selected Process Plan + Eco Footprint"]

    R3Top --> R3Body
    R3Body --> R3Weight
    R3Roof --- R3Body
    R3Env --> R3Body
    R3Weight --> R3Out
```

**Functional Mapping:**
- **Top block:** Carries the "WHATs" from the previous R2 phase.
- **Center block:** The ∿-shaped body where importance × relationship calculations occur.
- **Roof block:** Captures process↔process interactions (e.g., welding conflicts with subsequent heat treatment).
- **Environmental rows:** ECQFD-specific augmentation — the differentiating feature from traditional QFD.
- **Output block:** Becomes the input to the R4 Production Planning phase.

---

### 4.3 R6 House of Quality — Reverse-Logistics Architecture

The R6 matrix is fundamentally a **reverse mapping** — it takes the product back from the customer to the recovery stream.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Product EoL Collection] --> P2[Sorting and Identification]
    P2 --> P3[Disassembly Strategy: Reuse vs Recycle]
    P3 --> P4[Material Recovery: Mechanical / Chemical / Thermal]
    P4 --> P5[Secondary Raw Material Output]
    P5 --> P6[Feedback to R1 Design Loop]

    subgraph R6Matrix["R6 House of Quality Content"]
        direction LR
        Q1["WHATs EoL Requirements"]
        Q2["HOWs Recovery Process Specs"]
        Q3["Relationship Matrix"]
        Q4["Disassembly Time and DDI"]
        Q5["Recovery Yield YR and RR"]
        Q1 --> Q3
        Q2 --> Q3
        Q3 --> Q4
        Q3 --> Q5
    end

    P3 -. informs .-> R6Matrix
    R6Matrix -. drives .-> P4
```

**Key Insight for Students:** Note the **feedback loop from P6 to R1**. This is what makes ECQFD a **closed-loop** sustainable system — lessons from end-of-life inform the next-generation product design.

---

### 4.4 R3 vs R6 — Comparative Functional Flow

This diagram contrasts the two cycles side-by-side, which is a frequently asked **2-mark KTU comparison question**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R3Block["R3 Cycle: Forward Manufacturing"]
        direction TB
        R3A[Part Specs] --> R3B[Process Selection]
        R3B --> R3C[Energy and Waste Audit]
        R3C --> R3D[Eco Optimized Process Plan]
    end

    subgraph R6Block["R6 Cycle: Reverse Recovery"]
        direction TB
        R6A[End of Life Product] --> R6B[Disassembly Planning]
        R6B --> R6C[Material Stream Sorting]
        R6C --> R6D[Circular Material Passport]
    end

    R3D -. feedback for next gen .-> R6A
    R6D -. material data .-> R3A
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** The R3 → R6 → R3 loop is the **heart of circular manufacturing**. R3 optimizes the **forward** material/energy flow; R6 closes the **reverse** loop.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define Environmentally Conscious Quality Function Deployment (ECQFD). Mention any two environmental indicators integrated in the R3 cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~60 words):**

ECQFD is an **extended QFD framework** that incorporates environmental requirements (ERs), regulatory constraints, and sustainability metrics alongside the traditional voice of the customer across all houses of quality (R1 to R6). It translates the "voice of the environment" into engineering and process specifications.

**Two environmental indicators in the R3 cycle:**
1. **Energy Intensity (MJ/kg of part)**
2. **Process Waste Index (PWI)** — also acceptable: Hazardous Substance Score (HSS), VOC emissions, or coolant toxicity.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition = 1 mark; mention of framework = 1 mark; two environmental indicators (½ + ½) = 1 mark.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is the Recyclability Ratio (RR)? State the ISO standard benchmark for "highly recyclable" products.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~50 words):**

The **Recyclability Ratio (RR)** is the percentage of total product mass that can be routed to a viable material recovery pathway (reuse, remanufacture, or recycle), defined as:

$$
\text{RR} = \frac{m_{\text{recoverable}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

**ISO Standard:** Per **ISO 14062**, a product is classified as **"highly recyclable"** when **RR ≥ 75%**.

> **Mark Distribution:** Formula = 1.5 marks; ISO benchmark with number = 1 mark; correct interpretation = 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) — R3 Cycle Focus**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **R3 Process Planning Cycle** of ECQFD with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **four stages** of R3 matrix construction and highlight **three environmental indicators** that augment the traditional R3 matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer is evaluating **three casting processes** (Sand Casting, Die Casting, Investment Casting) for an aluminum housing. The customer/environmental requirements and their importance ratings are:

| Req. ID | Requirement | Importance $I_j$ |
|:-------:|:-----------|:----------------:|
| R1 | Dimensional accuracy | 9 |
| R2 | Surface finish | 7 |
| R3 | Low cost | 8 |
| R4 | Low energy use | 6 |
| R5 | Low waste generation | 5 |

The relationship matrix is:

| Req. | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Investment Casting |
|:----:|:------------:|:-----------:|:------------------:|
| R1 | 1 | 9 | 9 |
| R2 | 1 | 9 | 9 |
| R3 | 9 | 3 | 1 |
| R4 | 1 | 3 | 9 |
| R5 | 3 | 9 | 3 |

Compute the absolute and normalized weights, and recommend the optimal process. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) [7 marks] — The R3 Process Planning Cycle of ECQFD**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The R3 cycle is the **third House of Quality** in the ECQFD cascade. It takes the **part characteristics** finalized in R2 and converts them into **manufacturing process plans** with explicit **environmental optimization**.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the R3 anatomy. The student is expected to draw:

```
         ┌──────────────────────┐
         │ R2 Part Charact. Im- │
         │ ported as "WHATs"    │
         └──────────┬───────────┘
                    ↓
         ┌──────────────────────┐
         │ Relationship Matrix  │
         │ (Body 9/3/1/0 + Roof)│
         └──────────┬───────────┘
                    ↓
         ┌──────────────────────┐
         │ + Environmental Rows │
         │ (Energy, PWI, HSS)   │
         └──────────┬───────────┘
                    ↓
         ┌──────────────────────┐
         │ Output: Eco-Optimized│
         │ Process Plan         │
         └──────────────────────┘
```

**Four Stages (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Input Ingestion:** The R2 outputs (part specs, tolerances, material grades) become the "WHATs" of R3.
2. **Process Identification:** Candidate manufacturing processes are listed as the "HOWs" (e.g., casting, machining, forming, joining).
3. **Relationship Matrix Construction:** The ∿-shaped body captures WHAT↔HOW correlation (9, 3, 1, 0 scale); the ◿-shaped roof captures process↔process synergy/conflict.
4. **Environmental Augmentation:** Three rows are appended for **Energy Intensity (MJ/kg)**, **Process Waste Index (%)**, and **Hazardous Substance Score**.

**Three Environmental Indicators (1 mark, distribute as ½+½+0):**
- Energy Intensity
- Process Waste Index
- Hazardous Substance Score (or any equivalent: VOC, coolant toxicity)

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Compute absolute weights $w_j$ for each process**

**Sand Casting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{SC} &= (1 \times 9) + (1 \times 7) + (9 \times 8) + (1 \times 6) + (3 \times 5) \\
&= 9 + 7 + 72 + 6 + 15 \\
&= 109
\end{aligned}
$$

**Die Casting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{DC} &= (9 \times 9) + (9 \times 7) + (3 \times 8) + (3 \times 6) + (9 \times 5) \\
&= 81 + 63 + 24 + 18 + 45 \\
&= 231
\end{aligned}
$$

**Investment Casting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{IC} &= (9 \times 9) + (9 \times 7) + (1 \times 8) + (9 \times 6) + (3 \times 5) \\
&= 81 + 63 + 8 + 54 + 15 \\
&= 221
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Normalize the weights (1 mark)**

Total weight = $109 + 231 + 221 = 561$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{w}_{SC} &= \frac{109}{561} \times 100 = 19.43\% \\
\hat{w}_{DC} &= \frac{231}{561} \times 100 = 41.18\% \\
\hat{w}_{IC} &= \frac{221}{561} \times 100 = 39.40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Recommendation (1 mark)**

| Process | Absolute Weight | Normalized Weight | Rank |
|:-------:|:---------------:|:-----------------:|:----:|
| Sand Casting | 109 | 19.43% | 3 |
| **Die Casting** | **231** | **41.18%** | **1** |
| Investment Casting | 221 | 39.40% | 2 |

> **Recommendation:** **Die Casting** is the optimal process. It scores highest because it dominates on accuracy, finish, and waste reduction, while still maintaining acceptable energy and cost profiles. Investment Casting is a close second and would be preferred if extremely intricate geometry is required.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — R6 Cycle Focus (Alternative Choice)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **R6 Recycling / End-of-Life Cycle** of ECQFD. Describe the **five-stage reverse-logistics pipeline** and the **four key metrics** used to evaluate EoL performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive battery pack has the following components:

| Component | Material | Mass (kg) | Recovery Yield $Y_R$ |
|:---------:|:---------|:---------:|:--------------------:|
| Li-ion cells | Li, Co, Ni, Cu, Al | 200 | 92% |
| Aluminum housing | Al 6061 | 50 | 95% |
| Steel tray | Mild steel | 80 | 98% |
| Cooling liquid | Ethylene glycol | 15 | 80% |
| BMS PCB | FR-4 + Cu + ICs | 5 | 75% |
| Wiring harness | Cu + PVC | 10 | 85% |
| **Total** | — | **360** | — |

Compute the **Recyclability Ratio (RR)** and the **Overall Material Recovery Yield (OMRY)**. Comment on the design's compliance with **ISO 14062**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) [7 marks] — R6 Recycling / End-of-Life Cycle of ECQFD**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The R6 cycle is the **sixth and final House of Quality** in the ECQFD framework. It systematically plans for the **end-of-life (EoL)** of the product — covering reuse, remanufacture, recycling, and safe disposal — using a matrix similar in structure to R1–R5 but with reverse-logistics content.

**Five-Stage Reverse-Logistics Pipeline (4 marks, distribute as 1 each for first 4 stages, then 0 for connecting text):**

1. **EoL Scenario Definition:** Partition the product's terminal life into three scenarios — Reuse, Remanufacture, Recycle.
2. **Disassembly Strategy Mapping:** Develop a Disassembly Sequence Diagram (DSD) with Disassembly Depth and Disassembly Time measured.
3. **Material Recovery Analysis:** Tag each component with material type, mass fraction, and recovery pathway.
4. **R6 Matrix Construction:** Build the HoQ with EoL requirements as WHATs and recovery process characteristics as HOWs.
5. **Strategic Output Generation:** Produce DfD rules, Material Passports, Take-Back Logistics Plans.

**Four Key EoL Performance Metrics (2 marks, ½ each):**

- **Recyclability Ratio (RR):** $RR = \frac{m_{\text{recoverable}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100$
- **Disassembly Efficiency ($\eta_D$):** Ratio of theoretical minimum disassembly time to actual time.
- **Disassembly Direction Index (DDI):** Measures linearity of disassembly (1 = perfectly linear).
- **Material Recovery Yield ($Y_R$):** Percentage of recovered mass that is usable secondary raw material.

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Total and Recoverable Mass (1 mark)**

$m_{\text{total}} = 200 + 50 + 80 + 15 + 5 + 10 = 360 \text{ kg}$

Since all components have a designated recovery pathway:
$m_{\text{recoverable}} = 360 \text{ kg}$

**Step 2: Compute Recyclability Ratio (1 mark)**

$$
\text{RR} = \frac{360}{360} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute Mass-Weighted Recovery Yield — OMRY (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OMRY} &= \frac{\sum (m_i \times Y_{R,i})}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(200 \times 0.92) + (50 \times 0.95) + (80 \times 0.98) + (15 \times 0.80) + (5 \times 0.75) + (10 \times 0.85)}{360} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{184 + 47.5 + 78.4 + 12 + 3.75 + 8.5}{360} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{334.15}{360} \times 100 \\
&= 92.82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: ISO 14062 Compliance & Comments (2 marks)**

| Metric | Computed | ISO 14062 Benchmark | Status |
|:------:|:--------:|:-------------------:|:------:|
| RR | 100% | ≥ 75% | ✅ Pass |
| OMRY | 92.82% | ≥ 90% recommended | ✅ Pass (marginal) |

> **Design Comment:** The pack **meets ISO 14062** for recyclability, but the **cooling liquid (80% yield)** and the **BMS PCB (75% yield)** are the weakest links. The PCB recovery is constrained by the presence of **hazardous Pb-free solders and rare ICs**. Recommendations:
> - Substitute ethylene glycol with **water-based or biodegradable coolant** to improve the 80% yield.
> - Adopt a **modular BMS architecture** with snap-fit connectors (high DDI) to allow PCB harvesting before shredding.
> - Print a **digital material passport** on the pack label for downstream recyclers (EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542 compliant).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in R3/R6 Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing R3 with R4:** Students often describe *quality control charts* (which is R4) under R3. **R3 = Process Selection**, R4 = Production/Quality Control.
> 2. **Forgetting the 9/3/1/0 scale:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if you write "rating is 1–5" instead of the standard 9/3/1/0.
> 3. **Skipping the normalization step:** A common 2-mark loss. Always show both $w_j$ AND $\hat{w}_j$.
> 4. **Mixing up RR with OMRY:** RR = "Can it be recycled?" (binary routing). OMRY = "How much is actually recovered?" (yield-weighted). They are NOT the same metric.
> 5. **Omitting the ISO 14062 reference:** Mentioning the standard elevates the answer from "average" to "exemplary" — worth at least ½ mark in valuation.
> 6. **Not drawing the diagram:** Even a rough sketch earns 1 mark; a missing diagram is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — R3 and R6 Cycles**

- ⭐ **ECQFD = QFD + Environmental Requirements** integrated into all six houses.
- ⭐ **R3 = "How to MAKE it green"** (process selection with energy/waste audit).
- ⭐ **R6 = "How to UNMAKE it green"** (end-of-life, recycling, circularity).
- ⭐ The relationship matrix uses the **9 / 3 / 1 / 0** scale — **never** use 1–5.
- ⭐ The **absolute weight formula**: $w_j = \sum r_{ij} \cdot I_i$ — must be normalized to 100%.
- ⭐ **Three environmental rows in R3:** Energy Intensity, Process Waste Index (PWI), Hazardous Substance Score (HSS).
- ⭐ **Five stages in R6 pipeline:** EoL scenario → Disassembly → Material recovery → R6 matrix → Strategic output.
- ⭐ **Four R6 metrics:** RR, $\eta_D$, DDI, $Y_R$ — all dimensionless or %.
- ⭐ **ISO 14062 benchmark:** RR ≥ 75% ⇒ "highly recyclable".
- ⭐ The R3 ↔ R6 **feedback loop** is what distinguishes ECQFD from linear QFD — and is the **essence of the circular economy**.
- ⭐ In numerical problems: **always normalize, always show units, always conclude with a recommendation.**
- ⭐ Common distractors in MCQs: "R3 is for service planning" (FALSE — that's R5); "R6 replaces R4" (FALSE — they coexist).
- ⭐ The **closed loop** in the master flow diagram (R6 → R1 feedback) is the **most frequently diagrammed** element in KTU answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Impact Assessment Methods - CML, EI 95 and EI 99
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Environmental Impact Assessment Methods - CML, EI 95 and EI 99 # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)? **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** is Phase 3 of the ISO 14040/14044 framed Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology. Its purpose is to translate the **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** data (emissions, resource extractions, land use in kg, MJ, m³) into a limited set of **environmental impact scores** that are easier to interpret and act upon. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** LCIA is the phase of LCA aimed at understanding and evaluating the magnitude and significance of the potential environmental impacts of a product system throughout its life cycle. LCIA methods fall broadly into two philosophical camps: | Approach Type | Scientific Anchor | Output | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Midpoint (Problem-Oriented)** | Environmental mechanism | "Stratospheric ozone depletion" | CML, EDIP, ReCiPe Midpoint | | **Endpoint (Damage-Oriented)** | Cause–effect chain ending in human welfare / ecosystem health | "Years of life lost", "Species lost" | EI 95, EI 99, Eco-indicator 99, ReCiPe Endpoint | --- ## 1.2 The Three LCIA Methods Under Study ### A. CML Method (Centrum voor Milieukunde Leiden) > [!NOTE] > **CML** stands for **Centrum voor Milieukunde Leiden** (Center of Environmental Science, Leiden University), Netherlands. First developed in 1992 by Heijungs *et al.* and substantially revised in 2001 as **CML 2001**. The CML method is a **midpoint (problem-oriented)** LCIA method. It characterizes emissions and resource extractions into impact categories such as Global Warming Potential, Acidification, Eutrophication, Ozone Depletion, Human Toxicity, etc., **before** the cause–effect chain reaches its endpoint. **Conceptual Analogy — Midpoint vs. Endpoint:** Think of a doctor's diagnosis. A **midpoint diagnosis** says "Your blood pressure is 160/100 mmHg" (the problem indicator). An **endpoint diagnosis** says "You have a 35% risk of stroke in the next 10 years" (the damage). CML = midpoint diagnosis. EI 95 / EI 99 = endpoint diagnosis. ### B. EI 95 (Environmental Impact 95) > [!NOTE] > **EI 95** is the impact assessment module of the **EPS (Environmental Priority Strategies)** system, developed in 1992 by **Bo Steen and Sven-Olof Ryding** at the Swedish Environmental Research Institute (**IVL**). It was originally created for AB Volvo's environmental product development work. EI 95 is a **damage-oriented (endpoint)** method. It models the full cause–effect chain from emissions through exposure, effect, and damage, and finally expresses the damage in **"Environmental Load Units" (ELU)**. The weighting is based on society's **willingness-to-pay (WTP)** to avoid the damage. ### C. EI 99 (Environmental Impact 99) > [!NOTE] > **EI 99** is the 1999 update of the EPS methodology, published by Bo Steen of IVL, Sweden. It revises the characterization factors, valuation principles, and safeguard subjects of EI 95 to reflect newer science and policy. EI 99 retains the endpoint orientation of EI 95 but updates the science and introduces a clearer **Safeguard Subject → Damage Category → Impact Category** hierarchy. It is also sometimes referenced as part of the **EPS 2000** system. --- ## 1.3 Quick Comparative Snapshot | Feature | CML | EI 95 | EI 99 | |---|---|---|---| | **Year** | 1992 / 2001 | 1992 | 1999 | | **Developer** | Heijungs *et al.*, Leiden University | Steen & Ryding, IVL Sweden | Steen, IVL Sweden | | **Orientation** | Midpoint | Endpoint | Endpoint | | **Result Unit** | kg-eq (e.g., kg CO₂-eq, kg SO₂-eq) | ELU (Environmental Load Unit) | ELU (updated) | | **Weighting** | Optional / external | Built-in (WTP-based) | Built-in (WTP-based) | | **No. of Impact Categories** | ~10–18 | ~13 | ~13 (revised) | | **Key Strength** | Scientific transparency | Single aggregated score | Improved transparency + updated science | | **Key Weakness** | Less intuitive | Subjective valuation | Subjective valuation | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 ISO 14040/14044 LCIA Framework (Mandatory Elements) According to **ISO 14044 §4.4**, LCIA consists of **mandatory** and **optional** elements: ``` MANDATORY: 1. Selection of Impact Categories 2. Classification (Assignment of LCI results → categories) 3. Characterization (Calculation of category indicator results) OPTIONAL: 4. Normalization 5. Grouping 6. Weighting ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **CML** performs steps 1–3 rigorously and treats 4–6 as optional. **EI 95 / EI 99** go all the way to step 6 (weighting) to produce a single aggregated score. --- ## 2.2 Deep Dive — CML Method ### 2.2.1 Philosophy CML is **midpoint, problem-oriented** — it stops at the "environmental problem" stage. The reasoning: **less uncertainty**. Many assumptions about exposure, dose–response, and valuation are avoided, making the science auditable. ### 2.2.2 Standard CML Impact Categories (CML 2001 baseline) 1. Abiotic resource depletion 2. Acidification potential 3. Eutrophication potential 4. Global warming potential (GWP 100) 5. Ozone layer depletion potential (ODP) 6. Human toxicity potential 7. Freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity potential 8. Marine aquatic ecotoxicity potential 9. Terrestrial ecotoxicity potential 10. Photochemical ozone creation potential 11. Land use ### 2.2.3 Characterization Formula For every substance *i* emitted to compartment *c*, with **characterization factor** *CF(i,c)*, the **category indicator result** is: $$I_{cat} = \sum_{i} \sum_{c} CF(i,c) \cdot m_{i,c}$$ Where: - $I_{cat}$ = category indicator (e.g., kg CO₂-eq for global warming) - $m_{i,c}$ = mass of substance *i* emitted to compartment *c* (from LCI) - $CF(i,c)$ = characterization factor (e.g., GWP of CH₄ = 25 kg CO₂-eq) ### 2.2.4 Engineering Utility Used in **comparative product LCAs**, **carbon footprinting**, **EPDs (Environmental Product Declarations)**, and **manufacturing process selection** (e.g., choosing between machining and additive manufacturing based on GWP, acidification, particulate matter). --- ## 2.3 Deep Dive — EI 95 Method ### 2.3.1 Philosophy EI 95 is **endpoint, damage-oriented**. It uses the entire causal chain: > **Emission → Fate & Transport → Exposure → Dose–Response → Damage → Monetary Value** The valuation principle is **WTP (Willingness-To-Pay)** — society's willingness to pay to avoid or restore the damage. ### 2.3.2 Impact Categories (13 in EI 95) 1. Life expectancy (Human health — mortality) 2. Severe morbidity 3. Morbidity 4. Severe nuisance 5. Nuisance 6. Crop growth capacity 7. Wood growth capacity 8. Fish and meat production 9. Production capacity for drinking water 10. Soil acidification capacity 11. Depletion of abiotic resources 12. Depletion of biotic resources 13. Depletion of fossil resources ### 2.3.3 Unit — Environmental Load Unit (ELU) All categories are converted into **ELU per kg emission**. The conversion uses **Environmental Protection Cost (EPC)**: the cost to society of reducing (or restoring) the impact by one unit. ### 2.3.4 Characterization Formula (Generic) $$ELU_{total} = \sum_{i} \sum_{c} CF_{EI95}(i,c) \cdot m_{i,c}$$ Where $CF_{EI95}(i,c)$ is the EI 95 characterization factor expressed in **ELU/kg**. --- ## 2.4 Deep Dive — EI 99 Method ### 2.4.1 Philosophy EI 99 inherits the endpoint orientation of EI 95 but introduces: - **Safeguard Subjects** (three: *Human Health, Ecosystem Production Capacity, Abiotic Resources*) - **Damage Categories** (intermediate groupings under each safeguard subject) - **Impact Categories** (the actual operational indicators) - Updated characterization factors reflecting post-1995 science - Clear separation of **operational** vs. **non-operational** safeguard subjects ### 2.4.2 Hierarchy Structure ``` Safeguard Subject (3) ↓ Damage Category (e.g., Human Health → Respiratory Effects) ↓ Impact Category (e.g., DALY/kg emission) ↓ Characterization Factor (CF) ``` ### 2.4.3 Formula (Same structure, updated CFs) $$ELU_{99} = \sum_{i} \sum_{c} CF_{EI99}(i,c) \cdot m_{i,c}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Eco-indicator 99** (PRé Consultants, Huijbregts et al.) with **EI 99** (IVL Sweden, Steen). They are **different methods** but both endpoint-oriented. The KTU syllabus (and most Indian textbook treatments of LCIA) refers specifically to the **IVL EI 95 / EI 99** family as developed by Steen. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Method | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $I_{cat} = \sum CF(i,c) \cdot m_{i,c}$ | Category indicator result | All three | kg-eq or ELU | | $GWP = \sum CF_{CO_2}(i) \cdot m_i$ | Global warming potential | CML | kg CO₂-eq | | $AP = \sum CF_{SO_2}(i) \cdot m_i$ | Acidification potential | CML | kg SO₂-eq | | $EP = \sum CF_{PO_4}(i) \cdot m_i$ | Eutrophication potential | CML | kg PO₄³⁻-eq | | $ODP = \sum CF_{CFC11}(i) \cdot m_i$ | Ozone depletion potential | CML | kg CFC-11-eq | | $HTP = \sum CF(i) \cdot m_i$ | Human toxicity potential | CML | kg 1,4-DCB-eq | | $ELU = \sum CF_{EI95}(i,c) \cdot m_{i,c}$ | Environmental load unit (EI 95) | EI 95 | ELU | | $ELU = \sum CF_{EI99}(i,c) \cdot m_{i,c}$ | Environmental load unit (EI 99) | EI 99 | ELU | | $EPC$ | Environmental protection cost (WTP-based) | EI 95/99 | €/unit damage | | $DALY$ | Disability-Adjusted Life Years (health endpoint) | EI 99 | yr | | $PDF \cdot m^{2} \cdot yr$ | Potentially Disappeared Fraction of species (ecosystem endpoint) | EI 99 | species·m²·yr | > Boundary Conditions: > - System boundary must be **identical** when comparing alternatives. > - Functional unit must be **fixed**. > - Allocation procedures (cut-off, system expansion, economic) must be **consistent** across methods. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Manufacturing | Method | Use Case in Manufacturing | |---|---| | **CML** | Selecting between two machining strategies; carbon-footprinting a factory; ISO 14067 product carbon footprint | | **EI 95** | Aggregated single-score comparison of alternative product designs (e.g., steel vs. aluminium car body) | | **EI 99** | Strategic product portfolio decisions where trade-offs between human health, ecosystem, and resources must be weighed | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Global Warming with CML **Problem:** A manufacturing process emits the following over one functional unit: - 100 kg of CO₂ - 5 kg of CH₄ - 0.5 kg of N₂O Using CML characterization factors (IPCC GWP 100, AR5 values commonly used in CML 2001 baseline): - CF(CO₂) = **1 kg CO₂-eq/kg** - CF(CH₄) = **25 kg CO₂-eq/kg** - CF(N₂O) = **298 kg CO₂-eq/kg** **Derive the Global Warming Potential (GWP).** --- ### Step 1 — State the CML Midpoint Formula $$GWP = \sum_{i=1}^{n} CF_{GWP}(i) \cdot m_i$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Three Substances $$GWP = CF(CO_2) \cdot m_{CO_2} + CF(CH_4) \cdot m_{CH_4} + CF(N_2O) \cdot m_{N_2O}$$ ### Step 3 — Insert Numerical Values $$GWP = (1 \cdot 100) + (25 \cdot 5) + (298 \cdot 0.5)$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate Each Term $$GWP = 100 + 125 + 149$$ ### Step 5 — Final Result $$\boxed{GWP = 374 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq per functional unit}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Single-Score Aggregation with EI 99 **Problem:** A product system emits: - 374 kg CO₂-eq (climate change impact) - 1.2 kg SO₂-eq (acidification) EI 99 aggregation (illustrative safeguard subject weighting): - Climate change damage factor: $w_{climate} = 0.40$ ELU/kg CO₂-eq - Acidification damage factor: $w_{acid} = 1.10$ ELU/kg SO₂-eq > *Note: Real EI 99 factors are derived through full damage pathways; values shown are illustrative.* ### Step 1 — Apply EI 99 Aggregation Formula $$ELU_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot I_{k}$$ Where $I_k$ is the category indicator for category *k*, and $w_k$ is the corresponding ELU weighting factor. ### Step 2 — Substitute Values $$ELU_{total} = (0.40 \cdot 374) + (1.10 \cdot 1.2)$$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate $$ELU_{total} = 149.6 + 1.32$$ ### Step 4 — Final Result $$\boxed{ELU_{total} \approx 150.92 \text{ ELU per functional unit}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Cross-Method Comparison Matrix (Analytical) Suppose two alternative manufacturing routes (A = conventional machining; B = additive manufacturing) yield the LCI data below for one component: | LCI Flow | Route A (kg) | Route B (kg) | |---|---|---| | CO₂ | 100 | 60 | | CH₄ | 5 | 1 | | SO₂ | 2.0 | 0.5 | | PO₄³⁻ (to water) | 0.05 | 0.02 | | CFC-11-eq | 0 | 0.0001 | | Crude oil | 80 | 50 | | Method | Route A Result | Route B Result | Lower-Impact Route | |---|---|---|---| | **CML — GWP** | 100 + 125 = **225 kg CO₂-eq** | 60 + 25 = **85 kg CO₂-eq** | **B** | | **CML — AP** | 2.0 · 1 = **2.0 kg SO₂-eq** | 0.5 · 1 = **0.5 kg SO₂-eq** | **B** | | **CML — EP** | 0.05 · 1 = **0.05 kg PO₄³⁻-eq** | 0.02 · 1 = **0.02 kg PO₄³⁻-eq** | **B** | | **CML — ODP** | 0 | 0.0001 kg CFC-11-eq | **A** | | **CML — Abiotic Depletion** | 80 · 1 = **80 kg oil-eq** (illustrative) | 50 · 1 = **50 kg oil-eq** | **B** | | **EI 99 — Aggregated ELU** | Sum w·I for all five categories | Same calculation | **Depends on weights** | ### Key Insight CML reveals **which environmental problem** is affected (B is worse for ozone layer, better for everything else). EI 99 collapses everything into one number, hiding this **trade-off**. This is the central methodological debate in LCIA. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Question — Why Does CML Prefer Midpoints? **Reasoning chain:** ``` Step 1: Endpoint methods require modeling Fate, Exposure, Dose-Response, and Valuation. Step 2: Each step introduces scientific uncertainty (e.g., regional variability in fate). Step 3: At endpoints, uncertainties multiply (Monte Carlo uncertainty grows). Step 4: Midpoints are closer to the inventory → fewer model assumptions → less uncertainty. Step 5: Therefore, midpoint results (CML) are more defensible scientifically. Step 6: Endpoint results (EI 95/99) are more communicable to decision-makers. Step 7: Conclusion: Use CML for rigorous comparison; use EI 95/99 for strategic single-score ranking. ``` This is known in the LCIA community as the **"Midpoint–Endpoint Trade-off"** and is a frequently tested KTU concept. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LCIA Methodology Flow (ISO 14044) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[LCI Results
kg, MJ, m³] --> B[Selection of
Impact Categories] B --> C[Classification
Assignment to Categories] C --> D[Characterization
CF · mass] D --> E{Category Indicator
Result} E -->|Optional| F[Normalization] F --> G[Grouping] G --> H[Weighting] H --> I[Single Score] style A fill:#e1f5ff style D fill:#fff4e1 style E fill:#ffe1e1 style I fill:#e1ffe1 ``` --- ## 4.2 Cause–Effect Chain (Why Endpoints Have More Uncertainty) ```mermaid flowchart LR E[Emission
kg] --> F[Fate and Transport] F --> X[Exposure] X --> R[Dose Response] R --> D[Damage
Endpoint] D --> V[Valuation
WTP] V --> S[Single Score
ELU] style E fill:#e1f5ff style S fill:#e1ffe1 style D fill:#ffe1e1 ``` > [!NOTE] > Each arrow in the diagram represents a **scientific model step** that adds uncertainty. CML stops early (between F and X). EI 95/99 go all the way to V. --- ## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — CML vs EI 95 vs EI 99 ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CML_BLOCK[CM L METHOD] C1[Midpoint Categories
10 to 18 impact categories] --> C2[Characterization
kg eq substance] C2 --> C3[No mandatory weighting
Optional external] end subgraph EI95_BLOCK[EI 95 METHOD] E1[13 Endpoint Categories] --> E2[Damage Pathway
WTP based] E2 --> E3[ELU Aggregation
Single Score] end subgraph EI99_BLOCK[EI 99 METHOD] I1[3 Safeguard Subjects] --> I2[Damage Categories] I2 --> I3[Impact Categories] I3 --> I4[Updated CFs
ELU Aggregation] end LCI[LCI Data] --> CML_BLOCK LCI --> EI95_BLOCK LCI --> EI99_BLOCK style LCI fill:#fff4e1 style CML_BLOCK fill:#e1f5ff style EI95_BLOCK fill:#ffe1e1 style EI99_BLOCK fill:#e1ffe1 ``` --- ## 4.4 EI 99 Hierarchical Architecture (Safeguard Subjects) ```mermaid flowchart TB SS[EI 99 Safeguard Subjects] SS --> HH[Human Health] SS --> EPC[Ecosystem Production Capacity] SS --> AR[Abiotic Resources] HH --> DC1[Damage Categories
e.g. Respiratory Effects
Cardiovascular Effects] HH --> IC1[Impact Category
e.g. DALY per kg] EPC --> DC2[Damage Categories
e.g. Terrestrial Ecotoxicity
Land Use] EPC --> IC2[Impact Category
e.g. PDF m² yr] AR --> DC3[Damage Categories
e.g. Mineral Depletion
Fossil Depletion] AR --> IC3[Impact Category
e.g. MJ surplus] style SS fill:#ffe1e1 style HH fill:#fff4e1 style EPC fill:#e1ffe1 style AR fill:#e1f5ff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model] **Define the CML method of Life Cycle Impact Assessment. Mention any six impact categories used in CML 2001.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **CML Method (1 mark):** CML stands for *Centrum voor Milieukunde Leiden*, the Center of Environmental Science at Leiden University, Netherlands. It is a **midpoint (problem-oriented)** LCIA method developed by Heijungs *et al.* in 1992 and updated as CML 2001. It classifies LCI flows into impact categories and characterizes them using scientifically-derived characterization factors expressed as equivalents of a reference substance. > > **Six Impact Categories (2 marks — ½ mark each, naming any six):** > 1. Abiotic resource depletion > 2. Acidification potential (AP) > 3. Eutrophication potential (EP) > 4. Global warming potential (GWP 100) > 5. Ozone layer depletion potential (ODP) > 6. Human toxicity potential (HTP) > > *(Acceptable also: Freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity, Marine aquatic ecotoxicity, Terrestrial ecotoxicity, Photochemical ozone creation, Land use.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **What is meant by "Environmental Load Unit (ELU)"? Name the LCIA methods that use this unit.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** The *Environmental Load Unit (ELU)* is the aggregated, dimensionless (or rather, ELU-specific) single-score result unit used in endpoint LCIA methods. One ELU represents a normalized economic-environmental damage burden, derived by multiplying each emission's damage factor (incorporating exposure, dose–response, and society's willingness-to-pay to prevent the damage) by the emission quantity, then summing across categories. It allows comparison and aggregation of very different environmental impacts (e.g., toxicity, climate change, resource depletion) into a single number. > > **Methods that use ELU (1 mark):** **EI 95** (Environmental Impact 95) and **EI 99** (Environmental Impact 99), both developed by Bo Steen and colleagues at IVL, Sweden, as part of the EPS (Environmental Priority Strategies) family. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper] **(a)** With neat schematics, explain the **midpoint** and **endpoint** approaches in LCIA. Compare the CML method with the EI 95 method along any **seven** parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A manufacturing process emits per functional unit: **120 kg CO₂**, **4 kg CH₄**, **0.8 kg N₂O**, and **2.5 kg SO₂**. Using CML characterization factors (CF for GWP: CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 25, N₂O = 298 kg CO₂-eq/kg; CF for AP: SO₂ = 1 kg SO₂-eq/kg), calculate the **Global Warming Potential** and **Acidification Potential** of the process. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Midpoint vs Endpoint (7 marks)** | # | Parameter | CML (Midpoint) | EI 95 (Endpoint) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Orientation** | Problem-oriented (midpoint) | Damage-oriented (endpoint) | | 2 | **Stage in cause–effect chain** | Early (close to inventory) | Late (after dose–response, valuation) | | 3 | **Result unit** | kg-eq of reference substance (e.g., kg CO₂-eq) | ELU (Environmental Load Unit) | | 4 | **Number of categories** | ~10–18 | 13 | | 5 | **Weighting** | Optional, not built-in | Built-in, WTP-based | | 6 | **Scientific uncertainty** | Lower | Higher (multiple modeling steps) | | 7 | **Communicability to decision-makers** | Moderate | High (single score) | | 8 | **Developed by / Year** | Heijungs *et al.*, 1992 (revised 2001) | Steen & Ryding, 1992 | **Schematics expected (drawing marks):** - A cause–effect chain showing where midpoint ends and endpoint ends. - Block diagrams showing CML pipeline and EI 95 pipeline. **Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks)** **GWP Calculation:** $$GWP = \sum CF_{GWP}(i) \cdot m_i$$ $$GWP = (1 \cdot 120) + (25 \cdot 4) + (298 \cdot 0.8)$$ $$GWP = 120 + 100 + 238.4$$ $$\boxed{GWP = 458.4 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}}$$ **AP Calculation:** $$AP = \sum CF_{AP}(i) \cdot m_i$$ $$AP = (1 \cdot 2.5)$$ $$\boxed{AP = 2.5 \text{ kg SO}_2\text{-eq}}$$ (Only SO₂ contributes; CO₂, CH₄, N₂O are not acidifying species.) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper — Alternative Choice] **(a)** Describe the **EI 95** and **EI 99** methods of LCIA. Highlight the key improvements introduced in EI 99 over EI 95. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The LCI of a product system yields **150 kg CO₂-eq** (GWP), **3 kg SO₂-eq** (AP), and **0.6 kg CFC-11-eq** (ODP). Using EI 99-style aggregated weighting factors (illustrative): $w_{GWP}=0.40$, $w_{AP}=1.10$, $w_{ODP}=10.0$ ELU per kg-eq, compute the **total ELU score**. State which category dominates the impact. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — EI 95 vs EI 99 (7 marks)** **EI 95 (3.5 marks):** - Developed by **Bo Steen and Sven-Olof Ryding (1992)** at IVL, Sweden. - Part of the **EPS (Environmental Priority Strategies)** system, originally for **AB Volvo**. - **Endpoint, damage-oriented** approach. - Uses **13 impact categories** spanning human health, ecosystem production, and resource depletion. - Valuation by **Willingness-To-Pay (WTP)** / Environmental Protection Cost (EPC). - Output unit: **ELU (Environmental Load Unit)**. - Single aggregated score allows ranking of alternatives. **EI 99 (3.5 marks):** - Developed by **Bo Steen (1999)** as a major update of EI 95. - Introduces a **three-tier hierarchy**: 1. **Safeguard Subjects** (Human Health, Ecosystem Production Capacity, Abiotic Resources) 2. **Damage Categories** (intermediate) 3. **Impact Categories** (operational indicators like DALY, PDF·m²·yr, MJ surplus) - **Updated characterization factors** reflecting post-1995 science. - **Clearer separation** of operational vs. non-operational safeguard subjects. - Improved **transparency** and documentation of damage pathway assumptions. - Known as part of the **EPS 2000** framework. **Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks)** **EI 99 Aggregated Score Formula:** $$ELU_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot I_k$$ **Substitute the three categories:** $$ELU_{total} = (0.40 \cdot 150) + (1.10 \cdot 3) + (10.0 \cdot 0.6)$$ **Evaluate each term:** $$ELU_{total} = 60.0 + 3.3 + 6.0$$ $$\boxed{ELU_{total} = 69.3 \text{ ELU per functional unit}}$$ **Dominant Category:** Compute percentage contributions: - GWP: 60.0 / 69.3 = **86.6 %** - AP: 3.3 / 69.3 = 4.8 % - ODP: 6.0 / 69.3 = **8.7 %** > **Conclusion (1 mark):** Despite its very low characterization factor (10.0), the ODP impact contributes only 8.7 %, but if the CFC-11-eq emission were increased or weight decreased, it would dominate. **GWP dominates at 86.6 %**, meaning mitigation efforts should first target CO₂ emissions in this product system. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing EI 99 with Eco-indicator 99:** They are **different methods** from different organizations. Eco-indicator 99 is by PRé Consultants (Huijbregts et al.). EI 99 is by IVL (Steen). KTU syllabus = **IVL's EI 99**. > 2. **Forgetting units:** Always quote final values with units (kg CO₂-eq, kg SO₂-eq, ELU). > 3. **Mixing orientation in a single comparison:** Do **not** aggregate CML midpoint categories with EI 99 ELU scores — they have different denominators. > 4. **Skipping the formula statement:** Even in 7-mark problems, examiners expect the formula written before substitution. > 5. **Missing classification:** If asked for "AP" or "ODP", ensure the substances are correctly classified (e.g., CO₂ does not contribute to AP or ODP). --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must-memorize for KTU):** - **CML** = **C**entrum voor **M**ilieukunde **L**eiden; **midpoint**; **kg-eq** units; 10–18 categories; **Heijungs et al. (1992, revised 2001)**; **lower uncertainty**; **no mandatory weighting**. - **EI 95** = **E**nvironmental **I**mpact **95**; **endpoint**; **ELU** units; 13 categories; **Steen & Ryding (1992)** at **IVL Sweden**; **WTP-based valuation**; originally for **Volvo**. - **EI 99** = update of EI 95; **Steen (1999)**; introduces **3 Safeguard Subjects** (Human Health, Ecosystem Production Capacity, Abiotic Resources); **revised CFs**; part of **EPS 2000**. - **NOT the same** as Eco-indicator 99 (different developer). - **Core formula (all methods):** $I_{cat} = \sum CF(i,c) \cdot m_{i,c}$ - **GWP example value to remember:** CH₄ = **25**, N₂O = **298** kg CO₂-eq/kg (CML / IPCC AR5). - **Midpoint vs. Endpoint trade-off:** Midpoint = more science, less communication; Endpoint = more communication, more uncertainty. - **LCIA = Phase 3 of LCA** (per ISO 14040/14044); mandatory elements = Selection, Classification, Characterization; optional = Normalization, Grouping, Weighting. - **Manufacturing context:** Use CML for **process comparison** (carbon footprinting, ISO 14067); use EI 99 for **portfolio-level** strategic decisions.

topics.title: ISO 14001, EMS and PAS 2050 Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** ISO 14001, EMS and PAS 2050 Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 ISO 14001 — The Global Benchmark for Environmental Stewardship

**ISO 14001** is an internationally recognized standard published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** that specifies the requirements for an effective **Environmental Management System (EMS)**. The current revision is **ISO 14001:2015**, which replaced the earlier 2004 version. It follows the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** — also called Annex SL — that aligns it with other major ISO management standards (ISO 9001 for Quality, ISO 45001 for Occupational Health & Safety).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme expects students to articulate the *structure* of ISO 14001, the *PDCA cycle*, and the role of *continuous improvement* in an EMS. Memorize the seven clauses (4 through 10) — they appear in nearly every exam.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Houseplant Care Manual"

Imagine you have a houseplant. To keep it healthy, you need a **manual** that says:
- Check the soil, light, and temperature (**Context of the Organization**)
- Decide when and how much to water (**Planning**)
- Actually do the watering (**Support & Operation**)
- Check the leaves for yellowing (**Performance Evaluation**)
- Adjust your watering schedule (**Improvement**)

**ISO 14001 is that "manual" — but for a factory, a process, or an entire company interacting with the environment.** It tells a business *how* to identify, manage, and reduce its environmental footprint in a structured, auditable way.

---

## 1.2 EMS — Environmental Management System (The Engine of the Standard)

An **Environmental Management System (EMS)** is the *set of policies, procedures, processes, and practices* that an organization uses to implement its environmental policy and manage its environmental aspects. ISO 14001 is the *framework*; the EMS is the *actual working system* the company builds within that framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (ISO 14001:2015, Clause 3.1.2):**
> *"Environmental management system — part of the management system used to manage environmental aspects, fulfil compliance obligations, and address risks and opportunities."*

### Key Components of an EMS

| Component | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Environmental Policy** | Top-management commitment statement |
| **Planning** | Identify aspects, obligations, risks, objectives |
| **Implementation & Operation** | Resources, competence, communication, documentation |
| **Checking & Corrective Action** | Monitoring, measurement, internal audit, non-conformity |
| **Management Review** | Top management evaluates EMS suitability |
| **Continual Improvement** | PDCA-driven enhancement cycle |

---

## 1.3 PAS 2050 — The Carbon Footprint Calculator

**PAS 2050** stands for **Publicly Available Specification 2050**. It is a method for assessing the **life-cycle greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions** of goods and services, published by the **British Standards Institution (BSI)** in 2008, with a major update in **2011**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PAS 2050 matters in Manufacturing:** While ISO 14001 tells you *how to manage* environmental impacts, PAS 2050 tells you *how to measure* the carbon footprint of a specific product from **cradle to grave** (raw material extraction → manufacture → distribution → use → disposal).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Ingredients Nutrition Label"

Think of a packet of biscuits. The nutrition label tells you the calories, sugar, fat, and protein. **PAS 2050 is the "carbon label"** of a product — it tells you exactly how much **CO₂-equivalent (CO₂e)** was emitted to make, transport, use, and dispose of that product. Two biscuits may look identical, but one might have a "carbon label" of **0.8 kg CO₂e** while the other has **1.5 kg CO₂e** because of how its ingredients were sourced and processed.

---

## 1.4 Quick Comparative Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of This Module**

| Feature | **ISO 14001** | **EMS** | **PAS 2050** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | International Standard | Management Framework | Assessment Method |
| **Focus** | Environmental management system | Implementation of ISO 14001 | Product carbon footprint |
| **Scope** | Whole organization | Whole organization | Specific product/service |
| **Approach** | PDCA cycle (Annex SL) | Operational deployment | Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) based |
| **Output** | EMS certification | Effective environmental practices | kg CO₂e per functional unit |
| **Issuing Body** | ISO (Geneva) | Internal to organization | BSI (UK) |
| **Mandatory?** | Voluntary (market-driven) | Required if pursuing ISO 14001 | Voluntary (regulatory / branding) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DNA of ISO 14001:2015 — The High-Level Structure (Annex SL)

The 2015 revision unified the structure of all ISO management system standards. ISO 14001:2015 contains **10 clauses**:

- **Clauses 1–3**: Scope, Normative References, Terms & Definitions (informational)
- **Clause 4**: Context of the Organization
- **Clause 5**: Leadership
- **Clause 6**: Planning
- **Clause 7**: Support
- **Clause 8**: Operation
- **Clause 9**: Performance Evaluation
- **Clause 10**: Improvement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask: *"Explain the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle as applied to ISO 14001."* You must map the four PDCA phases onto Clauses 4–10.

### Mapping PDCA onto ISO 14001 Clauses

| PDCA Phase | ISO 14001 Clauses | What the Organization Does |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | 4 (Context), 6 (Planning) | Identify issues, risks, opportunities, environmental aspects, compliance obligations |
| **Do** | 7 (Support), 8 (Operation) | Provide resources, competence, awareness, communication, operational controls |
| **Check** | 9 (Performance Evaluation) | Monitor, measure, analyze, audit, evaluate compliance |
| **Act** | 10 (Improvement) | Non-conformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

---

## 2.2 The Seven Pillars of an Effective EMS (Logical Breakdown)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Logic of an EMS**

1. **Establish Environmental Policy** → Top management defines intent and direction.
2. **Identify Environmental Aspects & Impacts** → What inputs/outputs affect the environment? (e.g., wastewater, emissions, waste).
3. **Determine Legal & Other Requirements** → Local, national, international laws, plus voluntary commitments.
4. **Set Objectives & Targets** → Measurable goals (e.g., "reduce water use by 20% in 18 months").
5. **Implement Operational Controls** → SOPs, training, emergency preparedness.
6. **Monitor & Measure** → KPIs, internal audits, compliance evaluation.
7. **Review & Improve** → Management review, corrective actions, prevent recurrence.

---

## 2.3 PAS 2050 — The Carbon Footprint Methodology

PAS 2050 follows a **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** approach and considers **six greenhouse gases** regulated under the Kyoto Protocol:

- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**
- **Methane (CH₄)**
- **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)**
- **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)**
- **Perfluorocarbons (PFCs)**
- **Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆)**

Each gas is converted to **CO₂-equivalent (CO₂e)** using its **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**.

### PAS 2050 Life-Cycle Stages

| Stage | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pre-production (Cradle to Gate)** | Raw material extraction, processing, supplier activities |
| **Production (Gate to Gate)** | Manufacturing, assembly, factory utilities |
| **Distribution & Storage** | Transport to warehouses, retail outlets, refrigeration |
| **Use** | Consumer usage, energy/water during product life |
| **End-of-Life** | Disposal, recycling, landfill, incineration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Boundary Rule:** PAS 2050 distinguishes between **Cradle-to-Grave** (full life cycle) and **Cradle-to-Gate** (up to factory exit). Be explicit in exam answers about which boundary you adopt.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Carbon Footprint & GHG Quantification

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Equations for PAS 2050 / Carbon Calculations**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CO_{2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)$ | Total CO₂-equivalent emissions | kg CO₂e |
| 2 | $CF_{product} = \frac{\sum GHG_{emissions}}{Functional\ Unit}$ | Carbon Footprint per functional unit | kg CO₂e / unit |
| 3 | $GWP_i = \frac{Energy\ absorbed\ by\ 1\ kg\ GHG_i}{Energy\ absorbed\ by\ 1\ kg\ CO_2}$ | Global Warming Potential (100-year) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_{total} = E_{direct} + E_{indirect} + E_{other}$ | Sum of Scope 1, 2, and other indirect emissions | kg CO₂e |
| 5 | $E_{saved} = Baseline_{emissions} - Project_{emissions}$ | Emission reduction from a project | kg CO₂e / year |

### Variable Definitions

- $AD_i$ = **Activity Data** (e.g., litres of fuel, kWh of electricity, tonnes of material)
- $EF_i$ = **Emission Factor** (e.g., **2.68 kg CO₂e per litre of diesel**)
- $GWP_i$ = **Global Warming Potential** of gas $i$ (e.g., CH₄ = **28**, N₂O = **265** — IPCC AR5, 100-yr values)
- $Functional\ Unit$ = the defined reference quantity to which emissions are normalized (e.g., 1 litre of packaged milk)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GWP Reference Values (KTU High-Yield Constants — memorize these):**
> - **CO₂ = 1**
> - **CH₄ = 28**
> - **N₂O = 265**
> - **SF₆ = 23,500**
> - **HFC-134a = 1,300**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISO 14001**: Used by over **400,000 certified organizations** globally (ISO Survey 2022). Mandatory for suppliers to many automotive OEMs (e.g., Tata Motors, Ford).
- **EMS**: Reduces regulatory fines, lowers insurance premiums, improves resource efficiency.
- **PAS 2050**: Used by **Tesco, Walmart, and Unilever** for product carbon labelling. Basis for the **Carbon Trust** standard and most ISO 14067 product carbon footprint studies.
- **Manufacturing Edge**: A factory certified to ISO 14001 + PAS 2050 can win **green procurement** contracts, access **carbon credits**, and comply with **CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism)** in the EU.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Carbon Footprint of a Manufactured Product

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A factory produces **1,000 litres of packaged drinking water**. The following data is available. Calculate the **carbon footprint per litre** using the PAS 2050 framework.

**Given Data (Cradle-to-Gate):**

| Activity | Activity Data (AD) | Emission Factor (EF) | GHG |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electricity for production | 500 kWh | 0.82 kg CO₂e / kWh | CO₂ |
| Diesel for forklift | 40 litres | 2.68 kg CO₂e / litre | CO₂ |
| Process methane leak | 0.5 kg | — | CH₄ |
| Refrigerant (HFC-134a) top-up | 0.02 kg | — | HFC-134a |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Functional Unit (FU).**

For product carbon footprints, the FU is the unit the consumer perceives. Here, the FU is **1 litre of packaged water**.

$$FU = 1\ litre\ packaged\ drinking\ water$$

**Step 2 — Compute direct CO₂ emissions from electricity.**

Electricity is a Scope 2 emission. Use the EF directly (already in CO₂e):

$$E_{elec} = AD_{elec} \times EF_{elec}$$

$$E_{elec} = 500\ kWh \times 0.82\ kg\ CO_2e / kWh$$

$$E_{elec} = 410\ kg\ CO_2e$$

**Step 3 — Compute direct CO₂ emissions from diesel combustion.**

Diesel fuel combustion is Scope 1. The EF is already a CO₂e value:

$$E_{diesel} = AD_{diesel} \times EF_{diesel}$$

$$E_{diesel} = 40\ litres \times 2.68\ kg\ CO_2e / litre$$

$$E_{diesel} = 107.2\ kg\ CO_2e$$

**Step 4 — Convert methane (CH₄) mass to CO₂-equivalent.**

The CH₄ mass given is the **activity data** (in kg of CH₄ emitted). The emission factor is implicit in the GWP. Use the master equation:

$$CO_{2e} = AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i$$

For CH₄, the emission factor for **mass of CH₄ to CO₂ mass** is **44/16 = 2.75** (molar mass ratio). But PAS 2050 allows direct use of GWP if EF is not given. Since the problem states "0.5 kg of CH₄ leaked," we treat AD as mass of CH₄ and apply GWP:

$$E_{CH_4} = 0.5\ kg\ CH_4 \times 1 \times 28$$

$$E_{CH_4} = 14\ kg\ CO_2e$$

**Step 5 — Convert HFC-134a leak to CO₂-equivalent.**

$$E_{HFC} = 0.02\ kg\ HFC\text{-}134a \times 1 \times 1300$$

$$E_{HFC} = 26\ kg\ CO_2e$$

**Step 6 — Sum total emissions for the batch.**

$$E_{total} = E_{elec} + E_{diesel} + E_{CH_4} + E_{HFC}$$

$$E_{total} = 410 + 107.2 + 14 + 26$$

$$E_{total} = 557.2\ kg\ CO_2e$$

**Step 7 — Normalize to the Functional Unit (per litre).**

$$CF_{per\ litre} = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{litres}}$$

$$CF_{per\ litre} = \frac{557.2\ kg\ CO_2e}{1000\ litres}$$

$$CF_{per\ litre} = 0.5572\ kg\ CO_2e / litre$$

### Result

$$\boxed{CF = 0.5572\ kg\ CO_2e\ per\ litre\ of\ packaged\ water}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Emission Reduction from a Manufacturing Improvement Project

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A foundry emits **12,000 kg CO₂e/month** in its baseline (2023) operations. After installing an induction furnace and waste-heat recovery, emissions drop to **8,400 kg CO₂e/month** in 2024. Calculate the **annual emission reduction** and the **percentage reduction**.

**Step 1 — Compute monthly reduction.**

$$\Delta E_{monthly} = Baseline_{emissions} - Project_{emissions}$$

$$\Delta E_{monthly} = 12000 - 8400 = 3600\ kg\ CO_2e / month$$

**Step 2 — Annualize the reduction (12 months).**

$$E_{saved} = \Delta E_{monthly} \times 12$$

$$E_{saved} = 3600 \times 12 = 43200\ kg\ CO_2e / year$$

$$E_{saved} = 43.2\ tonnes\ CO_2e / year$$

**Step 3 — Percentage reduction.**

$$\% Reduction = \frac{\Delta E_{monthly}}{Baseline_{emissions}} \times 100$$

$$\% Reduction = \frac{3600}{12000} \times 100 = 30\%$$

### Result

$$\boxed{E_{saved} = 43.2\ tonnes\ CO_2e / year,\quad \% Reduction = 30\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ISO 14001 vs PAS 2050 vs Other Standards

| Criterion | **ISO 14001** | **PAS 2050** | **ISO 14064** | **ISO 14067** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Management system | Product carbon footprint | Organization/project GHG | Product carbon footprint (ISO version) |
| **Issued by** | ISO | BSI (UK) | ISO | ISO |
| **Year (latest)** | 2015 | 2011 | 2018–2019 | 2018 |
| **Output** | EMS certification | kg CO₂e / FU | Inventory report | kg CO₂e / FU |
| **Life Cycle?** | Indirect (operational) | Yes (Cradle-to-Grave) | N/A (inventory) | Yes |
| **Use Case in Mfg.** | Factory-level EMS | Labelling of a product | Corporate carbon reporting | International product CFP |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram #1 — The PDCA Cycle Applied to ISO 14001:2015

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plan: Clause 4 - Context of Organization] --> P2[Plan: Clause 6 - Identify Aspects, Risks, Opportunities]
    P2 --> P3[Plan: Clause 6 - Set Environmental Objectives]
    P3 --> D1[Do: Clause 7 - Resources, Competence, Awareness]
    D1 --> D2[Do: Clause 7 - Communication and Documentation]
    D2 --> D3[Do: Clause 8 - Operational Controls and Emergency Prep]
    D3 --> C1[Check: Clause 9 - Monitor and Measure]
    C1 --> C2[Check: Clause 9 - Evaluate Compliance]
    C2 --> C3[Check: Clause 9 - Internal Audit]
    C3 --> A1[Act: Clause 10 - Nonconformity and Corrective Action]
    A1 --> A2[Act: Clause 10 - Continual Improvement]
    A2 --> P1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram #2 — The EMS Implementation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Management Commitment] --> B[Environmental Policy]
    B --> C[Planning: Aspects and Impacts]
    C --> D[Legal Requirements]
    D --> E[Objectives and Targets]
    E --> F[Resources and Training]
    F --> G[Operational Controls]
    G --> H[Emergency Preparedness]
    H --> I[Monitoring and Measurement]
    I --> J[Internal Audit]
    J --> K[Management Review]
    K --> L{Conformity Achieved?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Continual Improvement Loop]
    L -- No --> N[Corrective Action]
    N --> F
    M --> B
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram #3 — PAS 2050 Cradle-to-Grave Carbon Footprint Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Extraction] --> B[Material Processing]
    B --> C[Manufacturing Production]
    C --> D[Distribution and Storage]
    D --> E[Retail and Use by Consumer]
    E --> F[End of Life Disposal]
    F --> G[Recycling / Landfill / Incineration]
    A --> H[Activity Data x Emission Factor x GWP]
    B --> H
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Total kg CO2 Equivalent]
    I --> J[Divide by Functional Unit]
    J --> K[Final Carbon Footprint kg CO2e per FU]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram #4 — Hierarchy of Sustainability Standards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[ISO 14000 Family]
    root1 --> n1[ISO 14001 - EMS Standard]
    root1 --> n2[ISO 14004 - EMS Implementation Guidance]
    root1 --> n3[ISO 14040-14044 - LCA Framework]
    root1 --> n4[ISO 14064 - GHG Quantification]
    root1 --> n5[ISO 14067 - Product Carbon Footprint]
    root1 --> n6[ISO 14097 - Climate Finance]
    root1 --> n7[PAS 2050 - BSI Carbon Footprint Method]
    n1 --> o1[Annex SL High Level Structure]
    n1 --> o2[PDCA Cycle]
    n7 --> o3[6 GHGs - Kyoto Basket]
    n7 --> o4[Life Cycle Stages]
    n5 --> o5[Cradle to Grave Boundary]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and explain any three benefits of implementing ISO 14001 in a manufacturing organization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ISO 14001 is a globally accepted standard for Environmental Management Systems. Three benefits in manufacturing are:

1. **Legal Compliance and Reduced Risk (1 mark):** It helps organizations identify applicable environmental legislation and systematically evaluate compliance, thereby reducing the risk of penalties, shutdowns, or lawsuits.

2. **Cost Reduction through Resource Efficiency (1 mark):** The standard pushes companies to monitor inputs (energy, water, raw materials) and minimize waste, leading to direct cost savings — e.g., lower electricity bills, reduced material scrap.

3. **Market Access and Brand Value (1 mark):** ISO 14001 certification is a prerequisite for many global supply chains (automotive, electronics). It improves corporate image and enables access to "green" tenders and eco-conscious customers.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is PAS 2050? State its purpose and the six greenhouse gases it covers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** PAS 2050 (Publicly Available Specification 2050) is a method developed by the **British Standards Institution (BSI)** in 2008 (revised 2011) for the **assessment of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions across the life cycle of a product or service**.

**Purpose (1 mark):** To provide a consistent, transparent method to quantify the **carbon footprint** of products, expressed as **kg CO₂-equivalent per functional unit**, enabling carbon labelling, benchmarking, and reduction strategies.

**Six GHGs covered (1 mark):** CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, HFCs, PFCs, SF₆ (the **Kyoto Protocol basket**).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks. A choice is given between two questions (OR pattern). You must answer EITHER the full Q-A OR the full Q-B. Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle as applied to the ISO 14001:2015 Environmental Management System. Map each PDCA phase to the relevant ISO 14001 clauses and provide an example from a manufacturing context.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A company uses 2000 kWh of grid electricity and 100 litres of diesel per month. Electricity emission factor = 0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh; diesel emission factor = 2.68 kg CO₂e/litre. Calculate the total monthly CO₂e emissions and the annual emissions in tonnes.*

#### Model Solution

**(a) PDCA Cycle in ISO 14001:2015 [7 marks]**

The PDCA cycle is a four-stage iterative management method. ISO 14001:2015 integrates PDCA into its 10-clause structure as follows:

**PLAN (Clauses 4 & 6) [2 marks]:** The organization understands its context (Clause 4) — internal/external issues, stakeholder expectations, and the scope of the EMS. In Clause 6, it identifies **environmental aspects** (inputs/outputs that interact with the environment), **compliance obligations** (legal & voluntary), and **risks and opportunities**. It then sets measurable **environmental objectives**.

*Manufacturing example:* A paint factory identifies VOC emissions and wastewater discharge as significant aspects, and sets a target to reduce VOC by 30% in 24 months.

**DO (Clauses 7 & 8) [2 marks]:** Clause 7 ensures **resources, competence, awareness, communication, and documented information** are in place. Clause 8 implements **operational controls** — SOPs, maintenance schedules, emergency preparedness.

*Example:* The factory installs scrubbers, trains operators on solvent handling, and prepares spill-response kits.

**CHECK (Clause 9) [1.5 marks]:** The organization **monitors, measures, and analyzes** its environmental performance. It conducts **internal audits** and **evaluates compliance** with legal obligations.

*Example:* Monthly stack monitoring for VOC concentration, quarterly audits, and an annual review of consent-to-operate conditions.

**ACT (Clause 10) [1.5 marks]:** The organization addresses **nonconformities** with **corrective action** and drives **continual improvement** of the EMS, closing the loop by feeding lessons back into the Plan stage.

*Example:* VOC levels are still above target — root cause analysis shows inadequate filter maintenance, so a new SOP is issued.

---

**(b) Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Emissions from electricity [3 marks]:**

$$E_{elec} = 2000\ kWh \times 0.82\ kg\ CO_2e / kWh$$

$$E_{elec} = 1640\ kg\ CO_2e\ per\ month$$

**Step 2 — Emissions from diesel [3 marks]:**

$$E_{diesel} = 100\ litres \times 2.68\ kg\ CO_2e / litre$$

$$E_{diesel} = 268\ kg\ CO_2e\ per\ month$$

**Step 3 — Total monthly emissions [0.5 marks]:**

$$E_{total} = 1640 + 268 = 1908\ kg\ CO_2e / month$$

**Step 4 — Annual emissions in tonnes [0.5 marks]:**

$$E_{annual} = 1908 \times 12 = 22896\ kg\ CO_2e / year$$

$$E_{annual} = 22.896\ tonnes\ CO_2e / year$$

$$\boxed{E_{monthly} = 1908\ kg\ CO_2e,\quad E_{annual} \approx 22.9\ tonnes\ CO_2e}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Define an Environmental Management System (EMS). With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the key elements of an EMS as per ISO 14001:2015.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A refrigeration unit leaks 0.05 kg of HFC-134a and 0.1 kg of CH₄. Using GWP (HFC-134a = 1300, CH₄ = 28), calculate the total CO₂-equivalent emissions.*

#### Model Solution

**(a) EMS Definition and Block Diagram [7 marks]**

**Definition [2 marks]:** An **Environmental Management System (EMS)** is a structured set of policies, processes, and procedures that enables an organization to manage its environmental impacts, meet legal obligations, and continuously improve its environmental performance, in alignment with **ISO 14001:2015**.

**Key Elements (with block diagram) [5 marks]:**

A typical EMS block flow is:

```
TOP MANAGEMENT COMMITMENT
        ↓
ENVIRONMENTAL POLICY
        ↓
PLANNING (Aspects → Legal Req. → Objectives)
        ↓
IMPLEMENTATION & OPERATION (Resources, Training, Controls, Emergencies)
        ↓
CHECKING (Monitoring, Internal Audit, Compliance Evaluation)
        ↓
MANAGEMENT REVIEW
        ↓
CONTINUAL IMPROVEMENT → (loop back to POLICY)
```

Each block is documented, and the system is externally audited for certification.

---

**(b) Refrigerant Leakage Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — CO₂e from HFC-134a [3 marks]:**

$$E_{HFC} = 0.05\ kg \times 1 \times 1300$$

$$E_{HFC} = 65\ kg\ CO_2e$$

**Step 2 — CO₂e from CH₄ [3 marks]:**

$$E_{CH_4} = 0.1\ kg \times 1 \times 28$$

$$E_{CH_4} = 2.8\ kg\ CO_2e$$

**Step 3 — Total CO₂-equivalent [1 mark]:**

$$E_{total} = 65 + 2.8 = 67.8\ kg\ CO_2e$$

$$\boxed{E_{total} = 67.8\ kg\ CO_2e}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**

1. **Do not confuse ISO 14001 with ISO 14000.** ISO 14000 is the *family*; ISO 14001 is the *EMS-specific standard*. Examiners dock **½ to 1 mark** for this.

2. **PDCA Mapping Mistakes:** Students often map *only* Clause 8 to "Do" and miss Clauses 4 & 6 in "Plan." The 2015 revision **explicitly moves** context-of-organization and planning to Plan.

3. **Forgetting to multiply by GWP:** In PAS 2050 / carbon footprint problems, the most common error is reporting **kg of gas** instead of **kg CO₂e**. Always convert via GWP.

4. **No Unit Declaration:** Always write the unit (kg CO₂e, tCO₂e/yr). KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for missing units in numerical answers.

5. **Functional Unit Neglect:** PAS 2050 questions always require a **functional unit**. Reporting total emissions without normalizing loses the 1 mark assigned to the FU step.

6. **HFC GWP Values:** Memorize at least HFC-134a = **1300** and SF₆ = **23,500**. The CH₄ GWP used is **28** (IPCC AR5) — not 21 or 25 (older values).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **ISO 14001:2015** is the EMS standard; follows the **High-Level Structure (Annex SL)** and **PDCA cycle**.
- The 10 clauses are grouped as **Plan (4, 6), Do (7, 8), Check (9), Act (10)**.
- **EMS** is the *system*; ISO 14001 is the *standard* that prescribes it.
- Benefits of EMS: **legal compliance, cost reduction, market access, risk mitigation, brand value**.
- **PAS 2050** is a **carbon footprint assessment** method by BSI — not a management standard.
- PAS 2050 covers **6 Kyoto GHGs** and expresses emissions in **kg CO₂e per functional unit**.
- **Master Equation:** $CO_{2e} = AD \times EF \times GWP$ — memorize it verbatim.
- **Key GWP Values:** CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265, HFC-134a = 1300, SF₆ = 23,500.
- **Life cycle stages in PAS 2050:** Pre-production → Production → Distribution → Use → End-of-life.
- **ISO 14064** is for **organization-level** GHG inventories (Scopes 1, 2, 3).
- **ISO 14067** is the ISO equivalent of PAS 2050 for **product carbon footprint**.
- Always **declare units, the functional unit, and the system boundary** in carbon-footprint answers.
- Real-world link: ISO 14001 + PAS 2050 = factory EMS + product carbon label = market competitiveness in green supply chains (CBAM, EU, automotive OEMs).
- One-liner exam hooks:
  - "ISO 14001 is a *framework*, EMS is the *factory's working system*."
  - "PAS 2050 quantifies the carbon; ISO 14001 manages the environment."
  - "PDCA = Plan (4,6) – Do (7,8) – Check (9) – Act (10)."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Impact Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Environmental Impact Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Impact Parameters (EIPs)** are the quantified, measurable indicators used to characterize the magnitude and nature of an environmental burden imposed by a manufacturing process, product, or system across its life cycle. They translate raw physical releases and resource extractions into standardized units suitable for benchmarking, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), and Environmental Management Systems (EMS) such as **ISO 14001**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, EIPs are broadly classified into:

1. **Input Parameters** – Resource consumption (energy, water, raw materials, land).
2. **Output Parameters** – Emissions to air, water, and soil (waste streams).
3. **Impact Category Parameters** – Aggregated mid-point or end-point indicators such as Global Warming Potential (GWP), Acidification Potential (AP), Eutrophication Potential (EP), Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP), and Human Toxicity Potential (HTP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OESFT612, Module 2):** Students must be able to identify, define, and apply the standard EIP metrics listed under ISO 14040 / 14044 LCA framework, and correlate them with manufacturing process selection decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a factory is a **human body** undergoing an annual health check-up. The doctor doesn't just check one symptom — they measure blood pressure (energy efficiency), cholesterol (toxic emissions), weight gain/loss (waste accumulation), and kidney function (water discharge). Each metric tells the doctor where the body is stressed.

- **Energy consumed** = the body's daily calorie intake.
- **CO₂ emitted** = the carbon dioxide exhaled.
- **Hazardous waste generated** = toxins the body cannot process.
- **Water discharged** = urine and sweat.

Just as a doctor cannot diagnose a patient from a single number, a sustainability engineer cannot judge a manufacturing process from one metric. Environmental Impact Parameters form the **complete diagnostic dashboard** that tells us whether a process is *sustainable* or *pathological*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Reference Unit:** Most air-borne emissions are normalized to **kg CO₂-equivalent (kg CO₂e)** using IPCC Global Warming Potential (GWP-100) factors. **GWP = 1** is assigned to CO₂ itself.

| Parameter Category | Typical Unit | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Consumption | **MJ** or **kWh** | Direct cost + carbon footprint driver |
| Material Use | **kg** | Resource depletion |
| Water Consumption | **m³** or **L** | Scarcity stress |
| CO₂ Emissions | **kg CO₂e** | Climate change |
| Particulate Matter (PM) | **kg PM2.5e** | Respiratory health |
| Solid Waste | **kg** | Landfill pressure |
| Eco-indicator 99 | **Points (Pt)** | Aggregated single score |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Classification

#### A. Input-Based Parameters (Resource Footprints)
