1. **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED)** — total primary energy extracted to deliver one functional unit of product.
2. **Material Intensity (MI)** — material mass per unit of useful output.
3. **Water Footprint (WF)** — blue, green, and grey water components.
4. **Land Use (LU)** — m²·year occupied or transformed.

#### B. Output-Based Parameters (Emissions & Waste)

1. **Carbon Footprint (CF)** — sum of all greenhouse gases in CO₂e.
2. **Acidification Potential (AP)** — emissions of SO₂, NOₓ, NH₃ expressed in **kg SO₂e**.
3. **Eutrophication Potential (EP)** — nutrient releases (PO₄³⁻, NO₃⁻) expressed in **kg PO₄³⁻e**.
4. **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** — halogenated substances in **kg CFC-11e**.
5. **Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential (POCP)** — VOCs in **kg C₂H₄e**.

#### C. End-Point (Damage-Oriented) Parameters

1. **Human Health (HH)** — DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Years).
2. **Ecosystem Quality (EQ)** — species·year lost.
3. **Resources (R)** — MJ surplus energy to extract future minerals.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All boundary conditions assume **ISO 14040 cradle-to-gate** unless stated otherwise.

| Sl. | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Global Warming Potential (GWP) | $GWP = \sum_{i} m_i \cdot GWP_i$ | $m_i$ = mass of GHG *i*; $GWP_i$ = 100-yr potential | **kg CO₂e** |
| 2 | Carbon Footprint (CF) | $CF = \sum_{j} AD_j \cdot EF_j$ | $AD_j$ = activity data; $EF_j$ = emission factor | **kg CO₂e** |
| 3 | Energy Intensity (EI) | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{N_{output}}$ | $E_{total}$ = energy input; $N_{output}$ = parts produced | **MJ/unit** |
| 4 | Material Efficiency (η_m) | $\eta_m = \dfrac{M_{useful}}{M_{input}} \times 100$ | useful vs. total material mass | **%** |
| 5 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{E}{m_{product}}$ | energy per kg product | **MJ/kg** |
| 6 | Environmental Factor (E-factor) | $E = \dfrac{W_{total}}{M_{product}}$ | $W_{total}$ = all waste except water | **kg waste/kg product** |
| 7 | Water Footprint (WF) | $WF = W_{blue} + W_{green} + W_{grey}$ | blue (surface/ground), green (rain), grey (dilution) | **m³** |
| 8 | Acidification Potential (AP) | $AP = \sum_i m_i \cdot AP_i$ | $AP_i$ for SO₂, NOₓ, NH₃ | **kg SO₂e** |
| 9 | Eutrophication Potential (EP) | $EP = \sum_i m_i \cdot EP_i$ | $EP_i$ for phosphate, nitrate | **kg PO₄³⁻e** |
| 10 | Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) | $CED = E_{renew} + E_{nren} + E_{nuclear}$ | renewable + non-renewable + nuclear | **MJ-eq** |
| 11 | Process Mass Intensity (PMI) | $PMI = \dfrac{M_{total \ in}}{M_{product}}$ | total input mass / product mass | **dimensionless** |
| 12 | Atom Economy (AE) | $AE = \dfrac{MW_{desired}}{MW_{all \ reactants}} \times 100$ | molecular weight basis | **%** |

> **Real-world utility:** In discrete manufacturing (e.g., CNC machining), the **E-factor** is the most practical single metric for shop-floor decisions. In the chemical industry, **PMI** and **Atom Economy** dominate because raw material losses dominate the cost structure.

### 2.3 Operational Logic: Why EIPs Matter in Manufacturing

1. **Benchmarking** — Comparing two competing processes (e.g., machining vs. additive manufacturing) on the same functional unit.
2. **Hot-Spot Identification** — Pinpointing the life-cycle stage contributing the largest share of impact (e.g., material extraction in automotive).
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Meeting limits set under **ISO 14001**, **EU CBAM**, and Indian **PAT Scheme**.
4. **Eco-design Feedback** — Driving redesign toward lower-impact alternatives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Carbon Footprint from Activity Data

**Problem Setup:** A CNC machining cell consumes **2,500 kWh** of grid electricity and **800 kg** of LPG per month. Calculate the monthly Carbon Footprint in **kg CO₂e**.

**Given Standard Emission Factors (India CEA averages):**
- Grid electricity: **0.82 kg CO₂e / kWh**
- LPG combustion: **2.98 kg CO₂e / kg**

#### Step 1 — Identify the contributing streams

The Carbon Footprint is the linear superposition of all greenhouse gas releases multiplied by their GWP-100 factors.

$$
CF = \sum_{j=1}^{n} AD_j \cdot EF_j \cdot GWP_j
$$

**Conversion logic:** Each activity data point (kWh of electricity, kg of fuel) is multiplied by its specific emission factor to obtain the **kg CO₂e** contribution of that stream alone.

#### Step 2 — Substitute the electricity contribution

Let $j = 1$ denote grid electricity.

$$
CF_1 = AD_1 \cdot EF_1 \cdot GWP_1
$$

$$
CF_1 = 2500 \ \text{kWh} \times 0.82 \ \dfrac{\text{kg CO}_2\text{e}}{\text{kWh}} \times 1
$$

$$
CF_1 = 2050 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute the LPG contribution

Let $j = 2$ denote LPG combustion. CO₂ already has $GWP = 1$.

$$
CF_2 = AD_2 \cdot EF_2 \cdot GWP_{CO_2}
$$

$$
CF_2 = 800 \ \text{kg} \times 2.98 \ \dfrac{\text{kg CO}_2\text{e}}{\text{kg}} \times 1
$$

$$
CF_2 = 2384 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

#### Step 4 — Aggregate by superposition

$$
CF_{total} = CF_1 + CF_2
$$

$$
CF_{total} = 2050 + 2384 = 4434 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Of the 4,434 kg CO₂e, **46.2%** originates from electricity and **53.8%** from fuel combustion. Mitigation should prioritize fuel-substitution (e.g., electric furnace) or solar PV for the grid load.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Energy Intensity and SEC

**Problem Setup:** A forging press consumes **18,000 MJ** of energy and produces **2,400 components** of average mass **0.5 kg** each in one shift. Compute:
- (a) Energy Intensity per part.
- (b) Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) per kg of product.

#### Step (a) — Energy Intensity per unit

By definition:

$$
EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{N_{output}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Total energy consumed by the machine is divided by the number of good parts produced during that same accounting period.

$$
EI = \dfrac{18000 \ \text{MJ}}{2400 \ \text{parts}}
$$

$$
EI = 7.5 \ \text{MJ/part}
$$

#### Step (b) — Specific Energy Consumption per kg

Total product mass:

$$
m_{product} = N_{output} \cdot m_{part}
$$

$$
m_{product} = 2400 \times 0.5 = 1200 \ \text{kg}
$$

Then:

$$
SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{m_{product}}
$$

$$
SEC = \dfrac{18000 \ \text{MJ}}{1200 \ \text{kg}}
$$

$$
SEC = 15 \ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Environmental Factor (E-Factor)

**Problem Setup:** A batch process yields **500 kg** of desired product. Side streams include **40 kg** solvent waste, **15 kg** aqueous effluent, and **5 kg** heavy-metal sludge. Determine the E-factor (excluding water from effluent by convention).

By definition:

$$
E = \dfrac{W_{total}}{M_{product}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** E-factor is intentionally defined excluding water because water dominates the mass balance and obscures meaningful waste-reduction signals.

$$
W_{total} = W_{solvent} + W_{sludge}
$$

> **Note:** Aqueous effluent is reported separately as **Water Footprint**, not in E.

$$
W_{total} = 40 + 5 = 45 \ \text{kg}
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{45}{500} = 0.09
$$

**Industry benchmark comparison:**

| Industry Segment | Typical E-Factor |
|---|---|
| Oil refining | < 0.1 |
| Bulk chemicals | 1 – 5 |
| Fine chemicals | 25 – 100 |
| Pharmaceuticals | 25 – 200 |

**Interpretation:** An E-factor of **0.09** places this process in the *oil refining / bulk* regime — highly mass-efficient. Pharmaceutical plants producing similar tonnage would have E factors two to three orders of magnitude higher.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mid-Point vs End-Point Parameters

| Dimension | Mid-Point (Problem-Oriented) | End-Point (Damage-Oriented) |
|---|---|---|
| Aggregation | Low (single issue) | High (3 damage categories) |
| Uncertainty | Lower | Higher (modelling chains) |
| Example Metric | GWP, AP, EP | DALY, species·yr, MJ-surplus |
| Typical Method | CML 2001, TRACI | Eco-indicator 99, ReCiPe |
| Use Case | Hot-spot analysis | Single-score communication |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EIP Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturing System Boundary] --> B[Input Flows]
    A --> C[Internal Process]
    A --> D[Output Flows]

    B --> B1[Energy: MJ or kWh]
    B --> B2[Raw Material: kg]
    B --> B3[Water: m3]
    B --> B4[Land Use: m2 yr]

    C --> C1[Casting]
    C --> C2[Machining]
    C --> C3[Forming]
    C --> C4[Joining]

    D --> D1[Air Emissions]
    D --> D2[Water Discharges]
    D --> D3[Solid Waste]

    D1 --> D1a[GWP: kg CO2e]
    D1 --> D1b[AP: kg SO2e]
    D1 --> D1c[POCP: kg C2H4e]

    D2 --> D2a[EP: kg PO4 e]
    D2 --> D2b[HTP: kg DCB e]

    D3 --> D3a[Hazardous Waste: kg]
    D3 --> D3b[Non Hazardous: kg]

    B1 --> E[Mid Point Impact Categories]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    D1a --> E
    D1b --> E
    D1c --> E
    D2a --> E
    D2b --> E
    D3a --> E
    D3b --> E

    E --> F[End Point Damage Assessment]
    F --> F1[Human Health: DALY]
    F --> F2[Ecosystem Quality: species yr]
    F --> F3[Resources: MJ surplus]

    F1 --> G[Single Score Indicator]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Lifecycle Stage Contribution to EIPs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Raw Material Extraction] -->|High impact| M1[Material Footprint]
    L2[Manufacturing] -->|Medium impact| M2[Energy Footprint]
    L3[Distribution] -->|Low impact| M3[Transport Emissions]
    L4[Use Phase] -->|High impact| M4[Operational Energy]
    L5[End of Life] -->|Medium impact| M5[Waste Burden]

    M1 --> N[Total Environmental Burden]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    M4 --> N
    M5 --> N
```

### 4.3 EIP Decision Tree — Selection of Right Indicator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Process Type?} --> Q2[Chemical Process]
    Q1 --> Q3[Mechanical Process]
    Q1 --> Q4[Electronics Process]

    Q2 --> R1[Use PMI or Atom Economy]
    Q3 --> R2[Use E factor or SEC]
    Q4 --> R3[Use CED and HTP]

    R1 --> S[Compare kg waste per kg product]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S

    S --> T[Benchmark vs Industry Average]
    T --> U[Identify Hot Spot for Eco Redesign]
```

> **Diagram interpretation note:** The flow diagrams above replace physical schematics (such as factory layouts) with a **functional block architecture** that captures interactions between EIP categories. This is the recommended fallback representation when physical drawings are not feasible.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define Environmental Impact Parameters. List any four input-based and four output-based parameters used in manufacturing sustainability assessment.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

Environmental Impact Parameters (EIPs) are quantitative indicators that describe the environmental burden imposed by a manufacturing process across its life cycle, expressed in standardized units for LCA and EMS applications.

**Input-based parameters:**
1. Cumulative Energy Demand (MJ)
2. Raw Material Consumption (kg)
3. Water Footprint (m³)
4. Land Use (m²·yr)

**Output-based parameters:**
1. Carbon Footprint (kg CO₂e)
2. Acidification Potential (kg SO₂e)
3. Eutrophication Potential (kg PO₄³⁻e)
4. Solid Hazardous Waste (kg)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q2. What is meant by Global Warming Potential (GWP)? How is the GWP of a mixture of gases calculated?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** GWP is the cumulative radiative forcing effect of a greenhouse gas over a chosen time horizon (commonly 100 years), expressed relative to the forcing of 1 kg of CO₂.

**Calculation:** For a mixture of *n* gases,

$$
GWP_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot GWP_i
$$

where $m_i$ is the mass of the *i*-th GHG released and $GWP_i$ is its published GWP-100 factor. The reference gas (CO₂) has $GWP = 1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2, Q-Style]**

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**(a)** Explain the classification of Environmental Impact Parameters into mid-point and end-point categories with suitable examples. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A grinding operation uses **3,200 kWh** of electricity and **150 kg** of cutting fluid (containing 8% by mass mineral oil that is sent to incineration). Calculate the total Carbon Footprint in **kg CO₂e** using the data: electricity emission factor = **0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh**; mineral oil incineration = **3.10 kg CO₂e/kg**. Comment on the dominant source. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution for (a):**

Mid-point (problem-oriented) parameters characterize a single environmental problem in its own units. Examples:
- GWP (kg CO₂e)
- AP (kg SO₂e)
- EP (kg PO₄³⁻e)
- HTP (kg 1,4-DCB e)

End-point (damage-oriented) parameters aggregate multiple mid-points into three damage categories:
1. Human Health (DALY)
2. Ecosystem Quality (species·yr)
3. Resources (MJ surplus)

**Advantages of Mid-point:** Lower modelling uncertainty; transparency of single-issue comparison; suitable for industrial benchmarking.

**Limitations of Mid-point:** Difficult to compare across categories (e.g., GWP vs HTP have no common unit).

**Advantages of End-point:** Single-score communication; aids policy decisions; useful for eco-labelling.

**Limitations of End-point:** Higher uncertainty due to longer modelling chains; value choices embedded in weighting.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Electricity contribution.**

$$
CF_{elec} = 3200 \ \text{kWh} \times 0.82 \ \dfrac{\text{kg CO}_2\text{e}}{\text{kWh}}
$$

$$
CF_{elec} = 2624 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 2 — Mineral oil mass in the fluid.**

$$
m_{oil} = 150 \times 0.08 = 12 \ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Incineration contribution.**

$$
CF_{oil} = 12 \ \text{kg} \times 3.10 \ \dfrac{\text{kg CO}_2\text{e}}{\text{kg}}
$$

$$
CF_{oil} = 37.2 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate.**

$$
CF_{total} = 2624 + 37.2 = 2661.2 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Comment:** Electricity dominates at **98.6%** of total emissions; mitigation should target the electricity source (renewable procurement, VFD drives, machine retrofits) rather than fluid reformulation.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**(a)** Define E-factor, Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), and Process Mass Intensity (PMI). For a pharmaceutical batch producing **200 kg** of API from **2,400 kg** total input (including water and solvents), determine the E-factor and PMI. Solvent waste = **600 kg**, aqueous waste = **1,100 kg**, other solid waste = **80 kg**. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Discuss the role of ISO 14040/14044 framework in quantifying Environmental Impact Parameters. How does it support sustainable manufacturing decisions? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definitions:**
- **E-factor:** Mass ratio of all waste (excluding water) to product mass; quantifies waste intensity.
- **SEC:** Total energy consumed per unit mass of product (MJ/kg).
- **PMI:** Total mass of all input materials (including reagents, solvents, water) divided by mass of product.

**Step 1 — E-factor calculation.**

Excluding water per convention:

$$
W_{total} = W_{solvent} + W_{other} = 600 + 80 = 680 \ \text{kg}
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{680}{200} = 3.4
$$

This lies in the **bulk chemicals** range (typical pharma E-factor is 25–200), so this particular process is unusually efficient — possibly a continuous-flow API synthesis.

**Step 2 — PMI calculation.**

$$
PMI = \dfrac{M_{total \ in}}{M_{product}} = \dfrac{2400}{200} = 12
$$

A PMI of 12 means **12 kg of input is needed for every 1 kg of product** — a strong indicator of significant solvent recovery opportunity.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**ISO 14040 / 14044 — LCA Framework:**

The ISO 14040 and 14044 standards define a four-phase Life Cycle Assessment methodology:

1. **Goal & Scope Definition** — Functional unit, system boundary, allocation rules.
2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** — Quantification of inputs (energy, material) and outputs (emissions, waste).
3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** — Translation of LCI into EIP categories (mid-point and end-point).
4. **Interpretation** — Hot-spot identification, sensitivity analysis, recommendations.

**Role in Sustainable Manufacturing:**

- Provides a **standardized, comparable methodology** for any process or product.
- Enables **cradle-to-grave** analysis, avoiding burden-shifting between life-cycle stages.
- Supports **eco-design** by identifying dominant contributors to GWP, AP, EP, etc.
- Underpins **EPD (Environmental Product Declarations)** and **CBAM** compliance.
- Facilitates **benchmarking** between competing manufacturing routes (e.g., forging vs. additive).
- Integrates with **ISO 14001 EMS** for continuous improvement and **PDCA cycles**.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit errors** — Mixing kWh and MJ without converting (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ). Always state the unit clearly in the final answer.
> 2. **Including water in E-factor** — E-factor convention excludes water. Adding 1,100 kg of aqueous waste here would inflate the answer to **8.9**, which is wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting GWP normalization** — When asked about methane, students often write kg of CH₄ instead of **kg CO₂e = kg CH₄ × 28**.
> 4. **Skipping aggregation commentary** — Computing a number without identifying the dominant source forfeits the interpretation marks.
> 5. **Confusing mid-point and end-point** — They are NOT interchangeable; using "DALY" when asked for GWP is a clear fail.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **EIP** = quantitative environmental burden indicator; categorized as **Input, Output, Impact Category**.
- **GWP** is the universal currency for climate change; CO₂ has **GWP = 1**; CH₄ = **28**; N₂O = **265** (AR5 values).
- **CF = Σ AD × EF × GWP** — superposition of activity-data contributions.
- **EI = E_total / N_output** — energy per part; **SEC = E_total / m_product** — energy per kg.
- **E-factor excludes water**; **PMI includes water**; **Atom Economy** is molecular-weight based.
- **Mid-point** = single-issue (GWP, AP, EP); **End-point** = damage categories (HH, EQ, R).
- **Water Footprint** = blue + green + grey components.
- **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED)** = E_renew + E_non-renew + E_nuclear.
- **ISO 14040/14044** is the LCA backbone with four mandatory phases.
- **Functional Unit** must be defined before any EIP calculation; otherwise comparison is invalid.
- **Allocation** for co-products follows ISO hierarchy: system expansion → physical → economic.
- Industry E-factor ranking: oil refining < bulk chemicals < fine chemicals < pharmaceuticals.
- **Eco-indicator 99** aggregates end-points into a single score expressed in **Points (Pt)**.
- **CBAM** (EU) uses embedded emissions = direct + indirect (electricity) emissions per kg product.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainability Assessment - Concept Models and Various Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sustainability Assessment - Concept Models and Various Approaches # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Sustainability Assessment (SA)** is a systematic, structured, and holistic evaluation process that measures, analyzes, and communicates the environmental, social, and economic performance of a product, process, system, or organization across its entire life cycle, with the objective of supporting decision-making toward sustainable development. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: OESFT612 – Sustainable Manufacturing)**, Sustainability Assessment is positioned as the *quantitative and qualitative backbone* of sustainable manufacturing — converting the abstract "triple bottom line" philosophy into measurable engineering metrics and industrial decisions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)** > Sustainability Assessment is examined under two complementary lenses: > 1. **Concept Models** — the *theoretical frameworks* that define what sustainability means and how it is structured (e.g., Triple Bottom Line, DPSIR, Brundtland). > 2. **Various Approaches** — the *practical methodologies* that operationalize these models in manufacturing (e.g., LCA, MFA, EMA, EIA, Carbon Footprinting). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of **Sustainability Assessment** like a **medical health check-up for a manufacturing company or product**. | Medical Analogy | Sustainability Assessment Equivalent | |---|---| | Doctor's full body check-up | Comprehensive sustainability audit | | Blood test, X-ray, ECG | LCA, Carbon Footprint, Energy Analysis | | BMI, cholesterol levels | Sustainability indicators & KPIs | | Diet & lifestyle recommendations | Improvement strategies, cleaner production | | Patient's medical history | Product Life Cycle (cradle-to-grave) | Just as a doctor doesn't rely on a single test to declare you healthy, **sustainability cannot be assessed by one metric alone**. It needs a *combination of concept models (the diagnostic philosophy)* and *various approaches (the diagnostic tools)*. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction to Remember:** > - **Concept Models** = *What* we measure and *why* (philosophical/structural frameworks). > - **Approaches** = *How* we measure it (practical tools and methodologies). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **LCA** | Life Cycle Assessment (ISO 14040/14044) | | **CFP** | Carbon Footprint (measured in **kg CO₂-eq**) | | **EF** | Ecological Footprint (measured in **global hectares, gha**) | | **MFA** | Material Flow Analysis (measured in **tonnes/year**) | | **EMA** | Energy/Material Analysis (measured in **MJ** or **kg**) | | **DPSIR** | Drivers-Pressures-State-Impact-Response framework | | **TBL** | Triple Bottom Line (People, Planet, Profit) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Concept Models of Sustainability Assessment Concept models are **theoretical scaffolding structures** that define the boundaries, dimensions, and interactions of sustainability. They answer the question: *"How should we think about sustainability?"* ### 2.1.1 The Brundtland Model (Three-Pillar Model) The most foundational concept model, derived from the **Brundtland Commission Report (1987)**: - **Sustainable Development** = Development that meets the needs of the *present* without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. - Three interconnected pillars: 1. **Environmental Sustainability** — Ecosystem integrity, biodiversity, resource conservation. 2. **Social Sustainability** — Equity, health, community welfare. 3. **Economic Sustainability** — Profitability, value creation, long-term viability. ### 2.1.2 Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — Elkington's Model Coined by **John Elkington (1994)**, the TBL model expands traditional accounting to include three bottom lines: - **People (Social Equity)** - **Planet (Environmental Quality)** - **Profit (Economic Viability)** > [!NOTE] > The TBL is the *most frequently examined concept model* in KTU exams. Memorize the three "P"s: **People, Planet, Profit**. ### 2.1.3 DPSIR Framework (Drivers–Pressures–State–Impact–Response) A causal concept model developed by the **European Environment Agency (EEA)** to analyze cause-effect relationships in sustainability: | Component | Meaning | Manufacturing Example | |---|---|---| | **D** – Drivers | Socio-economic forces driving activities | Market demand for electric vehicles | | **P** – Pressures | Resulting environmental pressures | Mining of lithium and cobalt | | **S** – State | Resulting change in environmental state | Local water/soil contamination | | **I** – Impact | Consequences for ecosystems/people | Health issues in mining communities | | **R** – Response | Policy/industry action taken | Recycling mandates, ESG reporting | ### 2.1.4 The Five-Capital Model (Forum for the Future) Sustainability is built on five forms of capital that must all be maintained or enhanced: 1. **Natural Capital** — ecosystems, land, water, minerals. 2. **Human Capital** — knowledge, skills, health. 3. **Social Capital** — networks, trust, institutions. 4. **Manufactured Capital** — infrastructure, tools, technology. 5. **Financial Capital** — monetary assets. ### 2.1.5 Planetary Boundaries Model (Rockström, 2009) Identifies **9 planetary boundaries** within which humanity must operate: - Climate change, biodiversity loss, biogeochemical flows (N & P), land-system change, freshwater use, novel entities, ozone depletion, ocean acidification, atmospheric aerosol loading. ## 2.2 Various Approaches to Sustainability Assessment These are the *operational methodologies* used in industry and academia. ### 2.2.1 Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) — ISO 14040 / 14044 The **gold-standard approach** for product/process sustainability evaluation. Four mandatory phases: 1. **Goal & Scope Definition** — Define system boundaries, functional unit. 2. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** — Quantify inputs/outputs (energy, materials, emissions). 3. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** — Translate inventory data into impact categories. 4. **Interpretation** — Identify hotspots, draw conclusions, recommendations. ### 2.2.2 Material Flow Analysis (MFA) Tracks the *flow and stock* of materials through a defined system (factory, region, nation). ### 2.2.3 Energy Analysis / Emergy Analysis Quantifies total energy inputs (direct + indirect) required for a product/process. ### 2.2.4 Carbon Footprinting Measures total **Greenhouse Gas (GHG)** emissions in **CO₂-equivalent units**. ### 2.2.5 Ecological Footprinting Expresses human demand on nature in **global hectares (gha)** of biologically productive land. ### 2.2.6 Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) A regulatory tool to evaluate environmental consequences *before* project approval. ### 2.2.7 Sustainability Indicators & Index Systems Composite indicators like **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)**, **GRI (Global Reporting Initiative)** metrics. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Carbon Footprint | $CF = \sum_{i} (m_i \cdot GWP_i)$ | **kg CO₂-eq** | GHG accounting | | Ecological Footprint | $EF = \dfrac{A_{consumption}}{A_{productivity}}$ | **gha** | Resource demand vs biocapacity | | Energy Intensity | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | **MJ/unit** | Manufacturing efficiency | | Material Efficiency | $ME = \dfrac{M_{product}}{M_{input}} \times 100$ | **%** | Material utilization | | Global Warming Potential | $GWP = \dfrac{IR_{gas}}{IR_{CO_2}}$ | dimensionless | Comparative GHG impact | | Recyclability Rate | $R_r = \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** | Circular economy metric | | E-factor (Waste) | $E = \dfrac{M_{waste}}{M_{product}}$ | **kg waste/kg product** | Green chemistry metric | | Cumulative Energy Demand | $CED = \sum_{j} E_{input,j}$ | **MJ-eq** | Embodied energy | | Functional Unit (LCA) | $FU$ (e.g., 1 kWh, 1 tonne-km) | Defined per study | LCA normalization | | Impact Category Indicator | $IC = \sum_{s} CF_s \cdot m_s$ | **kg eq per FU** | LCIA characterization | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Rule:** Never confuse *Concept Models* (the "why/structure") with *Approaches* (the "how/tools"). Examiners frequently test this distinction. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Carbon Footprint Calculation (ISO 14067 Style) **Problem Statement:** A small manufacturing unit emits the following gases annually: - **CH₄**: 50 kg - **N₂O**: 10 kg - **CO₂**: 5000 kg Compute the total **Carbon Footprint (CO₂-equivalent)** using GWP₁₀₀ values from IPCC. ### Step 1: Recall the GWP₁₀₀ values (IPCC AR5 standard) - $GWP_{CO_2} = 1$ - $GWP_{CH_4} = 28$ - $GWP_{N_2O} = 265$ ### Step 2: Apply the carbon footprint summation formula $$CF = \sum_{i} \left( m_i \times GWP_i \right)$$ Where $m_i$ is the mass of gas $i$ emitted (kg) and $GWP_i$ is its 100-year Global Warming Potential. ### Step 3: Substitute values for each gas component For **CO₂**: $$CF_{CO_2} = 5000 \times 1 = 5000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$ For **CH₄**: $$CF_{CH_4} = 50 \times 28 = 1400 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$ For **N₂O**: $$CF_{N_2O} = 10 \times 265 = 2650 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$ ### Step 4: Sum all contributions $$CF_{total} = 5000 + 1400 + 2650$$ $$\boxed{CF_{total} = 9050 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}}$$ ### Step 5: Interpretation The manufacturing unit's annual carbon footprint is **9050 kg CO₂-equivalent**. CH₄ contributes ~15.5%, N₂O contributes ~29.3%, and direct CO₂ contributes ~55.2%. **N₂O, despite small mass, dominates emissions** — highlighting why *emission-by-mass* analysis can be misleading without GWP weighting. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Four Phases of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) ### Phase 1: Goal & Scope Definition - Define **Functional Unit (FU)**: e.g., "1 tonne of steel produced." - Define **System Boundaries**: cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave, or cradle-to-cradle. - Define **Impact Categories**: GWP, acidification, eutrophication, etc. ### Phase 2: Life Cycle Inventory (LCI) - Collect data on all inputs (raw materials, energy) and outputs (emissions, waste) for each life cycle stage. - Example: For 1 FU of steel: - Iron ore: 1500 kg - Coal (coke): 800 kg - Electricity: 2000 MJ - CO₂ emitted: 2000 kg ### Phase 3: Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) - Apply characterization factors to translate inventory into impact categories. - Example formula for Global Warming Potential (GWP): $$GWP_{total} = \sum_{s} \left( m_s \times CF_s \right)$$ Where $m_s$ is the mass of substance $s$ and $CF_s$ is its characterization factor (kg CO₂-eq/kg). ### Phase 4: Interpretation - Identify **hotspots** (life cycle stages with highest impact). - Conduct **sensitivity analysis** to check robustness. - Draw conclusions and recommendations (e.g., shift to renewable electricity). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Concept Models vs. Approaches | Aspect | Concept Models | Various Approaches | |---|---|---| | **Nature** | Theoretical / Philosophical | Operational / Practical | | **Question Answered** | What is sustainability? How is it structured? | How do we measure/implement it? | | **Output Type** | Framework / Diagram | Quantitative Data / Report | | **Examples** | TBL, DPSIR, Planetary Boundaries | LCA, MFA, Carbon Footprint | | **Origin** | Academia, Policy Think Tanks | Industry, Standards Bodies (ISO) | | **Time Horizon** | Long-term (decades/centuries) | Short to mid-term (product/process life) | | **KTU Exam Weightage** | High (definitions & diagrams) | Very High (numericals & comparisons) | | **User** | Strategists, Policy Makers | Engineers, Designers, Auditors | --- ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Material Flow Analysis (MFA) for a Manufacturing Plant **Given:** A plant processes 1000 tonnes of aluminium input annually. Output: 850 tonnes finished product, 100 tonnes recyclable scrap, 50 tonnes landfill waste. ### Step 1: Apply mass balance $$M_{input} = M_{product} + M_{recyclable} + M_{waste}$$ $$1000 = 850 + 100 + 50 \rightarrow \text{Balance verified: } 1000 = 1000 \text{ ✓}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Material Efficiency (ME) $$ME = \frac{M_{product}}{M_{input}} \times 100$$ $$ME = \frac{850}{1000} \times 100 = 85\%$$ ### Step 3: Calculate Recyclability Rate (R_r) $$R_r = \frac{M_{recyclable}}{M_{total \, output}} \times 100$$ $$R_r = \frac{100}{950} \times 100 \approx 10.53\%$$ ### Step 4: Calculate E-factor (Environmental factor) $$E = \frac{M_{waste}}{M_{product}}$$ $$E = \frac{50}{850} \approx 0.0588 \text{ kg waste/kg product}$$ ### Step 5: Interpretation Material efficiency is **85%**, indicating good utilization. However, recyclability rate (~10.5%) is low — the factory should invest in **closed-loop scrap recovery** to improve circularity. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Models Overview (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Concept Models of Sustainability] --> B[Brundtland Model
Three Pillars] A --> C[Triple Bottom Line
TBL - Elkington] A --> D[DPSIR Framework
EEA Causal Model] A --> E[Five Capital Model
Forum for the Future] A --> F[Planetary Boundaries
Rockstrom 2009] B --> B1[Environmental Pillar] B --> B2[Social Pillar] B --> B3[Economic Pillar] C --> C1[People] C --> C2[Planet] C --> C3[Profit] D --> D1[Drivers] D --> D2[Pressures] D --> D3[State] D --> D4[Impact] D --> D5[Response] E --> E1[Natural Capital] E --> E2[Human Capital] E --> E3[Social Capital] E --> E4[Manufactured Capital] E --> E5[Financial Capital] ``` ## 4.2 Various Approaches — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph LR P[Sustainability Assessment Approaches] --> Q[Product Level] P --> R[Process Level] P --> S[System or Policy Level] Q --> Q1[LCA
ISO 14040 14044] Q --> Q2[Carbon Footprint
ISO 14067] Q --> Q3[Ecological Footprint] R --> R1[Material Flow Analysis MFA] R --> R2[Energy Analysis EMA] R --> R3[E factor Analysis] S --> S1[Environmental Impact Assessment EIA] S --> S2[Strategic Environmental Assessment SEA] S --> S3[ESG and GRI Reporting] Q1 --> T[Output: kg CO2 eq, MJ, kg eq per FU] Q2 --> T Q3 --> U[Output: global hectares gha] R1 --> V[Output: tonnes per year] R2 --> V S1 --> W[Output: EIA Report] S3 --> X[Output: Sustainability Report] ``` ## 4.3 LCA Four-Phase Framework (ISO 14040) ```mermaid graph TD A[Phase 1: Goal and Scope Definition] --> B[Phase 2: Life Cycle Inventory LCI] B --> C[Phase 3: Life Cycle Impact Assessment LCIA] C --> D[Phase 4: Interpretation] D --> E{Hotspots Identified?} E -->|Yes| F[Improvement Strategies] E -->|No| G[Document and Conclude] F --> A ``` ## 4.4 DPSIR Causal Chain Flow (Sustainability Indicator Logic) ```mermaid graph LR drv[Drivers
Market Demand] --> prs[Pressures
Resource Extraction] prs --> sta[State
Environmental Degradation] sta --> imp[Impact
Ecosystem and Human Health] imp --> res[Response
Policy and Industry Action] res --> drv ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`). Labels use uppercase plain text without markdown formatting inside double quotes, ensuring clean rendering in KTU digital notes. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Sustainability Assessment. List any four concept models used in sustainability assessment.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Sustainability Assessment (SA) is a systematic process of evaluating the environmental, social, and economic impacts of a product, process, or system over its entire life cycle, to support decision-making toward sustainable development. **Four Concept Models:** 1. **Brundtland Model (Three-Pillar Model)** — Environment, Society, Economy. 2. **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — People, Planet, Profit (Elkington, 1994). 3. **DPSIR Framework** — Drivers, Pressures, State, Impact, Response (EEA). 4. **Five-Capital Model** — Natural, Human, Social, Manufactured, Financial Capital. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) and Carbon Footprint (CF). Mention their respective ISO standards.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) | Carbon Footprint (CF) | |---|---|---| | Scope | Multi-impact (GWP, acidification, eutrophication, etc.) | Single impact (GHG emissions only) | | ISO Standard | **ISO 14040 / 14044** | **ISO 14067** | | Output Unit | Multiple (kg CO₂-eq, kg SO₂-eq, etc.) | kg CO₂-equivalent only | | Phases | 4 (Goal, LCI, LCIA, Interpretation) | 4 (similar but GHG-focused) | | Application | Comprehensive product/process evaluation | Climate impact disclosure, ESG | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** and **DPSIR framework** as concept models of sustainability assessment, with suitable manufacturing examples. **(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing process uses **2000 kWh electricity** (emission factor = **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**) and **500 m³ natural gas** (emission factor = **2.02 kg CO₂/m³**) annually. Calculate the total Carbon Footprint. If the plant adopts solar power reducing grid electricity by 60%, what is the new Carbon Footprint? #### Model Answer — Part (a): **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — Proposed by John Elkington (1994), the TBL framework expands traditional business accounting to include three performance dimensions: - **People (Social Equity):** Worker safety, fair wages, community welfare. - **Planet (Environmental Quality):** Emissions, waste, resource use. - **Profit (Economic Viability):** Profitability, market share, long-term growth. *Manufacturing Example:* A textile factory investing in solar panels (Planet), paying fair wages (People), and reducing long-term energy costs (Profit). **DPSIR Framework** — A causal model by the European Environment Agency that traces cause-effect: - **Drivers** → socio-economic forces (e.g., consumer demand for EVs). - **Pressures** → resulting environmental load (e.g., lithium mining). - **State** → physical change (e.g., soil contamination). - **Impact** → consequences (e.g., biodiversity loss). - **Response** → actions (e.g., battery recycling policies). *Manufacturing Example:* Rising EV demand (Driver) → lithium extraction (Pressure) → groundwater depletion (State) → community health impact (Impact) → circular battery policies (Response). #### Model Answer — Part (b): **Step 1: Original Carbon Footprint** $$CF_{electricity} = 2000 \times 0.82 = 1640 \text{ kg CO}_2$$ $$CF_{gas} = 500 \times 2.02 = 1010 \text{ kg CO}_2$$ $$CF_{total, original} = 1640 + 1010 = 2650 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$ **Step 2: Apply 60% reduction in grid electricity** $$E_{reduced} = 2000 \times (1 - 0.60) = 800 \text{ kWh}$$ $$CF_{electricity, new} = 800 \times 0.82 = 656 \text{ kg CO}_2$$ **Step 3: New total carbon footprint** $$CF_{total, new} = 656 + 1010 = 1666 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$ **Step 4: Percentage reduction** $$\Delta = \frac{2650 - 1666}{2650} \times 100 = 37.13\% \text{ reduction}$$ **Final Answer:** Original CF = **2650 kg CO₂-eq**; New CF = **1666 kg CO₂-eq**; Reduction = **37.13%**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **four phases of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** as per **ISO 14040**, with the help of a suitable manufacturing example. **(b) [7 Marks]** A factory processes **500 tonnes of steel** annually. Inputs: iron ore = 1200 tonnes, coal = 600 tonnes, electricity = 1500 MWh. Outputs: finished steel = 500 tonnes, slag = 250 tonnes, CO₂ emissions = 1800 tonnes. Calculate **(i) Material Efficiency** and **(ii) E-factor**. #### Model Answer — Part (a): **Phase 1 — Goal & Scope Definition:** - Define purpose (e.g., compare two steel production routes). - Define **Functional Unit (FU)**: e.g., "1 tonne of steel." - Define **system boundaries**: cradle-to-gate. **Phase 2 — Life Cycle Inventory (LCI):** - Data collection on all material/energy inputs and emissions. - *Example:* For 1 tonne steel — 1.6 t iron ore, 0.7 t coal, 1.8 GJ energy, 2.0 t CO₂. **Phase 3 — Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA):** - Translate inventory into impact categories using characterization factors. - Categories: GWP, AP (acidification), EP (eutrophication), ODP, etc. **Phase 4 — Interpretation:** - Identify **hotspots** (e.g., blast furnace = 80% of GWP). - Conduct sensitivity/uncertainty analysis. - Provide recommendations (e.g., shift to Electric Arc Furnace using renewable electricity). #### Model Answer — Part (b): **Given Data:** - Inputs: Iron ore = 1200 t, Coal = 600 t, Electricity = 1500 MWh. - Outputs: Finished steel = 500 t, Slag = 250 t, CO₂ emissions = 1800 t. - Total input mass = 1200 + 600 = 1800 t (excluding electricity if massless). **(i) Material Efficiency:** $$ME = \frac{M_{product}}{M_{input}} \times 100$$ $$ME = \frac{500}{1800} \times 100 = 27.78\%$$ **(ii) E-factor:** E-factor uses only waste/by-products (not the main product): $$E = \frac{M_{waste}}{M_{product}}$$ $$E = \frac{250}{500} = 0.5 \text{ kg waste/kg steel}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT** include CO₂ emissions in E-factor — E-factor is *mass-based solid waste*, not gaseous emissions. > 2. **Do NOT** confuse the *input mass* (raw materials) with *output mass* (product + by-products). Many students mistakenly divide product by total input (including electricity, which has no mass). > 3. **Always** mention ISO standard numbers (ISO 14040 for LCA) when answering LCA questions — forgetting the ISO number costs marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Sustainability Assessment** - [x] **Sustainability Assessment** = systematic evaluation of environmental + social + economic impacts across a life cycle. - [x] **Concept Models** are *theoretical frameworks*; **Approaches** are *practical tools* — **never confuse them**. - [x] **Brundtland Model (1987):** Environment + Society + Economy. - [x] **Triple Bottom Line (Elkington, 1994):** People + Planet + Profit — *most-asked concept model*. - [x] **DPSIR Framework (EEA):** Drivers → Pressures → State → Impact → Response (causal chain). - [x] **Five-Capital Model:** Natural + Human + Social + Manufactured + Financial. - [x] **Planetary Boundaries (Rockström, 2009):** 9 boundaries within which humanity must operate. - [x] **LCA — ISO 14040 / 14044** — 4 phases: Goal → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation. - [x] **Carbon Footprint — ISO 14067** — unit: **kg CO₂-equivalent**; uses **GWP₁₀₀**. - [x] **Material Flow Analysis (MFA):** Tracks stocks and flows of materials; unit: **tonnes/year**. - [x] **Ecological Footprint:** Unit = **global hectares (gha)**. - [x] **Material Efficiency (ME):** % of input that becomes usable product. - [x] **E-factor:** kg of waste per kg of product (solid waste, NOT CO₂). - [x] **Recyclability Rate:** % of output that can be reprocessed. - [x] Always cite **ISO standards** in LCA/carbon footprint answers. - [x] Remember **IPCC GWP₁₀₀ values**: CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265 (AR5 standard). - [x] LCA **Functional Unit (FU)** is mandatory — define it before any computation. - [x] Memorize the **four LCA phases in order** — examiners test the sequence.

topics.title: Product Sustainability and Risk Assessment - Corporate Social Responsibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Product Sustainability and Risk Assessment - Corporate Social Responsibility # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** is a self-regulating business model that enables a manufacturing organization to be socially accountable to itself, its stakeholders, and the public. By practicing corporate social responsibility, companies can be conscious of the impact they are having on all aspects of society — economically, socially, and environmentally. As per the **KTU 2024 OESFT612 syllabus**, CSR in sustainable manufacturing is defined as: > *A strategic framework integrating ethical labor practices, environmental stewardship, stakeholder engagement, and transparent governance into the core manufacturing value chain, ensuring that product life-cycle decisions create positive social value while minimizing ecological and societal risks.* The **World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD)** defines CSR as *"the continuing commitment by business to contribute to economic development while improving the quality of life of the workforce and their families as well as of the community and society at large."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > CSR is NOT charity. It is a **strategic management discipline** that ties together **Product Stewardship**, **Risk Mitigation**, and **Stakeholder Value Creation** across the entire product life cycle (Cradle-to-Cradle). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Think of CSR as a "Three-Legged Stool" in a Manufacturing Factory:** Imagine a factory owner, Mr. Sharma, who runs a textile unit in Kerala. One day he realizes his business has three "legs" it must stand on: - **Leg 1 — Profit (Economic)**: The factory must make money to survive. Without profit, no one gets paid and the factory shuts down. - **Leg 2 — People (Social)**: The workers, their families, the local community — they must be treated fairly, paid decent wages, and kept safe. - **Leg 3 — Planet (Environmental)**: The river downstream that receives his dye effluents, the air his workers breathe, the energy he consumes. If any one leg breaks, the stool collapses. **CSR is the engineering blueprint that ensures all three legs are reinforced simultaneously.** In manufacturing terms, CSR is the **bolt, nut, and welding joint** that holds the economic engine, the social fabric, and the ecological boundary together. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (iPhone Example):** > Apple Inc.'s CSR program covers (a) reducing carbon footprint in iPhone manufacturing (Planet), (b) ensuring no child labor in Foxconn supply chain (People), and (c) maintaining shareholder returns (Profit). Remove any one and the brand value erodes. This is **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** in action. ## 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Frameworks (KTU High-Yield) | Symbol / Framework | Full Form | Origin / Authority | |---|---|---| | **TBL** | Triple Bottom Line | John Elkington (1994) | | **CSR** | Corporate Social Responsibility | Howard Bowen (1953, "Social Responsibilities of the Businessman") | | **GRI** | Global Reporting Initiative | Amsterdam, 1997 | | **ISO 26000** | International Standard for Social Responsibility | ISO, 2010 | | **UNGC** | United Nations Global Compact | Kofi Annan, 2000 | | **ESG** | Environmental, Social, Governance | Investors' lexicon | | **CSR-Pyramid** | Carroll's Four-Part CSR Model | Archie Carroll, 1991 | | **SDGs** | Sustainable Development Goals (17 Goals) | UN, 2015 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU Board Exams**, always cite at least one **standard framework** (ISO 26000, GRI, Carroll's Pyramid) when defining CSR. Examiners award marks for **framework-aware answers**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of CSR — A Structured Logic CSR in sustainable manufacturing is operationalized through three interconnected pillars. Each pillar has specific obligations, performance metrics, and risk implications. ### **Pillar 1: Economic Responsibility (The Foundation)** - The organization must be **profitable** to sustain itself and fulfill other responsibilities. - Manufacturing-specific duties: fair pricing, quality products, return on investment for shareholders, job creation, supply-chain resilience. - **Key Metric**: Operating Profit Margin, ROI, Economic Value Added (EVA). ### **Pillar 2: Legal Responsibility (The Boundary)** - Compliance with national and international laws: Factories Act (India), OSHA, REACH, RoHS, EU CE Marking, Indian Companies Act 2013 (Section 135). - Manufacturing-specific duties: occupational health & safety, environmental clearances, product safety standards, labor law adherence. - **Key Metric**: Number of compliance violations, regulatory fines, audit pass rate. ### **Pillar 3: Ethical Responsibility (The Moral Compass)** - Going beyond legal compliance to do what is "right, just, and fair." - Manufacturing-specific duties: fair wages above minimum wage, anti-child-labor, anti-corruption, supplier code of conduct. - **Key Metric**: Employee satisfaction index, supplier audit scores, ethics hotline complaints. ### **Pillar 4: Philanthropic Responsibility (The Aspiration)** - Voluntary contributions to improve quality of life: community development, education, disaster relief, environmental restoration. - Manufacturing-specific duties: plant-adopted schools, watershed programs, skill-training for locals. - **Key Metric**: CSR spend as % of profits (India mandates **2%** of average net profits under Section 135). > [!NOTE] > **Carroll's CSR Pyramid (1991)** stacks these four responsibilities hierarchically: **Economic (base) → Legal → Ethical → Philanthropic (apex)**. Each layer is a prerequisite for the next. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Framework Sheet | Framework / Concept | Structure / Formula | Application in Manufacturing | |---|---|---| | **Carroll's Pyramid** | $\text{CSR} = f(E, L, Et, Ph)$ where $E$=Economic, $L$=Legal, $Et$=Ethical, $Ph$=Philanthropic | Diagnostic tool to audit CSR maturity of a plant | | **Triple Bottom Line** | $\text{TBL} = (P_e, P_s, P_e)$ for Profit, People, Planet | Sustainability reporting | | **CSR Risk Score** | $R = P \times S \times \text{Detectability}^{-1}$ | Risk assessment matrix | | **Risk Priority Number (FMEA)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Failure Mode analysis in CSR-related processes | | **Carbon Footprint** | $\text{CF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (A_i \times EF_i)$ | Environmental pillar KPI | | **Eco-Efficiency** | $\eta_{eco} = \frac{\text{Product Value}}{\text{Environmental Impact}}$ | Resource productivity | | **CSR Spend Ratio (India)** | $\text{CSR\%} = \frac{\text{CSR Expenditure}}{\text{Avg. Net Profit (3 yrs)}} \times 100 \geq 2\%$ | Statutory compliance | | **Stakeholder Salience** | $S = f(\text{Power}, \text{Legitimacy}, \text{Urgency})$ | Mitchell, Agle & Wood (1997) model | | **Life Cycle Assessment Impact** | $\text{LCIA} = \sum (\text{Emissions} \times \text{Characterization Factors})$ | Product sustainability scoring | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Board Tip:** When writing the RPN or Risk formula, **always define each variable explicitly** (S=Severity, O=Occurrence, D=Detection on a 1–10 scale). Half-marks are lost when variables are left undefined. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In a smart factory or Industry 4.0 setup, CSR frameworks are operationalized through: 1. **Digital Twin + ESG Sensors** — Real-time monitoring of emissions, worker safety, and energy use. 2. **Blockchain-based Supply Chain** — Verifying ethical sourcing (e.g., conflict-free minerals, fair-trade cotton). 3. **AI-driven Risk Assessment** — Predictive models flagging supplier violations before they cascade. 4. **GRI-aligned Dashboards** — Automated sustainability reporting for board-level decisions. **Example**: A precision tools manufacturer in Coimbatore uses an **ESG dashboard** integrated with their ERP to track Scope-1, Scope-2, and Scope-3 emissions, water use per unit, and women-employment ratio — feeding into a GRI Standards report. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** > - **CO2**: Apply CSR frameworks to manufacturing decision-making. > - **CO3**: Evaluate product sustainability using life-cycle thinking. > - **CO4**: Conduct risk assessment for sustainable product development. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Assessment Matrices ## 3.1 Derivation: Carroll's CSR Pyramid and its Hierarchical Logic Carroll's model (1991) states that the **total CSR responsibility** of a firm is the sum of four components, weighted by their hierarchical priority. $$ \text{CSR}_{\text{total}} = w_1 \cdot E + w_2 \cdot L + w_3 \cdot Et + w_4 \cdot Ph $$ Where the weights follow the pyramid logic: - $w_1 = 1.0$ (Economic — base, non-negotiable) - $w_2 = 0.9$ (Legal — almost mandatory) - $w_3 = 0.7$ (Ethical — expected by society) - $w_4 = 0.5$ (Philanthropic — desired but not required) **Step-by-Step Logic:** **Step 1:** A manufacturing firm must first be economically viable. Without profit, no stakeholder obligations can be met. Hence, **E** forms the base of the pyramid. **Step 2:** Legal compliance is the **operating boundary**. A factory that pollutes beyond consent-board limits or uses child labor is illegal, regardless of profit. So **L** sits above E. **Step 3:** Ethical behavior extends beyond law. Paying minimum wage is legal; paying a **living wage** is ethical. This is **Et**. **Step 4:** Philanthropy is the **apex** — going beyond duty to actively uplift society (planting trees, funding schools). The narrower base of the pyramid represents how **fewer firms** reach this aspirational level. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Explain Carroll's Pyramid of CSR with reference to a manufacturing industry of your choice. (14 Marks)" — Always draw the pyramid, label all 4 tiers, and **map each tier to a real factory example**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Accounting Elkington's (1994) TBL framework extends traditional accounting's single bottom line (Profit) to three parallel "bottom lines." $$ \text{TBL Score} = \alpha \cdot P_e + \beta \cdot P_s + \gamma \cdot P_e $$ Where: - $P_e$ = Economic Performance Index (normalized 0–1) - $P_s$ = Social Performance Index (normalized 0–1) - $P_e$ = Environmental Performance Index (normalized 0–1) - $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ (weightage set by firm) **Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical):** A Kerala-based spice-processing firm computes its TBL for FY 2024-25: | Dimension | Raw Score | Normalized (0–1) | Weight | |---|---|---|---| | Economic (ROE 18%, Revenue growth 12%) | 0.75 | 0.75 | 0.4 | | Social (100% wages paid, 0 accidents, 30% women workforce) | 0.85 | 0.85 | 0.35 | | Environmental (30% renewable energy, 40% water recycled) | 0.70 | 0.70 | 0.25 | $$ \text{TBL} = (0.4 \times 0.75) + (0.35 \times 0.85) + (0.25 \times 0.70) $$ $$ = 0.300 + 0.2975 + 0.175 = 0.7725 $$ **Interpretation:** TBL = 0.77 (on 0–1 scale) = **77.25% sustainability performance**, classified as **"Sustainability Leader"** (>0.75 threshold). ## 3.3 CSR Risk Assessment Matrix (Analytical Matrix) The **CSR Risk Assessment Matrix** is a 5×5 grid plotting **Likelihood (L)** against **Severity / Impact (S)**. Each cell is assigned a risk score: $$ R_{ij} = L_i \times S_j \quad \text{where} \quad i, j \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} $$ ### **Full 5×5 Risk Matrix for CSR (Manufacturing Context):** | Likelihood ↓ \ Severity → | **1 - Insignificant** | **2 - Minor** | **3 - Moderate** | **4 - Major** | **5 - Catastrophic** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **5 - Almost Certain** | 5 (Med) | 10 (High) | 15 (High) | 20 (V.High) | 25 (V.High) | | **4 - Likely** | 4 (Low) | 8 (Med) | 12 (High) | 16 (V.High) | 20 (V.High) | | **3 - Possible** | 3 (Low) | 6 (Med) | 9 (Med) | 12 (High) | 15 (High) | | **2 - Unlikely** | 2 (Low) | 4 (Low) | 6 (Med) | 8 (Med) | 10 (High) | | **1 - Rare** | 1 (Low) | 2 (Low) | 3 (Low) | 4 (Low) | 5 (Med) | **Risk Classification Bands:** - $R \in [1, 4]$ → **Low Risk** (Green) — Monitor - $R \in [5, 9]$ → **Medium Risk** (Yellow) — Mitigate - $R \in [10, 15]$ → **High Risk** (Orange) — Action Plan - $R \in [16, 25]$ → **Very High Risk** (Red) — Immediate Escalation ### **Application Example (KTU Numerical):** A garment manufacturing unit assesses CSR risk for the event: *"Child labor in subcontractor facility."* - Likelihood (L) = **4** (Likely — common in informal subcontracting) - Severity (S) = **5** (Catastrophic — brand destruction, legal action, ILO violations) $$ R = 4 \times 5 = 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Very High Risk (Red Zone)} $$ **Mitigation Action Plan:** 1. **Immediate**: Terminate subcontractor, conduct third-party audit. 2. **Short-term**: Implement supplier code of conduct with zero-tolerance clause. 3. **Long-term**: Deploy blockchain-based worker identity verification. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write the matrix but **fail to compute R** for the given scenario. Always show: $R = L \times S = \text{numeric value} \rightarrow \text{risk band}$. ## 3.4 Stakeholder Salience Model — Mitchell, Agle & Wood (1997) The salience of a stakeholder to a manufacturing firm depends on three attributes: **Power (P)**, **Legitimacy (L)**, and **Urgency (U)**. $$ \text{Salience} = f(P, L, U) $$ | Type | Power | Legitimacy | Urgency | Salience Level | Manager's Action | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Dormant** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Low | Monitor | | **Discretionary** | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | Low | CSR-only engagement | | **Demanding** | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Medium | Negotiate | | **Dominant** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Medium-High | Active engagement | | **Dangerous** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | Medium-High | Address concerns | | **Dependent** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | Medium | Proxy acts for them | | **Definitive** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | **Highest** | Priority management | > [!NOTE] > **Definitive Stakeholders** (e.g., large institutional investors, regulators, major customers) command the most attention. For a CSR-aligned manufacturing firm, these are the **board-level decision influencers**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Carroll's CSR Pyramid (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD L1["PHILANTHROPIC
Voluntary Acts
Community Development"]:::apex L2["ETHICAL
Beyond Legal Duty
Fair Wages Anti Corruption"]:::tier3 L3["LEGAL
Compliance with Laws
Factories Act OSHA REACH"]:::tier2 L4["ECONOMIC
Profitability ROI
Job Creation"]:::base L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 classDef apex fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef tier3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef tier2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef base fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — Interlocking Circles ```mermaid graph LR A["PROFIT
Economic Value"]:::profit B["PEOPLE
Social Equity"]:::people C["PLANET
Environmental Integrity"]:::planet D["SUSTAINABLE
MANUFACTURING"]:::center A --- D B --- D C --- D A --- B B --- C A --- C classDef profit fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef people fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef planet fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef center fill:#fbc02d,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 CSR Risk Assessment Workflow (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1
Identify CSR Risks
Supply Chain Labor Emissions"]:::step1 S2["STEP 2
Categorize Stakeholders
Power Legitimacy Urgency"]:::step2 S3["STEP 3
Score Likelihood L 1-5"]:::step3 S4["STEP 4
Score Severity S 1-5"]:::step4 S5["STEP 5
Compute Risk R L times S"]:::step5 S6["STEP 6
Classify Risk Band
Low Medium High VHigh"]:::step6 S7["STEP 7
Mitigation Plan
Avoid Reduce Transfer Accept"]:::step7 S8["STEP 8
Monitor and Review
GRI ISO 26000 Reporting"]:::step8 S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 S8 -.Feedback Loop.-> S1 classDef step1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 classDef step2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 classDef step3 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#01579b classDef step4 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#01579b classDef step5 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f classDef step6 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f classDef step7 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 classDef step8 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.4 ISO 26000 Seven Core Subjects of Social Responsibility ```mermaid mindmap root((ISO 26000
Seven Core
Subjects)) OrgGovernance Accountability Transparency HumanRights Due Diligence Discrimination LaborPractices Wages Safety Child Forced Labor Environment Climate Pollution Resource Use FairOperating Anti Corruption Fair Competition ConsumerIssues Product Safety Information Privacy Community Development Education Health ``` ## 4.5 CSR Integration Across Product Life Cycle ```mermaid graph LR A["RAW MATERIAL
Ethical Sourcing
Conflict Free Minerals"]:::lc1 B["DESIGN
Eco Design DfE
Cradle to Cradle"]:::lc2 C["PRODUCTION
Cleaner Production
Worker Safety"]:::lc3 D["DISTRIBUTION
Green Logistics
Carbon Neutral Shipping"]:::lc4 E["USE
Product Longevity
Energy Efficiency"]:::lc5 F["END OF LIFE
Take Back Schemes
Recycling Reuse"]:::lc6 A ==> B ==> C ==> D ==> E ==> F F -.Circular Feedback.-> A classDef lc1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 classDef lc2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 classDef lc3 fill:#81c784,stroke:#1b5e20 classDef lc4 fill:#66bb6a,stroke:#1b5e20 classDef lc5 fill:#4caf50,stroke:#1b5e20 classDef lc6 fill:#388e3c,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper - Set A]** ### **Q1. Define Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) as applied to a manufacturing enterprise.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) is a self-regulating business model wherein a manufacturing enterprise integrates social and environmental concerns into its core operations and stakeholder interactions. It encompasses the firm's economic, legal, ethical, and philanthropic obligations (Carroll's Pyramid) toward workers, communities, the environment, and shareholders, ensuring that product life-cycle activities create shared value while minimizing negative externalities. > *(Keywords for full marks: self-regulation, integration, stakeholders, life-cycle, shared value.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** ### **Q2. List any THREE differences between CSR and Charity.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | CSR | Charity | |---|---|---| | **Nature** | Strategic, integrated into business model | Reactive, one-time voluntary act | | **Motivation** | Long-term value creation & risk mitigation | Altruism, brand image | | **Scope** | Covers all stakeholders across value chain | Targeted at specific beneficiaries | | **Legal Status** | Mandated under Section 135, Companies Act 2013 (India) for eligible firms | Purely voluntary | | **Measurement** | KPIs, GRI metrics, ESG scores | Spend-only, hard to measure impact | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - ESE Model Question Paper]** ### **Question A: 14 Marks (Choice Option 1)** > **"Explain Carroll's Pyramid of CSR in detail. With a suitable manufacturing case study, discuss how each tier of the pyramid is operationalized in the plant's daily operations."** *(14 Marks)* **Sub-part (a): Theory of Carroll's Pyramid — 7 Marks** **Model Answer (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1: Introduction (1 Mark)** Archie Carroll's CSR Pyramid (1991) is a four-tier hierarchical framework that defines the total social responsibility of a business. It states that CSR comprises Economic, Legal, Ethical, and Philanthropic responsibilities. **Step 2: Tier 1 — Economic Responsibility (2 Marks)** - The base of the pyramid. - Obligation: Be profitable, provide goods/services, generate employment, pay fair returns to investors. - *Manufacturing example:* A Kerala-based cashew processing unit achieving 15% ROE, paying suppliers within 30 days, and reinvesting 20% of profits in plant modernization. **Step 3: Tier 2 — Legal Responsibility (2 Marks)** - Compliance with local, national, and international laws. - *Manufacturing example:* Adherence to the **Factories Act 1948**, **OSHA standards**, **Pollution Control Board consent** under the Water Act and Air Act, and **REACH/RoHS** for export markets. **Step 4: Tier 3 — Ethical Responsibility (1 Mark)** - Beyond legal compliance — doing what is just and fair. - *Manufacturing example:* Paying workers 30% above minimum wage, anti-harassment policy, transparent supplier audits. **Step 5: Tier 4 — Philanthropic Responsibility (1 Mark)** - Apex of the pyramid — voluntary contributions. - *Manufacturing example:* Funding a village school, rainwater harvesting in the plant vicinity, free skill-training for local youth. --- **Sub-part (b): Numerical / Analytical Application — 7 Marks** > **Scenario:** A medium-scale auto-ancillary unit in Pune has the following CSR performance data for FY 2024-25. Construct Carroll's Pyramid and assign a **CSR Maturity Score** using weights $w_1 = 1.0$, $w_2 = 0.9$, $w_3 = 0.7$, $w_4 = 0.5$. | Tier | Performance Indicator | Raw Score (out of 100) | |---|---|---| | Economic (E) | ROE 18%, Revenue Growth 10% | 85 | | Legal (L) | 0 violations, 100% audit pass | 95 | | Ethical (Et) | Wage ratio 1.4×, 0 corruption cases | 75 | | Philanthropic (Ph) | 2.5% of profit on community projects | 60 | **Solution:** **Step 1:** Normalize raw scores to a 0–1 scale by dividing by 100. $$ E = 0.85, \quad L = 0.95, \quad Et = 0.75, \quad Ph = 0.60 $$ **Step 2:** Apply the weighted CSR formula: $$ \text{CSR}_{\text{score}} = (1.0 \times 0.85) + (0.9 \times 0.95) + (0.7 \times 0.75) + (0.5 \times 0.60) $$ **Step 3:** Compute each term: $$ = 0.850 + 0.855 + 0.525 + 0.300 $$ **Step 4:** Sum the terms: $$ = 2.530 \quad \text{on a maximum scale of } (1.0 + 0.9 + 0.7 + 0.5) = 3.1 $$ **Step 5:** Normalize to percentage: $$ \text{CSR Maturity \%} = \frac{2.530}{3.1} \times 100 = 81.6\% $$ **Step 6: Interpretation (1 Mark):** A CSR maturity score of **81.6%** classifies the firm as a **"Mature CSR Practitioner"** (Threshold: >75%). Recommendation: Strengthen philanthropic and ethical dimensions to reach the next level ("CSR Leader", >90%). --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Choice Option 2)** > **"Discuss the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework. Using a manufacturing case study, compute the TBL score and recommend strategies to improve it."** *(14 Marks)* **Sub-part (a): Theory of TBL — 7 Marks** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Origin (1 Mark)** John Elkington (1994) coined the **Triple Bottom Line** concept, arguing that firms should measure success not just by **Profit**, but also by **People** (social) and **Planet** (environmental) performance. **Step 2: The Three Dimensions (3 Marks)** - **Profit (Economic)**: Revenue, profit margins, ROI, tax contributions, job creation. - **People (Social)**: Worker welfare, community impact, human rights, diversity, health & safety. - **Planet (Environmental)**: Emissions, water use, waste, biodiversity impact, energy mix. **Step 3: Why TBL in Manufacturing? (2 Marks)** - Regulations like EU's **Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)** now mandate TBL disclosures. - Investors use **ESG ratings** (MSCI, Sustainalytics) that map directly onto TBL. - Consumers increasingly demand **sustainable products** — a McKinsey 2023 study shows 66% of consumers consider sustainability in purchases. **Step 4: Interlinkage (1 Mark)** The three dimensions are **not independent**. Reducing waste (Planet) lowers cost (Profit) and improves worker safety (People). This is the **"shared value"** principle (Porter & Kramer, 2011). --- **Sub-part (b): Numerical TBL Computation — 7 Marks** > **Scenario:** A Kerala-based spice processing firm computes its TBL for FY 2024-25 with the following data: | Dimension | Indicators | Raw Score (0–100) | Weight | |---|---|---|---| | **Profit** | ROE 18%, Revenue Growth 12%, Cost Efficiency 80% | 78 | 0.40 | | **People** | 100% wages paid, 0 LTAs, 30% women workforce, 95% training coverage | 85 | 0.35 | | **Planet** | 30% renewable energy, 40% water recycled, 25% waste reduction | 70 | 0.25 | **Solution:** **Step 1:** Normalize raw scores to 0–1. $$ P_{\text{profit}} = 0.78, \quad P_{\text{people}} = 0.85, \quad P_{\text{planet}} = 0.70 $$ **Step 2:** Apply the TBL weighted formula. $$ \text{TBL} = (w_1 \times P_{\text{profit}}) + (w_2 \times P_{\text{people}}) + (w_3 \times P_{\text{planet}}) $$ **Step 3:** Substitute values. $$ \text{TBL} = (0.40 \times 0.78) + (0.35 \times 0.85) + (0.25 \times 0.70) $$ **Step 4:** Compute each term. $$ = 0.312 + 0.2975 + 0.175 $$ **Step 5:** Sum. $$ \text{TBL} = 0.7845 \approx 78.45\% $$ **Step 6: Interpretation & Recommendations (2 Marks)** - The firm scores **78.45%** → **Sustainability Leader** (Threshold: 75%). - **Strengths**: Strong social performance (0.85). - **Weakness**: Environmental pillar (0.70) is the lowest. - **Recommendations**: 1. Increase renewable energy share from 30% → 50% via solar rooftop. 2. Install rainwater harvesting to boost water recycling from 40% → 65%. 3. Adopt **biomass briquette** boiler to reduce fossil dependence. 4. Target TBL ≥ 90% in 2 years for **Sustainability Champion** status. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > 1. **Skipping normalization**: Students forget to convert raw scores (out of 100) to a 0–1 scale. Always normalize. > 2. **Missing weight declaration**: Weights ($\alpha, \beta, \gamma$) must be explicitly stated and must sum to 1. > 3. **No interpretation band**: Always classify the final TBL score (e.g., <0.50 = Beginner, 0.50–0.75 = Performer, >0.75 = Leader). > 4. **Theory without case study**: KTU ESE 14-mark questions require both **theory (7 marks)** and **numerical/case (7 marks)**. Don't skip either. > 5. **Forgetting framework citations**: Always cite **Carroll (1991)**, **Elkington (1994)**, or **Freeman (1984)** for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Block — Read this the night before the KTU exam.** - **CSR = Economic + Legal + Ethical + Philanthropic** (Carroll's 4-tier Pyramid, 1991). - **TBL = Profit + People + Planet** (Elkington, 1994). TBL score = weighted sum (weights sum to 1). - **Stakeholders** are grouped by **Power, Legitimacy, Urgency** (Mitchell, Agle & Wood, 1997). Definitive stakeholders (all 3) get top priority. - **CSR Risk = Likelihood × Severity** (1–5 scale). Risk Bands: 1–4 (Low), 5–9 (Med), 10–15 (High), 16–25 (Very High). - **ISO 26000** = 7 core subjects: Organizational Governance, Human Rights, Labor Practices, Environment, Fair Operating Practices, Consumer Issues, Community Involvement. - **GRI Standards** = Most widely used global sustainability reporting framework (Universal + Topic-Specific Standards). - **India's Companies Act 2013, Section 135** mandates CSR spend ≥ **2% of average net profits** (last 3 years) for eligible companies (turnover ≥ ₹1000 cr OR profit ≥ ₹5 cr). - **UN SDGs (2015)** = 17 Sustainable Development Goals — manufacturing aligns especially with SDG 9 (Industry), SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption), SDG 13 (Climate Action). - **ESG** = Environmental, Social, Governance — the investment-world proxy for CSR. - **Cradle-to-Cradle** is the aspirational design philosophy — all outputs become inputs, zero waste. - **Always cite a framework + an example** in 14-mark answers. A theory answer without a manufacturing case study loses at least 2–3 marks. - **Numericals must show: Given → Formula → Substitution → Computation → Interpretation.** Half-marks are awarded for the interpretation step. - **Common acronyms to memorize**: CSR, TBL, ESG, GRI, ISO 26000, UNGC, SDGs, LCA, RPN, FMEA, OSHA, REACH, RoHS, CE, CSRD. - **KTU 2024 Hot Topics**: Greenwashing, Scope-3 emissions, circular economy, just transition, B-Corps certification, supply chain due diligence (EU CSDDD). - **Carroll's Pyramid weights (memory aid)**: **E**conomics is **1.0** (base) → **L**egal is **0.9** → **E**thical is **0.7** → **P**hilanthropic is **0.5**. Think "**E**very **L**evel **E**scalates **P**rogressively" but with decreasing structural weight.

topics.title: Life Cycle Analysis - Remanufacture and Disposal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Life Cycle Analysis - Remanufacture and Disposal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) — Formal Definition

**Life Cycle Analysis (LCA)**, also referred to as **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** or **Cradle-to-Grave Analysis**, is a systematic, standardized, and internationally recognized methodology used to quantify the **environmental impacts** associated with every stage of a product's life — from raw material extraction through manufacturing, use, and final end-of-life disposition.

As per the **ISO 14040** and **ISO 14044** standards (the governing international benchmarks for LCA), the technique provides a **holistic, systems-based framework** that prevents *burden-shifting* — that is, solving one environmental problem while unintentionally worsening another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** LCA is "a technique to assess the environmental aspects and potential impacts associated with a product, process, or service, by:
> - Compiling an inventory of relevant energy and material inputs and environmental releases
> - Evaluating the potential environmental impacts associated with identified inputs and releases
> - Interpreting the results to help decision-makers make a more informed choice"

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Passport" of a Product

Imagine every manufactured product — say, a **smartphone** — carries an invisible **environmental passport**. This passport tracks *every* gram of carbon dioxide, *every* liter of water, and *every* joule of energy consumed from the moment its raw minerals are mined in the Congo, through assembly in Shenzhen, use in your pocket, until its battery is buried in a landfill in Ghana.

**LCA is the act of stamping and reading that passport.**

| Life Stage | Environmental Passport Entry |
|---|---|
| Raw Material Extraction | "60 kg of CO₂-eq emitted to mine the cobalt and lithium" |
| Manufacturing | "85 kWh of electricity consumed during PCB assembly" |
| Distribution | "12 kg of CO₂ from container shipping" |
| Use Phase | "40 kWh consumed over 3 years of charging" |
| **Remanufacture / Disposal** | "**32 kg CO₂-eq recovered; 78% materials reintroduced**" |

## 1.3 Remanufacture — Definition

**Remanufacture** is an industrial process whereby a **used product (called a "core")** is restored to a condition equivalent to — or sometimes better than — a newly manufactured unit. It involves **disassembly, cleaning, inspection, repair, part replacement, reassembly, and testing**, all under strict quality standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Remanufacturing is NOT the same as refurbishing, repairing, or recycling. It is the *most value-preserving* end-of-life strategy because it retains **up to 85% of the original embodied energy**.

## 1.4 Disposal — Definition

**Disposal** refers to the **final deposition** of a product or its residues into the environment, typically through **landfilling, incineration (with or without energy recovery), or controlled dumping**. It represents the **least preferred** option in the **9R Waste Hierarchy** (Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Refurbish, Remanufacture, Repurpose, Recycle — and only lastly, **Recover/Dispose**).

## 1.5 The Intuitive Link Between the Three Concepts

```
LCA = the "Diagnosis Tool"      →  tells you HOW MUCH impact a product has
Remanufacture = the "Cure"      →  recovers value & minimizes end-of-life impact
Disposal = the "Last Resort"    →  where everything that can't be recovered ends up
```

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook for exams:** *LCA measures; Remanufacture rescues; Disposal dumps.*
