# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any two open-source and two commercial Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) software packages used for sustainability analysis. Mention the standard ISO framework they comply with.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Open-Source:** OpenLCA, Brightway2 *(1 mark)*
- **Commercial:** SimaPro, GaBi (Sphera) *(1 mark)*
- **ISO Compliance:** All the above comply with **ISO 14040** (Principles and Framework) and **ISO 14044** (Requirements and Guidelines) for Life Cycle Assessment. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Characterization Factor" with respect to LCIA. Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Characterization Factor (CF)** is a numerical multiplier that converts an inventory flow of a substance (in kg, MJ, or m³) into a common environmental impact unit (e.g., kg CO₂-eq). *(2 marks)*

**Example:** The GWP₁₀₀ of methane (CH₄) is **27.9 kg CO₂-eq per kg CH₄** (IPCC AR6). This means 1 kg of methane emitted has the same 100-year warming effect as 27.9 kg of CO₂. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules, the student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**a)** Explain in detail the **classification of sustainability software packages** along the axes of licensing, analytical scope, impact methodology, and database integration. Give **two examples per class**. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Describe the **functional architecture of a typical LCA software** and explain how it executes the four ISO 14040 phases. Illustrate with a block diagram. *(7 marks)*

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

| Axis | Class | Example 1 | Example 2 | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Licensing | Commercial | SimaPro | GaBi (Sphera) | 1.5 |
| Licensing | Open-Source | OpenLCA | Brightway2 | 1.0 |
| Scope | Streamlined | One Click LCA | eFootprint | 1.0 |
| Scope | CAD-Integrated | SolidWorks Sustainability | Autodesk Insight | 1.0 |
| Methodology | Midpoint | CML 2001 | TRACI 2.1 | 1.0 |
| Methodology | Endpoint | ReCiPe 2016 Endpoint | Eco-indicator 99 | 0.5 |
| Database | Generic | ecoinvent 3.x | USLCI | 1.0 |

**Final 1.0 mark** is awarded for the concluding statement on the importance of selecting a tool aligned with the user's goal (academic vs. industry, region, ISO compliance).

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Mention ISO 14040 Four Phases:** (1 mark)
Goal & Scope, LCI, LCIA, Interpretation.

**Step 2 — Explain Phase 1 (Goal & Scope):** (1 mark)
The software prompts the user to enter: functional unit, reference flow, system boundary (cradle-to-gate, gate-to-gate, cradle-to-grave), and allocation rules.

**Step 3 — Explain Phase 2 (LCI):** (2 marks)
The user constructs a **product tree** with foreground processes. The software automatically links to background datasets (ecoinvent, ELCD) for electricity, transport, raw materials. Each flow is tagged with mass, energy, or emission.

**Step 4 — Explain Phase 3 (LCIA):** (1.5 marks)
The software applies **characterization factors** from a chosen impact method (e.g., ReCiPe 2016). It computes midpoint scores such as GWP, AP, EP, HTP, ADP, CED.

**Step 5 — Explain Phase 4 (Interpretation):** (1 mark)
Software generates **Sankey diagrams, contribution bar charts, hot-spot analysis**, and runs **Monte Carlo simulation** (typically 10,000 iterations) for uncertainty quantification.

**Step 6 — Block Diagram:** (0.5 marks) — refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Restated]**

**a)** A 1 kg of primary aluminium ingot has the following LCI emissions:
- CO₂: 1.50 kg
- CH₄: 1.2 g
- N₂O: 0.02 g
- SF₆: 0.0001 g

Using the **GWP₁₀₀ characterization factors** (CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 27.9, N₂O = 273, SF₆ = 23,500), compute the **total Global Warming Potential** in kg CO₂-eq. Identify the **dominant contributor** and recommend **one mitigation strategy**. *(7 marks)*

**b)** Compare **SimaPro, GaBi, and OpenLCA** in terms of licensing, default impact method, supported databases, and suitability for KTU academic projects. *(7 marks)*

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

(This is the same analytical derivation as Section 3.1 of these notes; re-stated here for examiner convenience.)

| Flow | Mass $m_j$ (kg) | CF (kg CO₂-eq/kg) | Contribution $CF \cdot m_j$ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| CO₂ | 1.50 | 1.0 | 1.5000 |
| CH₄ | 0.0012 | 27.9 | 0.03348 |
| N₂O | 0.00002 | 273 | 0.00546 |
| SF₆ | 0.0000001 | 23,500 | 0.00235 |
| **Total** | — | — | **1.54129** |

$$\boxed{S_{GWP} = 1.54 \, \text{kg CO}_{2}\text{-eq per kg Al}}$$

**Dominant Contributor:** CO₂ contributes **~97.3%** of the GWP. *(1 mark)*

**Mitigation Strategy:** Use **renewable electricity** (hydropower) in the Hall-Héroult electrolysis process — expected GWP reduction of **70–80%**. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1 (Q.B-a):** A common error is to **confuse the units** — mixing grams (g) and kilograms (kg) without converting. Always convert all flows to the **same base unit (kg)** before multiplying with the CF. **Loss of marks: up to 2.**
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2 (Q.B-a):** Students often forget to **multiply** $CF \times m_j$ — they just list the CF and mass separately. **Loss of marks: up to 3.**

#### 📘 Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

| Feature | SimaPro | GaBi (Sphera) | OpenLCA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Licensing** (1.5) | Commercial, subscription | Commercial, subscription | **Free, open-source** |
| **Default Method** (1.5) | ReCiPe, CML, IPCC | CML, TRACI, EF 3.1 | All (plugin-based) |
| **Database** (1.5) | ecoinvent, ELCD, USLCI | GaBi DB, ecoinvent | ecoinvent (free cut-off) |
| **Academic Suitability for KTU** (1.5) | Limited (license cost) | Limited (license cost) | **Highly suitable (free, ISO-compliant)** |
| **Strength** (1.0) | Industry-grade reporting | Strong automotive datasets | Free, customizable, KTU-friendly |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #3 (Q.B-b):** Students often write only one or two comparison parameters. The KTU 2024 marking scheme requires **at least four** parameters × **three** tools → 12 cells, plus a concluding statement. **Loss of marks: up to 3** for missing parameters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **ISO 14040 / 14044** are the mandatory standards for any credible LCA software.
- [ ] The **four LCA phases** are: Goal & Scope → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation.
- [ ] **Characterization Equation:** $S_{i} = \sum_{j} CF_{i,j} \cdot m_{j}$ — the universal kernel of every LCA engine.
- [ ] **GWP₁₀₀ of CH₄ = 27.9**, **N₂O = 273**, **SF₆ = 23,500** (IPCC AR6) — these specific values are KTU-favorite numbers.
- [ ] **Three commercial packages** to memorize: **SimaPro, GaBi, Umberto**.
- [ ] **Two open-source packages** to memorize: **OpenLCA, Brightway2**.
- [ ] **OpenLCA + ecoinvent (free cut-off)** is the de-facto tool for KTU final-year sustainable manufacturing projects.
- [ ] **CML, TRACI, ReCiPe, EF 3.1, USEtox** are the five impact assessment methods you must be able to name and differentiate.
- [ ] **Monte Carlo simulation** is the standard technique for uncertainty analysis in modern LCA software (typically 10,000 runs).
- [ ] **Normalization** converts absolute impact scores into dimensionless ratios using a reference (e.g., per-capita world average).
- [ ] **Weighting** aggregates multiple impact categories into a **single eco-score (Pt)**, but it is **value-laden and non-ISO-mandatory**.
- [ ] **Functional Unit (FU)** is the basis for all comparisons in LCA — always define it first.
- [ ] **System Boundary** decisions (cradle-to-gate vs. cradle-to-grave) drastically change the result — always declare it.
- [ ] **Allocation** problem → solve by mass, economic value, or system expansion (ISO-preferred: system expansion).
- [ ] **Hot-spot identification** is the primary deliverable of interpretation: it tells the manufacturer *where* to act.
- [ ] **CAD-integrated tools** (SolidWorks Sustainability, Autodesk Insight) are used at the **design stage** (preventive LCA), while standalone LCA tools (SimaPro, OpenLCA) are used at the **retrospective stage**.
- [ ] Always convert all inventory flows to a **single base unit (kg)** before applying CFs — KTU examiner's #1 deduction point.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Software Packages for Sustainability Analysis**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, OESFT612, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indicators of Sustainability - Environmental, Economic, Societal and Business Indicators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Indicators of Sustainability - Environmental, Economic, Societal and Business Indicators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Sustainability Indicators?

A **Sustainability Indicator** is a quantitative or qualitative measure derived from observable facts that simplifies and clarifies complex sustainability phenomena, integrates information about physical, social, economic, and business systems, and tracks the condition or trend of these systems over time. These indicators function as diagnostic tools that translate raw data into meaningful information for decision-makers in manufacturing industries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board-Standard):**
> *Sustainability indicators are measurable variables that provide a basis for evaluating the performance of a manufacturing system or organization against the three pillars of sustainability (environmental, economic, and social), along with business-specific performance metrics.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of sustainability indicators as the **"Dashboard of a Car"**. When you drive a car, you don't just look at the speedometer — you also check the fuel gauge (resource consumption), engine temperature (system health), warning lights (alerts/risks), and odometer (long-term performance). 

Similarly, in a manufacturing firm:
- **Environmental indicators** = Fuel gauge & exhaust monitor (showing if the company is polluting or depleting resources)
- **Economic indicators** = Speedometer & odometer (showing financial speed and long-term economic distance)
- **Societal indicators** = Dashboard warning lights (showing social/people-related alerts)
- **Business indicators** = Engine performance & RPM (showing operational and strategic health)

Without these indicators, a manager is essentially "driving blind."

## 1.3 Classification of Sustainability Indicators

The four major categories of sustainability indicators, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus, are:

| Sl. No. | Category | Core Focus | Typical Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Environmental Indicators** | Resource use & emissions | Carbon footprint, water footprint, energy intensity |
| 2 | **Economic Indicators** | Financial & economic viability | NPV, ROI, cost-benefit ratio |
| 3 | **Societal Indicators** | People, equity & well-being | HDI, employment rate, Gini coefficient |
| 4 | **Business Indicators** | Strategic & operational performance | Eco-efficiency, market share, green revenue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – KTU 2024):**
> The four indicator families are **not independent silos**. They are deeply interlinked through the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework and the **DPSIR causal chain**. A question in KTU exams frequently asks students to *link* two or more indicator families.

## 1.4 Why Indicators Matter in Sustainable Manufacturing

Indicators serve four primary functions:
1. **Simplification** — Condense complex data into understandable metrics.
2. **Quantification** — Convert qualitative concepts (e.g., "well-being") into numbers.
3. **Communication** — Provide a common language for stakeholders (regulators, investors, communities).
4. **Decision Support** — Enable benchmarking, target-setting, and progress tracking.

## 1.5 The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Foundation

The **Triple Bottom Line**, conceptualized by **John Elkington in 1994**, is the philosophical backbone of sustainability indicators. It posits that organizational performance should be evaluated on three dimensions:

- **People** (Social/Societal)
- **Planet** (Environmental)
- **Profit** (Economic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Business indicators are often considered the "fourth dimension"** in modern sustainable manufacturing literature, as they integrate the three TBL pillars into firm-level strategic metrics. This is the convention followed in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 1.6 The DPSIR Framework (Causal Logic Behind Indicators)

The **DPSIR framework** (Drivers–Pressures–State–Impacts–Responses), developed by the **European Environment Agency (EEA)**, provides the causal logic that links sustainability indicators to the systems they measure.

- **D (Drivers):** Underlying socio-economic forces (e.g., population growth, industrial demand).
- **P (Pressures):** Direct human-induced effects (e.g., emissions, resource extraction).
- **S (State):** The resulting condition of the environment/society (e.g., air quality, poverty levels).
- **I (Impacts):** Consequences of state changes (e.g., health damage, ecosystem loss).
- **R (Responses):** Policy and management actions (e.g., regulations, clean technology adoption).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When answering descriptive questions, always connect a specific indicator to its DPSIR position. For example, *CO₂ emissions* is a **Pressure** indicator, while *Mean annual temperature* is a **State** indicator.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Environmental Indicators

Environmental indicators measure the impact of manufacturing activities on natural systems. They are divided into **input-based** (resource use) and **output-based** (emissions/waste) categories.

### 2.1.1 Energy Intensity (EI)

Energy intensity measures the energy consumed per unit of product or economic output.

$$EI = \frac{E_{consumed}}{Q_{output}}$$

Where:
- $E_{consumed}$ = Total energy consumed (MJ or kWh)
- $Q_{output}$ = Quantity of product produced (units, kg, or revenue ₹)

**Unit:** MJ/unit or kWh/unit

### 2.1.2 Carbon Footprint (CF)

The carbon footprint quantifies total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions expressed as **CO₂ equivalent (CO₂e)**.

$$CF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i$$

Where:
- $AD_i$ = Activity data for source *i* (e.g., liters of fuel, kWh of electricity)
- $EF_i$ = Emission factor for source *i*
- $GWP_i$ = Global Warming Potential of gas *i* (over 100 years)

**Unit:** kg CO₂e or tonnes CO₂e

### 2.1.3 Water Footprint (WF)

The water footprint accounts for direct and indirect freshwater use.

$$WF = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$$

Where:
- $WF_{blue}$ = Volume of surface/groundwater consumed
- $WF_{green}$ = Volume of rainwater consumed
- $WF_{grey}$ = Volume of water required to dilute pollutants to acceptable standards

**Unit:** m³ or liters per unit product

### 2.1.4 Material Intensity (MI)

Material intensity measures the material input per unit of output.

$$MI = \frac{M_{input}}{Q_{output}}$$

**Unit:** kg/unit

### 2.1.5 Waste Recycling Rate (WRR)

$$WRR = \frac{W_{recycled}}{W_{total}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.6 Specific Emissions

$$SE = \frac{E_{emission}}{Q_{output}}$$

Where $E_{emission}$ is the mass of a specific pollutant (SOx, NOx, PM, etc.) emitted.

### 2.1.7 Air Quality Index (AQI)

AQI aggregates multiple pollutants into a single number using pollutant-specific sub-indices.

$$AQI = \max(I_{PM2.5}, I_{PM10}, I_{SO_2}, I_{NO_2}, I_{CO}, I_{O_3})$$

The sub-index for each pollutant is:

$$I_p = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = Measured concentration of pollutant *p*
- $BP_{Hi}, BP_{Lo}$ = Breakpoint concentration limits
- $I_{Hi}, I_{Lo}$ = Corresponding AQI breakpoints

---

## 2.2 Economic Indicators

Economic indicators measure the financial viability and macroeconomic impact of sustainable practices.

### 2.2.1 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$

Where:
- $B_t$ = Benefits in year *t*
- $C_t$ = Costs in year *t*
- $r$ = Discount rate
- $T$ = Project lifetime

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if $NPV \geq 0$.

### 2.2.2 Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

$$BCR = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{B_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if $BCR \geq 1$.

### 2.2.3 Return on Investment (ROI)

$$ROI = \frac{Net\ Benefit}{Total\ Investment} \times 100\%$$

### 2.2.4 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

IRR is the discount rate $r$ that makes $NPV = 0$:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1 + IRR)^t} = 0$$

**Decision Rule:** Accept if $IRR \geq$ required rate of return (hurdle rate).

### 2.2.5 Eco-Efficiency (EE) — *The Bridge Indicator*

Eco-Efficiency is the ratio of economic value added to environmental impact added.

$$EE = \frac{Economic\ Value\ Added}{Environmental\ Impact\ Added}$$

**Unit:** ₹/kg CO₂e, or \$/m³ water, or ₹/MJ energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eco-Efficiency is the MOST important bridge indicator** in KTU exams because it sits at the intersection of *Environmental*, *Economic*, and *Business* indicator families.

### 2.2.6 Gross Value Added (GVA)

$$GVA = Output\ Value - Intermediate\ Consumption$$

### 2.2.7 Green GDP

$$Green\ GDP = GDP - Environmental\ Degradation\ Costs - Resource\ Depletion\ Costs$$

---

## 2.3 Societal Indicators

Societal indicators assess the well-being of people, communities, and societies affected by manufacturing activities.

### 2.3.1 Human Development Index (HDI)

HDI is a composite of three dimensions: **Health**, **Education**, **Standard of Living**.

$$HDI = \sqrt[3]{H_E \times H_{LE} \times H_{LI}}$$

Where:
- $H_E$ = Education index (mean of expected and mean years of schooling)
- $H_{LE}$ = Life expectancy index
- $H_{LI}$ = Logarithmic income index based on GNI per capita

### 2.3.2 Gini Coefficient (G)

The Gini coefficient measures income inequality, ranging from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality).

$$G = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_i - x_j \vert}{2 n^2 \bar{x}}$$

Where:
- $x_i, x_j$ = Incomes of individuals *i* and *j*
- $\bar{x}$ = Mean income
- $n$ = Number of individuals

### 2.3.3 Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)

$$LTIFR = \frac{Number\ of\ Lost\ Time\ Injuries \times 10^6}{Total\ Hours\ Worked}$$

### 2.3.4 Employment Rate

$$ER = \frac{Employed\ Population}{Working\ Age\ Population} \times 100\%$$

### 2.3.5 Social Return on Investment (SROI)

$$SROI = \frac{Present\ Value\ of\ Social\ Benefits}{Present\ Value\ of\ Social\ Investments}$$

---

## 2.4 Business Indicators

Business indicators measure firm-level strategic and operational sustainability performance.

### 2.4.1 Market Share Growth (MSG)

$$MSG = \frac{Market\ Share_{current} - Market\ Share_{previous}}{Market\ Share_{previous}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.4.2 Green Revenue Ratio (GRR)

$$GRR = \frac{Revenue\ from\ Green\ Products}{Total\ Revenue} \times 100\%$$

### 2.4.3 Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)

$$CSI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (S_i \times W_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = Score of satisfaction parameter *i* (typically 1–10)
- $W_i$ = Importance weight assigned to parameter *i*

### 2.4.4 Employee Turnover Rate (ETR)

$$ETR = \frac{Number\ of\ Employees\ Who\ Left}{Average\ Number\ of\ Employees} \times 100\%$$

### 2.4.5 Compliance Rate (CR)

$$CR = \frac{Number\ of\ Regulations\ Complied\ With}{Total\ Number\ of\ Applicable\ Regulations} \times 100\%$$

### 2.4.6 Innovation Index

A composite measure of new product/process introductions, R&D spend, and patents filed.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU Part B question in Module 4 will draw from these formulas.**

| # | Indicator | Formula | Unit | Indicator Family |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Energy Intensity | $EI = E_{consumed} / Q_{output}$ | MJ/unit | Environmental |
| 2 | Carbon Footprint | $CF = \sum AD_i \cdot EF_i \cdot GWP_i$ | kg CO₂e | Environmental |
| 3 | Water Footprint | $WF = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$ | m³ | Environmental |
| 4 | Material Intensity | $MI = M_{input} / Q_{output}$ | kg/unit | Environmental |
| 5 | Waste Recycling Rate | $WRR = (W_{recycled}/W_{total}) \times 100$ | % | Environmental |
| 6 | AQI Sub-Index | $I_p = I_{Lo} + \frac{(I_{Hi} - I_{Lo})(C_p - BP_{Lo})}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}}$ | dimensionless | Environmental |
| 7 | NPV | $NPV = \sum (B_t - C_t)/(1+r)^t$ | ₹ | Economic |
| 8 | BCR | $BCR = \sum B_t/(1+r)^t \div \sum C_t/(1+r)^t$ | ratio | Economic |
| 9 | ROI | $ROI = (Net\ Benefit / Investment) \times 100$ | % | Economic |
| 10 | IRR | $\sum (B_t - C_t)/(1+IRR)^t = 0$ | % | Economic |
| 11 | Eco-Efficiency | $EE = Value\ Added / Impact\ Added$ | ₹/kg | Bridge |
| 12 | Green GDP | $Green\ GDP = GDP - Env.\ Costs$ | ₹ | Economic |
| 13 | HDI | $HDI = (H_E \cdot H_{LE} \cdot H_{LI})^{1/3}$ | 0–1 | Societal |
| 14 | Gini Coefficient | $G = \sum \vert x_i - x_j \vert / (2 n^2 \bar{x})$ | 0–1 | Societal |
| 15 | LTIFR | $LTIFR = (LTI \times 10^6)/Hours$ | per million hours | Societal |
| 16 | SROI | $SROI = PV\ Social\ Benefits / PV\ Investments$ | ratio | Societal |
| 17 | Green Revenue Ratio | $GRR = (Green\ Rev. / Total\ Rev.) \times 100$ | % | Business |
| 18 | Employee Turnover | $ETR = (Left / Avg) \times 100$ | % | Business |
| 19 | Compliance Rate | $CR = (Complied / Total) \times 100$ | % | Business |
| 20 | CSI | $CSI = \sum (S_i \times W_i) / \sum W_i$ | score | Business |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In manufacturing practice, these indicators are deployed in:

- **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA):** Carbon footprint, water footprint, energy intensity are direct LCA outputs.
- **Eco-design:** Material intensity, eco-efficiency guide material/process selection.
- **Corporate Sustainability Reporting (GRI, SASB):** Pulls from all four families.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** AQI, LTIFR, compliance rate are mandatory.
- **Investment Appraisal:** NPV, BCR, IRR (with carbon costs internalized) are the financial backbone.
- **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) Reporting:** Direct mapping of the four indicator families.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Indicator Classification Logic

Before derivations, let us establish the **analytical matrix** that connects indicator families to the **DPSIR causal chain** and **TBL pillars**. This is essential for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

| DPSIR Stage | Environmental Link | Economic Link | Societal Link | Business Link |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **D (Drivers)** | Industrial output growth | GDP growth | Population growth | Strategic expansion |
| **P (Pressures)** | CO₂ emissions, waste generation | Capital expenditure | Job demand | R&D investment |
| **S (State)** | Ambient air quality | Cost competitiveness | Employment rate | Market share |
| **I (Impacts)** | Climate change impacts | Profitability change | Inequality, well-being | Brand reputation |
| **R (Responses)** | Cleaner technology adoption | Carbon pricing | Social programs | ESG strategy |

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivations and Worked Examples

### 3.2.1 Carbon Footprint — Exhaustive Derivation

A small manufacturing unit operates 3 boilers. Boiler 1 uses 10,000 liters of diesel annually, Boiler 2 uses 50,000 m³ of natural gas, and Boiler 3 uses 20,000 kg of coal. **Compute the total carbon footprint in kg CO₂e.**

**Step 1: Identify the activity data, emission factors, and GWP values.**

| Source | Activity Data ($AD_i$) | Emission Factor ($EF_i$) | GWP ($GWP_i$) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Diesel | 10,000 L | 2.68 kg CO₂/L | 1 |
| Natural Gas | 50,000 m³ | 1.88 kg CO₂/m³ | 1 |
| Coal | 20,000 kg | 2.42 kg CO₂/kg | 1 |

> Note: GWP = 1 for CO₂; for methane it would be 28, for N₂O it would be 265 (AR5 values).

**Step 2: Apply the carbon footprint formula for each source.**

$$CF_i = AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i$$

**Step 3: Compute $CF_{diesel}$:**

$$CF_{diesel} = 10{,}000 \text{ L} \times 2.68 \frac{\text{kg CO}_2}{\text{L}} \times 1$$

$$CF_{diesel} = 26{,}800 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

**Step 4: Compute $CF_{natural\ gas}$:**

$$CF_{nat\ gas} = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \times 1.88 \frac{\text{kg CO}_2}{\text{m}^3} \times 1$$

$$CF_{nat\ gas} = 94{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

**Step 5: Compute $CF_{coal}$:**

$$CF_{coal} = 20{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 2.42 \frac{\text{kg CO}_2}{\text{kg}} \times 1$$

$$CF_{coal} = 48{,}400 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

**Step 6: Sum all contributions:**

$$CF_{total} = 26{,}800 + 94{,}000 + 48{,}400 = 169{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

$$CF_{total} = 169.2 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Coal contributes 28.6% of total emissions despite lower mass — its **emission factor is high (2.42)**. The natural gas source dominates (55.6%) because of the large volume. Mitigation should prioritize natural gas substitution (e.g., with biogas) and coal replacement (with biomass pellets).

---

### 3.2.2 AQI Sub-Index — Exhaustive Derivation

Given the following pollutant measurements and standard breakpoint tables (Indian NAQI):

| Pollutant | Concentration $C_p$ | $BP_{Lo}$ | $BP_{Hi}$ | $I_{Lo}$ | $I_{Hi}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| PM2.5 | 65 µg/m³ | 31 | 60 | 101 | 200 |
| PM10 | 120 µg/m³ | 101 | 250 | 101 | 200 |
| NO₂ | 90 µg/m³ | 81 | 180 | 101 | 200 |
| SO₂ | 50 µg/m³ | 81 | 160 | 51 | 100 |

**Find the AQI.**

**Step 1: Identify the formula:**

$$I_p = I_{Lo} + \frac{(I_{Hi} - I_{Lo})(C_p - BP_{Lo})}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}}$$

**Step 2: Compute $I_{PM2.5}$:**

$$I_{PM2.5} = 101 + \frac{(200 - 101)(65 - 31)}{60 - 31}$$

$$I_{PM2.5} = 101 + \frac{99 \times 34}{29}$$

$$I_{PM2.5} = 101 + \frac{3{,}366}{29} = 101 + 116.07 = 217.07$$

> Note: Since $C_p = 65$ exceeds $BP_{Hi} = 60$, the breakpoint is technically out of range. For this KTU-level problem, we use the highest available bracket (extrapolation) — students should write: *"Concentration exceeds the highest breakpoint; AQI is reported in the 'Hazardous' category (>300)."*

**Step 3: Compute $I_{PM10}$:**

$$I_{PM10} = 101 + \frac{(200 - 101)(120 - 101)}{250 - 101}$$

$$I_{PM10} = 101 + \frac{99 \times 19}{149} = 101 + 12.62 = 113.62$$

**Step 4: Compute $I_{NO_2}$:**

$$I_{NO_2} = 101 + \frac{(200 - 101)(90 - 81)}{180 - 81}$$

$$I_{NO_2} = 101 + \frac{99 \times 9}{99} = 101 + 9 = 110$$

**Step 5: Compute $I_{SO_2}$:**

$$I_{SO_2} = 51 + \frac{(100 - 51)(50 - 81)}{160 - 81}$$

$$I_{SO_2} = 51 + \frac{49 \times (-31)}{79} = 51 - 19.23 = 31.77$$

> Since $C_p = 50 < BP_{Lo} = 81$, the sub-index is below the lowest breakpoint. For the KTU exam, this means $I_{SO_2} \approx 31.77$ (Good category).

**Step 6: Determine overall AQI:**

$$AQI = \max(217.07,\ 113.62,\ 110,\ 31.77) = 217.07$$

$$AQI \approx 217 \quad (\text{Very Unhealthy Category})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The AQI is determined by the worst (highest) sub-index**, which is **PM2.5 = 217** — a Very Unhealthy category. This means the city's most acute air quality problem is particulate matter, not gaseous pollutants. Regulatory response should target PM2.5 sources (e.g., diesel generators, construction dust, industrial stacks).

---

### 3.2.3 HDI — Exhaustive Computation

For a hypothetical country:
- Life expectancy at birth: **72 years**
- Expected years of schooling: **14 years**
- Mean years of schooling: **10 years**
- GNI per capita: **\$18,000 (PPP)**
- Goal/targets: Life expectancy min=20, max=85; Schooling min=0, max=18, mean=15; GNI min=\$100, max=\$75,000

**Step 1: Compute the Life Expectancy Index $H_{LE}$:**

$$H_{LE} = \frac{LE - 20}{85 - 20} = \frac{72 - 20}{65} = \frac{52}{65} = 0.800$$

**Step 2: Compute the Education Index $H_E$:**

The Education Index is the mean of two sub-indices:

**Expected Years Index:**
$$EYS_i = \frac{14 - 0}{18 - 0} = \frac{14}{18} = 0.778$$

**Mean Years Index:**
$$MYS_i = \frac{10 - 0}{15 - 0} = \frac{10}{15} = 0.667$$

**Combined:**
$$H_E = \frac{0.778 + 0.667}{2} = \frac{1.445}{2} = 0.7225$$

**Step 3: Compute the Income Index $H_{LI}$:**

$$H_{LI} = \frac{\ln(GNI) - \ln(100)}{\ln(75{,}000) - \ln(100)}$$

$$H_{LI} = \frac{\ln(18{,}000) - \ln(100)}{\ln(75{,}000) - \ln(100)}$$

$$H_{LI} = \frac{9.798 - 4.605}{11.226 - 4.605} = \frac{5.193}{6.621} = 0.7843$$

**Step 4: Compute HDI:**

$$HDI = \sqrt[3]{H_E \times H_{LE} \times H_{LI}}$$

$$HDI = \sqrt[3]{0.7225 \times 0.800 \times 0.7843}$$

$$HDI = \sqrt[3]{0.4533} = 0.769$$

**Interpretation:** $HDI = 0.769$ falls in the **"High Human Development"** category (0.70–0.80) on the standard UNDP scale.

---

### 3.2.4 NPV — Exhaustive Computation

A factory invests ₹50 lakh in a clean-energy retrofit. Estimated annual savings (energy + emission fines avoided) = ₹12 lakh/year for 6 years. Discount rate = **10%**.

**Step 1: Write the NPV equation:**

$$NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 2: Compute the annuity factor (since savings are constant ₹12 L/year):**

$$AF = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10}$$

$$(1.10)^{-6} = \frac{1}{1.7716} = 0.5645$$

$$AF = \frac{1 - 0.5645}{0.10} = \frac{0.4355}{0.10} = 4.355$$

**Step 3: Compute present value of savings:**

$$PV_{savings} = 12 \text{ lakh} \times 4.355 = 52.26 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 4: Compute NPV:**

$$NPV = -50 + 52.26 = 2.26 \text{ lakh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision:** Since $NPV = +₹2.26 \text{ lakh} > 0$, the clean-energy retrofit is **financially viable** at a 10% discount rate. The BCR = 52.26 / 50 = **1.045**, confirming the same conclusion.

---

| **Eco-Efficiency** | CO₂, water, waste | Sales, profit | Production strategy |
| **Green GDP** | Resource depletion, pollution costs | National economic policy | Macro policy |
| **SROI** | Community impact | Brand value | CSR planning |
| **ESG Score** | E + S + G pillars | Investment decisions | Capital allocation |
