# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define sustainability indicators. List the four major categories of sustainability indicators as per the KTU 2024 syllabus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Sustainability indicators are measurable variables (quantitative or qualitative) that provide a basis for evaluating the performance of a manufacturing system against environmental, economic, societal, and business dimensions. They simplify complex data and track progress toward sustainability goals.

**Four Categories (1.5 marks):**
1. **Environmental Indicators** — measure resource use and emissions (e.g., carbon footprint).
2. **Economic Indicators** — measure financial viability (e.g., NPV, ROI).
3. **Societal Indicators** — measure social well-being (e.g., HDI, LTIFR).
4. **Business Indicators** — measure firm-level strategic performance (e.g., Green Revenue Ratio).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between eco-efficiency and eco-effectiveness. Give one example indicator for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Eco-Efficiency | Eco-Effectiveness |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Ratio of economic value added to environmental impact added — doing more with less | Absolute reduction of environmental impact regardless of output — doing the right things |
| **Philosophy** | "Less bad" — incremental improvement | "Good" — regenerative and restorative |
| **Example Indicator** | Energy per unit product (MJ/unit) | Cradle-to-cradle material loop closure rate |
| **Marks Allocated** | 2 marks (definition + concept) | 1 mark (example) |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** *Each Part B question in the module section carries 14 marks with internal choice. Students must attempt either Question A OR Question B. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **PART B – Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) Explain the DPSIR framework in detail. For each stage, give one specific indicator (environmental, economic, societal, or business) used in manufacturing sustainability assessment. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The DPSIR framework, developed by the European Environment Agency (EEA), is a causal chain that links the causes (drivers) to consequences (impacts) and policy responses. It has five sequential stages: **D**rivers → **P**ressures → **S**tate → **I**mpacts → **R**esponses.

**Detailed Stages (4 marks):**

1. **Drivers:** Socio-economic forces driving environmental change.
   - *Example indicator:* Industrial production index (Business) or population growth rate (Societal).
2. **Pressures:** Direct human-induced effects on the environment.
   - *Example indicator:* CO₂ emissions in tonnes (Environmental) or capital expenditure on polluting equipment (Economic).
3. **State:** The physical, chemical, and biological condition of the environment/society.
   - *Example indicator:* Ambient PM2.5 concentration (Environmental) or employment rate (Societal).
4. **Impacts:** Consequences of state changes.
   - *Example indicator:* Public health cost due to respiratory illness (Societal) or brand reputation loss (Business).
5. **Responses:** Policy, technology, and management actions.
   - *Example indicator:* Renewable energy investment as % of revenue (Business) or compliance rate (Environmental).

**Linkage Diagram (1.5 marks):**

> The DPSIR logic in manufacturing: Rising production demand (D) → Higher CO₂ emissions (P) → Worsened air quality (S) → Public health impact (I) → Adoption of clean technology (R). The R then loops back to influence future Drivers.

**(b) A manufacturing firm produces 50,000 units/year. Its annual energy consumption is 12,500 GJ, water consumption is 80,000 m³, and total CO₂ emissions are 2,500 tonnes. The firm generates ₹25 crore in revenue. Compute and interpret the energy intensity, water intensity, and eco-efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Output $Q = 50{,}000$ units/year
- Energy = 12,500 GJ = 12,500,000 MJ
- Water = 80,000 m³
- CO₂ = 2,500 tonnes = 2,500,000 kg
- Revenue = ₹25,00,00,000 (₹25 crore)

**Step 1: Energy Intensity (1.5 marks):**

$$EI = \frac{12{,}500{,}000\ \text{MJ}}{50{,}000\ \text{units}} = 250\ \text{MJ/unit}$$

**Step 2: Water Intensity (1.5 marks):**

$$WI = \frac{80{,}000\ \text{m}^3}{50{,}000\ \text{units}} = 1.6\ \text{m}^3/\text{unit}$$

**Step 3: Carbon Intensity (1.5 marks):**

$$CI = \frac{2{,}500{,}000\ \text{kg}}{50{,}000\ \text{units}} = 50\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/unit}$$

**Step 4: Eco-Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$EE = \frac{\text{Value Added}}{\text{Environmental Impact}} = \frac{₹25{,}00{,}00{,}000}{2{,}500{,}000\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e}}$$

$$EE = ₹100\ \text{per kg CO}_2\text{e}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (1 mark):**

- Energy intensity of **250 MJ/unit** is high — typical for medium-complexity metal products (benchmark: 50–150 MJ/unit for light manufacturing).
- Water intensity of **1.6 m³/unit** is moderate.
- Eco-efficiency of **₹100/kg CO₂e** means each kg of CO₂ emitted generates ₹100 of revenue — a metric the firm can use to benchmark against cleaner competitors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Always include **units** in the final answer — students frequently lose 1 mark for omitting MJ/unit or ₹/kg.
> 2. Do **not** confuse eco-efficiency with eco-effectiveness. Eco-efficiency is value added per impact added, not absolute reduction.
> 3. Note that 1 GJ = 1000 MJ. Many students fail to convert and write 12,500 MJ instead of 12,500,000 MJ.

---

### **PART B – Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) What is the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework? With the help of a suitable diagram, explain how business indicators extend the TBL into a four-pillar sustainability model. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**TBL Definition (1.5 marks):** The Triple Bottom Line, coined by **John Elkington in 1994**, is a sustainability framework that evaluates organizational performance on three dimensions: **People (Social)**, **Planet (Environmental)**, and **Profit (Economic)**. It challenges the traditional single-bottom-line focus on profit.

**Three Pillars Explained (3 marks):**
1. **Environmental (Planet):** Resource use, emissions, ecosystem health — measured by indicators like carbon footprint, water footprint, energy intensity.
2. **Social (People):** Labor practices, community impact, human rights — measured by HDI, LTIFR, Gini coefficient.
3. **Economic (Profit):** Profitability, cost-efficiency, value creation — measured by NPV, ROI, BCR.

**Extension to Four Pillars with Business Indicators (2.5 marks):** In modern sustainable manufacturing, a **fourth pillar — Business Performance** — is integrated with the TBL. This pillar includes firm-level strategic metrics such as:
- Green Revenue Ratio (GRR)
- Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)
- Compliance Rate
- Market Share Growth

**Justification:** Business indicators act as the **integration layer** that links all three TBL dimensions into actionable firm-level strategy. For example, eco-efficiency (a business indicator) requires environmental (CO₂), economic (revenue), and operational (process) data — making it a true synthesis metric.

**Diagram (sketch expected):** A Venn diagram of three overlapping circles (Planet, People, Profit) with a fourth overlapping circle (Business) at the center or as a wrapper.

**(b) An automotive component manufacturer reports the following data for one year: (i) Workforce size = 500, (ii) Lost-time injuries = 8, (iii) Total hours worked = 1.2 million, (iv) Gini coefficient of employee wages = 0.32, (v) Employee turnover = 12%. Compute and interpret the LTIFR and discuss the meaning of the Gini coefficient. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: LTIFR Computation (3 marks):**

$$LTIFR = \frac{Number\ of\ Lost\ Time\ Injuries \times 10^6}{Total\ Hours\ Worked}$$

$$LTIFR = \frac{8 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000}$$

$$LTIFR = \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} = 6.67\ \text{injuries per million man-hours}$$

**Interpretation (1.5 marks):** The firm's LTIFR of 6.67 is **higher than the global automotive industry benchmark of 2–3**. This signals a need for safety management improvements, additional operator training, and engineering controls.

**Step 2: Gini Coefficient Interpretation (2.5 marks):**

- $G = 0.32$ is on a scale of 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality).
- A value of **0.32 indicates moderate wage inequality** — most workers earn near the mean wage, but a small group of high earners (managers/specialists) pulls the Gini upward.
- International benchmarks: Scandinavian countries ~0.25–0.30; India ~0.40; South Africa ~0.60.
- For the firm: A Gini of 0.32 is acceptable but could be improved via transparent pay scales and reduced pay gaps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **The $10^6$ multiplier is mandatory** in LTIFR formula — students often forget and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Gini coefficient has no units** — students who write "0.32 units" lose marks.
> 3. The interpretation of the Gini value must reference the 0–1 scale explicitly.
> 4. Don't confuse **LTIFR** with **TRIR** (Total Recordable Injury Rate) — TRIR includes fatalities, lost-time, restricted-duty, and medical-aid cases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU Module 4 Exam**

### **Environmental Indicators**
- Carbon Footprint = $\sum AD_i \cdot EF_i \cdot GWP_i$ → answer in **kg CO₂e**.
- Water Footprint = **blue + green + grey** components.
- Energy Intensity (MJ/unit) ↓ → manufacturing efficiency ↑.
- AQI = **maximum** sub-index; AQI sub-index formula requires linear interpolation within breakpoints.
- Waste Recycling Rate (WRR) expressed as **percentage** — formula requires ×100%.

### **Economic Indicators**
- NPV **≥ 0** → accept; **NPV < 0** → reject.
- BCR **≥ 1** → accept; IRR **≥ hurdle rate** → accept.
- Green GDP = GDP − environmental degradation cost − resource depletion cost.
- Eco-Efficiency is the **bridge indicator** connecting environmental and economic families.

### **Societal Indicators**
- HDI = $\sqrt[3]{H_E \cdot H_{LE} \cdot H_{LI}}$; value 0 to 1.
- Gini = 0 (perfect equality) → 1 (perfect inequality).
- LTIFR = (LTI × $10^6$) / Total hours; the $10^6$ is **non-negotiable**.
- SROI = Present value of social benefits ÷ Present value of social investments.

### **Business Indicators**
- Green Revenue Ratio (GRR) is the **percentage of revenue from sustainable/eco-friendly products**.
- CSI = $\sum (S_i \times W_i) / \sum W_i$ — weighted score.
- Compliance Rate = (Complied / Total applicable) × 100%.

### **Framework Links**
- **DPSIR:** Drivers → Pressures → State → Impacts → Responses.
- **TBL:** People + Planet + Profit (+ Business in KTU 2024).
- **Eco-efficiency** is the most cross-cutting indicator — appears in environmental, economic, and business families.

### **Frequently Asked Conceptual Pairings**
- Carbon footprint ↔ DPSIR Pressure position.
- Air quality ↔ DPSIR State position.
- Clean tech investment ↔ DPSIR Response position.
- Eco-efficiency ↔ TBL intersection of all three pillars.

### **Common Calculation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here)**
- Forgetting unit conversion (GJ → MJ, ₹ lakh → ₹ crore).
- Omitting the $10^6$ multiplier in LTIFR.
- Using absolute value `|x|` in markdown tables — use `\vert x \vert` instead.
- Not writing units in the final answer.
- Confusing eco-efficiency with eco-effectiveness.

### **High-Frequency "Define & List" Type Questions**
- Define *Triple Bottom Line* — always attribute to **Elkington (1994)**.
- Define *DPSIR* — always attribute to **European Environment Agency (EEA)**.
- List *types of water footprint* — blue, green, grey (Hoekstra et al.).
- List *four indicator families* — environmental, economic, societal, business.

### **Golden Rule for 14-Mark Questions**
Always structure your answer as: **(i) Definition → (ii) Classification/Components → (iii) Formula/Methodology → (iv) Numerical Example (if asked) → (v) Interpretation/Engineering Significance**. This is the **exact KTU Board-Examiner rubric** and secures full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept Models and Various Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept Models and Various Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Concept Models in Sustainable Manufacturing

In the context of **Sustainable Manufacturing**, a **Concept Model** is a structured, abstract, and systematic representation of the philosophical, strategic, and operational principles that govern the integration of environmental, economic, and social dimensions into manufacturing systems. Concept models serve as **roadmaps** that translate the broad vision of *sustainability* into actionable engineering, management, and policy frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *Concept models are theoretical constructs that describe how manufacturing enterprises can conceptualize, plan, and execute production activities while preserving environmental integrity, ensuring economic viability, and upholding social responsibility throughout the product life cycle.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **house**. Before laying a single brick, an architect draws a **blueprint**. The blueprint does not contain the actual bricks, cement, or wood — it is a *conceptual model* of the house. It shows you the layout, the flow of rooms, the placement of windows, and how rainwater will drain.

In the same way, **Concept Models in Sustainable Manufacturing** are the "architectural blueprints" for designing factories, products, and supply chains that are **eco-friendly**, **profitable**, and **socially fair**. Without these blueprints, a factory may produce a product that is cheap but pollutes rivers, or a green product that no one can afford. Concept models balance these trade-offs intelligently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars (Triple Bottom Line – TBL)** that every concept model in sustainable manufacturing tries to balance:
> 1. **Planet** (Environmental Integrity)
> 2. **Profit** (Economic Viability)
> 3. **People** (Social Equity)

### 1.3 Why Concept Models Matter in Engineering Practice

- They convert **abstract sustainability goals** into **measurable engineering parameters** (e.g., carbon footprint in kg CO₂-eq, water usage in m³, energy efficiency in J/unit).
- They help manufacturers **standardize reporting** against global frameworks such as the **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**, **ISO 14040/14044 (LCA)**, and **ISO 50001 (Energy Management)**.
- They provide a **common language** for engineers, managers, policymakers, and society.
- They guide the **selection of tools** such as Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), Design for Environment (DfE), and Cleaner Production.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example:** A car manufacturer like **Toyota** uses the concept model of the **5R framework (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Redesign)** to design hybrid vehicles. Each "R" is a strategic decision point that engineers apply during design, production, use, and end-of-life phases.

### 1.4 Classification of Concept Models (Preview)

Concept models can be broadly classified into three families:

| Family | Core Philosophy | Typical Tools Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Economy Extensions** | "Reduce harm" at end-of-pipe | Pollution control, waste treatment |
| **Circular Economy Models** | "Eliminate waste by design" | Cradle-to-Cradle, Industrial Symbiosis, Remanufacturing |
| **Regenerative / Systems Models** | "Restore and regenerate ecosystems" | Biomimicry, Industrial Ecology, Natural Step |

The various **approaches** — such as *Lean Manufacturing*, *Green Manufacturing*, *Agile Manufacturing*, and *Cloud Manufacturing* — are essentially **operational philosophies** that get *combined* with these concept models to deliver sustainable outcomes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** *Lean Manufacturing* and *Green Manufacturing* are NOT the same. Lean focuses on **waste (muda) reduction** for cost and time savings; Green focuses on **environmental impact reduction**. Modern sustainable manufacturing integrates **both** as **Lean-Green Manufacturing**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Concept Models in Sustainable Manufacturing

Below is an exhaustive analytical breakdown of the most important concept models tested in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.

### 2.1.1 The 6R Concept Model

The **6R framework** is the most direct, engineering-actionable concept model in sustainable manufacturing. Each "R" represents a strategic intervention point along the product life cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 6Rs of Sustainable Manufacturing:**
> 1. **Reduce** — Minimize resource use (raw materials, energy, water) during production.
> 2. **Reuse** — Use a product/component again for the same or different purpose without major processing.
> 3. **Recycle** — Reprocess waste materials into new raw materials or products.
> 4. **Recover** — Extract value (energy or materials) from waste that cannot be recycled (e.g., waste-to-energy incineration).
> 5. **Redesign** — Modify product/process design to reduce environmental impact at source (DfE).
> 6. **Remanufacture** — Disassemble, inspect, refurbish, and reassemble used products to "as-new" condition.

### 2.1.2 The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — Elkington's Model (1994)

Proposed by **John Elkington**, the TBL model posits that a sustainable enterprise must perform well on three "bottom lines":

$$
\text{Sustainability} = f(\text{Planet}, \text{Profit}, \text{People})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** TBL is the *foundational* concept model. Every other approach (circular economy, industrial ecology, etc.) is essentially a *specialized operationalization* of the TBL philosophy.

### 2.1.3 Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C) Model

Developed by **Michael Braungart** and **William McDonough** (2002), the C2C model envisions two continuous material loops:

- **Biological Cycle** — Materials re-enter the biosphere as nutrients (e.g., biodegradable packaging).
- **Technical Cycle** — Materials re-enter manufacturing as high-quality inputs (e.g., metals, polymers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** *Waste = Food*. There is no "waste" in C2C; every output of one process is an input to another.

### 2.1.4 Industrial Ecology (IE)

Industrial Ecology treats industrial systems as **analogous to natural ecosystems** where the waste of one industry becomes the raw material of another. The classic example is the **Kalundborg Symbiosis** in Denmark, where a power plant's waste heat is used to warm a fish farm.

### 2.1.5 The Natural Step (TNS) Framework

Developed by **Karl-Henrik Robèrt**, TNS is based on four **System Conditions** for a sustainable society:

1. Nature's functions and diversity are not systematically increased.
2. Substances from the Earth's crust are not systematically increased.
3. Substances produced by society are not systematically increased.
4. People are not subject to structural barriers to participation.

### 2.1.6 Eco-Efficiency vs. Eco-Effectiveness

| Aspect | Eco-Efficiency | Eco-Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Reduce environmental impact per unit of output | Create positive environmental impact |
| **Philosophy** | "Doing more with less" | "Doing the right things" |
| **Metric** | Impact/output ratio | Absolute ecosystem restoration |
| **Origin** | WBCSD, 1992 | Braungart & McDonough, 2002 |

### 2.1.7 The Factor 4 and Factor 10 Approaches

- **Factor 4** — Quadruple resource productivity (proposed by the Club of Rome, 1997).
- **Factor 10** — Tenfold improvement in resource efficiency (factor-10 club, 1990s, 10 years in 10 years).
- These are **quantitative targets** used to operationalize sustainable manufacturing.

## 2.2 Various Approaches to Sustainable Manufacturing

Approaches are **operational philosophies** that, when combined with concept models, deliver sustainable outcomes.

### 2.2.1 Green Manufacturing (GM)

A manufacturing philosophy that emphasizes **minimizing environmental impact** across all stages of production. Key techniques include:
- Use of renewable energy sources.
- Substitution of hazardous materials.
- Adoption of cleaner production technologies.

### 2.2.2 Lean Manufacturing (LM)

Originally from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, lean focuses on **eliminating 8 wastes (mudas)**: defects, overproduction, waiting, transport, inventory, motion, over-processing, and unused talent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lean-Green Integration:** When lean and green are combined, the **7 wastes + environmental wastes** are simultaneously reduced. The integrated framework is called **Lean-Green Manufacturing**.

### 2.2.3 Agile Manufacturing (AM)

A system capable of **rapid response** to market changes. When aligned with sustainability, agile manufacturing supports **mass customization** with minimal overproduction — directly addressing one of the largest sources of waste.

### 2.2.4 Cloud Manufacturing (CM)

A service-oriented paradigm where **manufacturing resources** (machines, software, skills) are pooled and accessed over the internet "as a service." Cloud manufacturing can reduce **idle capacity** and **resource redundancy**, supporting sustainability.

### 2.2.5 Smart Manufacturing (SM) / Industry 4.0

Integration of **IoT, AI, Big Data, and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** to enable self-optimizing factories. Smart manufacturing enables:
- **Predictive maintenance** (reducing waste from breakdowns).
- **Real-time energy monitoring**.
- **Closed-loop control** of emissions and waste.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the key quantitative metrics, indicators, and target ratios used in concept models.

| # | Concept / Metric | Formula / Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carbon Footprint (CF)** | $CF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (A_i \times EF_i)$ | kg CO₂-eq | Climate impact assessment |
| 2 | **Eco-Efficiency (EE)** | $EE = \dfrac{\text{Product Value}}{\text{Environmental Impact}}$ | Value/Impact | Resource productivity |
| 3 | **Material Efficiency (ME)** | $ME = \dfrac{m_{useful}}{m_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Waste reduction |
| 4 | **Energy Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{output}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Energy auditing |
| 5 | **Recycling Rate (RR)** | $RR = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{total\,waste}} \times 100$ | % | Circularity measurement |
| 6 | **Water Footprint (WF)** | $WF = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$ | m³/unit | Water management |
| 7 | **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED)** | $CED = \sum E_i$ (over life cycle) | MJ | LCA energy analysis |
| 8 | **Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)** | $MCI = 1 - \dfrac{F_{W} + F_{M}}{2}$ (Linder et al., 2017) | 0 to 1 | Circular economy assessment |
| 9 | **Process Mass Intensity (PMI)** | $PMI = \dfrac{m_{total\,in}}{m_{product}}$ | kg/kg | Pharma/chem processes |
| 10 | **E-Factor (Environmental Factor)** | $E = \dfrac{m_{waste}}{m_{product}}$ | kg/kg | Green chemistry |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Always express any sustainability metric with both its **numerical value** and **unit**. A bare number is considered an incomplete answer in KTU valuation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Product Design:** Use the **6R framework** to guide Design for Environment (DfE) decisions.
- **Factory Layout:** Apply **Industrial Ecology** to create eco-industrial parks (e.g., Kalundborg, Denmark).
- **Supply Chain:** Use **Cradle-to-Cradle** to design closed-loop supply chains.
- **Energy Systems:** Use **Factor 10** targets in policy and ISO 50001 audits.
- **Chemical Industries:** Use **PMI and E-Factor** to rank process greenness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Eco-Efficiency Indicator from First Principles

Eco-Efficiency is the cornerstone quantitative concept in sustainable manufacturing. The following is a complete step-by-step derivation suitable for a 7-mark KTU sub-question.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Functional Unit

In any manufacturing process, the *functional unit* is a quantified performance of a product system. Let the functional unit be the production of $N$ units of a product over a time period $T$.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define Product Value (V)

The product value is the **economic worth created** by the manufacturing process. It can be quantified as:

$$
V = N \times P_{unit}
$$

where $P_{unit}$ is the price per unit. In broader TBL terms, $V$ can also include social value, but for engineering valuation:

$$
V = \text{Sales Revenue} - \text{Total Operating Cost}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Define Environmental Impact (I)

The environmental impact is the total resource depletion and emissions generated. It can be aggregated as:

$$
I = \alpha \cdot R_{mat} + \beta \cdot E_{use} + \gamma \cdot E_{em}
$$

where:
- $R_{mat}$ = total raw material consumed (kg)
- $E_{use}$ = total energy used (MJ)
- $E_{em}$ = total emissions (kg CO₂-eq)
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weighting factors (depending on LCA methodology)

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Formulate Eco-Efficiency

By the **WBCSD (World Business Council for Sustainable Development, 1992)** definition:

$$
EE = \frac{V}{I}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Express in Functional Units

Substituting the expressions for $V$ and $I$:

$$
EE = \frac{N \times P_{unit}}{\alpha \cdot R_{mat} + \beta \cdot E_{use} + \gamma \cdot E_{em}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Define Specific Eco-Efficiency (per unit product)

Dividing both numerator and denominator by $N$:

$$
EE_{specific} = \frac{P_{unit}}{\alpha \cdot r_{mat} + \beta \cdot e_{use} + \gamma \cdot e_{em}}
$$

where the lowercase letters denote per-unit values.

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Interpretation

$$
\text{If } EE_{after} > EE_{before} \Rightarrow \text{Improvement in sustainability}
$$

The greater the eco-efficiency, the more **value generated per unit of environmental burden**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A manufacturing unit produces 10,000 units of a product. The selling price is ₹500 per unit. Total material consumed is 2,000 kg; total energy consumed is 50,000 MJ; total emissions are 1,500 kg CO₂-eq. Given $\alpha = 0.5, \beta = 0.2, \gamma = 0.3$, compute the eco-efficiency.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Calculate Product Value:

$$
V = 10000 \times 500 = 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

Step 2 — Calculate aggregated environmental impact:

$$
I = (0.5 \times 2000) + (0.2 \times 50000) + (0.3 \times 1500)
$$

$$
I = 1000 + 10000 + 450 = 11450
$$

Step 3 — Compute Eco-Efficiency:

$$
EE = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{11450} \approx 436.68 \text{ ₹ per unit impact}
$$

Step 4 — Per-unit eco-efficiency:

$$
EE_{specific} = \frac{500}{1.145} \approx 436.68 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always show the units at every intermediate step. The KTU examiner allocates **1 mark** for correct final units in derivation problems.

## 3.3 Derivation: Material Circularity Indicator (MCI)

The **MCI** (Linder et al., 2017) measures how restorative a company's material flows are. The derivation proceeds as follows.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Define Material Flows

- $F_{in}$ = total input of virgin material
- $F_{out}$ = total output to landfill/incineration
- $F_{recycled}$ = recycled/reused material flow
- $W$ = total weight of product

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Define Linear Flow Inefficiency ($F_{L}$)

$$
F_{L} = \frac{F_{in} + F_{out} - 2 \cdot F_{recycled}}{2 \cdot W}
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Define the MCI

$$
MCI = 1 - F_{L}
$$

This gives:

$$
MCI = 1 - \left( \frac{F_{in} + F_{out} - 2 \cdot F_{recycled}}{2 \cdot W} \right)
$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Boundary Conditions

$$
0 \leq MCI \leq 1
$$

- $MCI = 0 \Rightarrow$ Fully linear (no circularity)
- $MCI = 1 \Rightarrow$ Fully circular (perfect closed loop)

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

The following matrix compares the major concept models across multiple criteria — a frequently tested KTU analytical question.

| Criterion | 6R Model | TBL (Elkington) | C2C (Braungart) | Industrial Ecology | TNS (Robèrt) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Engineering practice | Business strategy | Architectural philosophy | Ecology analogy | Science-based |
| **Focus** | Operational tactics | Strategic balance | Material cycles | Inter-firm flows | System conditions |
| **Scope** | Process level | Enterprise level | Product/Industry level | Industrial park level | Societal level |
| **Quantitative?** | Yes (rates) | Partial | No | Partial (mass balance) | Yes (4 conditions) |
| **Time Horizon** | Short–Mid | Mid–Long | Long | Long | Long |
| **Best Used For** | Shop-floor actions | Corporate reporting | Product design | Eco-industrial parks | Policy-making |
| **Limitation** | Silo-thinking | Difficult to quantify | Hard to implement | Site-specific | Abstract for engineers |

## 3.5 Decision Logic for Selecting an Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Tree for Approach Selection:**
>
> 1. **Is the goal to reduce waste in-house?** → Use **6R + Green Manufacturing**
> 2. **Is the goal to redesign the product for circularity?** → Use **Cradle-to-Cradle + DfE**
> 3. **Is the goal to balance profit, planet, people in reporting?** → Use **TBL + ISO 14040 LCA**
> 4. **Is the goal to enable rapid response with low inventory?** → Use **Lean + Agile integration**
> 5. **Is the goal to industrialize a region with shared resources?** → Use **Industrial Ecology + Symbiosis**
> 6. **Is the goal to align with national/international policy?** → Use **TNS + UN SDGs + Factor 10**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Hierarchy of Concept Models and Approaches

This block-level architecture shows the **top-down hierarchy** from the abstract concept of *Sustainability* down to the operational shop-floor approaches.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SUSTAINABILITY VISION"] --> nodeB["TRIPLE BOTTOM LINE"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["CIRCULAR ECONOMY PARADIGM"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["PLANET PILLAR"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["PROFIT PILLAR"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["PEOPLE PILLAR"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["CRADLE TO CRADLE"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["INDUSTRIAL ECOLOGY"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["6R CONCEPT MODEL"]
    nodeD --> nodeJ["GREEN MANUFACTURING"]
    nodeD --> nodeK["CLEANER PRODUCTION"]
    nodeE --> nodeL["LEAN MANUFACTURING"]
    nodeE --> nodeM["AGILE MANUFACTURING"]
    nodeF --> nodeN["SOCIAL COMPLIANCE"]
    nodeF --> nodeO["WORKER SAFETY"]
    nodeI --> nodeP["REDESIGN AND REMANUFACTURE"]
    nodeI --> nodeQ["RECYCLE AND RECOVER"]
    nodeJ --> nodeR["RENEWABLE ENERGY USE"]
    nodeK --> nodeS["HAZARDOUS MATERIAL SUBSTITUTION"]
    nodeL --> nodeT["WASTE ELIMINATION MUDAS"]
    nodeM --> nodeU["MASS CUSTOMIZATION"]
    nodeG --> nodeV["BIOLOGICAL CYCLE LOOP"]
    nodeG --> nodeW["TECHNICAL CYCLE LOOP"]
    nodeH --> nodeX["KALUNDBORG SYMBIOSIS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The topmost node is the broad vision (Sustainability). Each subsequent level refines the concept into a more specific, actionable framework. The terminal leaves (e.g., Mass Customization, Renewable Energy Use) represent **operational practices** implemented at the shop-floor level.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: The 6R Concept Model in the Product Life Cycle

This block diagram maps the **6 Rs** across the product life cycle stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["RAW MATERIAL EXTRACTION"] --> nodeB["DESIGN AND REDESIGN"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MANUFACTURING"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["DISTRIBUTION"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["USE PHASE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["END OF LIFE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["REUSE DIRECTLY"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["REMANUFACTURE"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["RECYCLE"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["RECOVER ENERGY"]
    nodeF --> nodeK["DISPOSAL LANDFILL"]
    nodeB -. "REDESIGN ACTION" .-> nodeL["ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT REDUCTION"]
    nodeC -. "REDUCE ACTION" .-> nodeL
    nodeG -. "REUSE LOOP" .-> nodeE
    nodeH -. "REMANUFACTURE LOOP" .-> nodeB
    nodeI -. "MATERIAL LOOP" .-> nodeA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the Diagram:** The **6 Rs** are not sequential — they are **intervention strategies** that can be applied at any stage of the product life cycle. REDESIGN happens at the design stage, REDUCE happens during manufacturing, REUSE/RECYCLE/RECOVER happen at end-of-life, and REMANUFACTURE bridges the end-of-life back to the design stage.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Integration of Lean-Green Manufacturing

This schematic illustrates the **synergistic integration** of Lean and Green philosophies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["LEAN MANUFACTURING FOCUS"] --> nodeB["ELIMINATE 8 MUDAS"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT KAIZEN"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["JUST IN TIME PRODUCTION"]
    nodeD["GREEN MANUFACTURING FOCUS"] --> nodeE["REDUCE EMISSIONS"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["RENEWABLE ENERGY"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["CLEANER TECHNOLOGIES"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["LEAN GREEN INTEGRATED SYSTEM"]
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["ZERO WASTE ZERO DEFECT"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["OPTIMIZED RESOURCE PRODUCTIVITY"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["ENHANCED COMPETITIVENESS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram is critical for KTU:** Questions on "Lean-Green integration" are **frequently asked** as 7-mark analytical questions. Drawing this schematic clearly is worth 2–3 marks in valuation.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: TBL Reporting Architecture

This block diagram shows how the **Triple Bottom Line** is operationalized in a manufacturing firm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["MANUFACTURING ENTERPRISE"] --> nodeB["TRIPLE BOTTOM LINE REPORTING"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ENVIRONMENTAL ACCOUNTING"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["ECONOMIC ACCOUNTING"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["SOCIAL ACCOUNTING"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["CARBON FOOTPRINT"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["WATER FOOTPRINT"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["WASTE GENERATION"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["REVENUE AND PROFIT"]
    nodeD --> nodeJ["OPERATIONAL COSTS"]
    nodeD --> nodeK["ROI ON GREEN INVESTMENT"]
    nodeE --> nodeL["EMPLOYEE WELFARE"]
    nodeE --> nodeM["COMMUNITY IMPACT"]
    nodeE --> nodeN["SUPPLY CHAIN ETHICS"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked to "explain TBL with a suitable diagram", the student must include **all three dimensions with at least two sub-indicators each**. A diagram with only the three pillars but no sub-indicators is considered incomplete and loses marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) concept model in sustainable manufacturing. List its three dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** is a concept model developed by **John Elkington (1994)** that measures an organization's sustainability performance along three interconnected dimensions:

1. **Planet (Environmental)** — Minimizing ecological footprint, reducing emissions, conserving resources.
2. **Profit (Economic)** — Maintaining financial viability and creating economic value.
3. **People (Social)** — Ensuring social equity, worker welfare, and community development.

A sustainable enterprise must perform well on **all three** simultaneously. TBL is widely used in corporate sustainability reporting and is the foundation of the modern ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) framework.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Briefly explain the "Cradle-to-Cradle" concept model. What is its key principle?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** model, proposed by **Michael Braungart and William McDonough (2002)**, is a concept model that envisions a **closed-loop material flow** with no waste. Its key principle is **"Waste = Food"** — every output of a process becomes a nutrient input for either:

- The **Biological Cycle** (biodegradable materials re-enter the biosphere safely), or
- The **Technical Cycle** (synthetic materials re-enter industrial manufacturing as high-quality inputs).

C2C contrasts with the traditional **Cradle-to-Grave** linear model, and is foundational to the **circular economy** philosophy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **6R concept model** of sustainable manufacturing. Discuss each of the 6 Rs with a suitable industrial example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat schematic diagram, describe the **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C) concept model**. Compare it with the **Cradle-to-Grave** model. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **6R concept model** is a practical, engineering-oriented framework that guides sustainable interventions across the product life cycle. The six Rs are:

1. **REDUCE** — Minimizing the consumption of raw materials, energy, and water at the source. *Example:* A textile mill adopting low-liquor dyeing technology that cuts water use by 50%.

2. **REUSE** — Using a product or component again, either for the same purpose or a new one, without significant reprocessing. *Example:* Refilling glass beverage bottles multiple times.

3. **RECYCLE** — Converting waste materials into new raw materials or products. *Example:* Melting aluminum scrap to produce new cans, saving ~95% energy vs. primary production.

4. **RECOVER** — Extracting value (typically energy) from waste that cannot be recycled. *Example:* Municipal solid waste incineration with energy recovery in waste-to-energy plants.

5. **REDESIGN** — Modifying product/process design to reduce environmental impact at source (Design for Environment, DfE). *Example:* Redesigning a refrigerator to use hydrocarbon refrigerants with low GWP.

6. **REMANUFACTURE** — Disassembling, cleaning, inspecting, repairing, and reassembling a used product to "as-new" condition. *Example:* Caterpillar's remanufactured diesel engines with warranty equal to new ones.

> [!TIP]
> **Order of Preference in the 6R Hierarchy:** REDESIGN > REDUCE > REUSE > REMANUFACTURE > RECYCLE > RECOVER. Higher-order Rs are more sustainable as they preserve more embedded value.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** model, developed by **Braungart and McDonough (2002)**, is illustrated schematically as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["BIOLOGICAL CYCLE"] --> nodeB["BIODEGRADABLE MATERIALS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["BIOSPHERE NUTRIENTS"]
    nodeC -. "REGROW" .-> nodeA
    nodeD["TECHNICAL CYCLE"] --> nodeE["DURABLE SYNTHETIC MATERIALS"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["REMANUFACTURE AND RECYCLE"]
    nodeF -. "REUSE IN INDUSTRY" .-> nodeD
```

**Comparison Table: Cradle-to-Cradle vs. Cradle-to-Grave**

| Parameter | Cradle-to-Grave (Linear) | Cradle-to-Cradle (Circular) |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Flow** | One-way: raw material → product → waste | Closed loop: waste becomes input |
| **Philosophy** | "End-of-life" disposal | "Cradle-to-cradle" cycles |
| **Waste Status** | Waste exists | No waste — only nutrients |
| **Resource Use** | Continuous virgin extraction | Perpetual reuse of materials |
| **Environmental Goal** | Minimize impact | Create positive impact |
| **Energy Focus** | Fossil-fuel dominant | Renewable energy preferred |
| **Example** | Single-use plastic bottle | Returnable glass bottle |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the two cycles in C2C. **Remember:** Biological cycle = organic/biodegradable materials; Technical cycle = synthetic/metallic materials. Mixing them up costs **2–3 marks**.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** What is **Industrial Ecology**? Explain with reference to the **Kalundborg Symbiosis** as a case study. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Lean-Green Manufacturing** integration approach. How does it help achieve sustainable manufacturing goals? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Industrial Ecology (IE)** is a concept model that views industrial systems as **analogous to natural ecosystems**. In nature, the waste of one organism is the food of another. IE applies the same principle to industries: the **by-products, waste heat, or waste materials of one industry become valuable inputs for another industry**.

**Key Features of Industrial Ecology:**
- **Material and energy flow analysis** across multiple firms.
- **Closed-loop material cycles** at the industrial-park or regional level.
- **Symbiotic inter-firm relationships** (Industrial Symbiosis).
- **System-level optimization** rather than firm-level optimization.

**Case Study: The Kalundborg Symbiosis, Denmark**

Kalundborg is a small Danish town that has evolved into the world's first **industrial eco-park** (since the 1970s). The network includes:

| Member Industry | Output (By-product) | Recipient Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Asnæs Power Station** | Steam, fly ash, gypsum | Novo Nordisk (pharma), local farms, drywall makers |
| **Novo Nordisk** | Sludge, yeast | Local farmers (as fertilizer/animal feed) |
| **Statoil Refinery** | Surplus gas | Gyproc (gypsum board manufacturer) |
| **Fish Farm** | Waste heat, cooling water | Asnæs Power Station |
| **Soil Remediation Plant** | Treated water | Local municipality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Outcomes of Kalundborg:**
> - Annual savings: ~ **24 million USD**
> - CO₂ emissions reduced by ~ **240,000 tonnes/year**
> - Water consumption reduced by ~ **3.6 million m³/year**
>
> Kalundborg is the **gold-standard reference case study** for Industrial Ecology and is **almost always expected** in KTU answers.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Lean Manufacturing** is a systematic approach developed from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** that focuses on **eliminating waste (muda)** to improve productivity, quality, and cost. The **8 wastes** eliminated by lean are: defects, overproduction, waiting, transport, inventory, motion, over-processing, and unused talent.

**Green Manufacturing** focuses on **reducing the environmental burden** of production — emissions, effluents, hazardous materials, and energy intensity.

**Lean-Green Integration** is the synergistic combination of these two philosophies. The rationale is that **many environmental wastes overlap with lean wastes**:

| Lean Waste (Muda) | Corresponding Green Waste |
|---|---|
| Overproduction | Excess energy/material consumption |
| Inventory | Material degradation, obsolescence |
| Defects | Rework → extra resource use |
| Transportation | Fuel emissions |
| Waiting | Energy wasted on idle machines |
| Over-processing | Excessive material/energy use |

**Benefits of Lean-Green Integration:**
1. **Cost Reduction** — Reduced resource use lowers operating costs.
2. **Environmental Compliance** — Lower emissions and waste ensure regulatory compliance.
3. **Brand Value** — Eco-conscious products improve market positioning.
4. **Employee Engagement** — Workers participate in both efficiency and environmental improvement.
5. **Synergy** — A single Kaizen event can target both lean and green wastes simultaneously.

**Implementation Steps:**
1. Conduct **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** that includes environmental metrics.
2. Identify **lean wastes that also have environmental implications**.
3. Apply **5S, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke** with an environmental lens.
4. Measure using **Eco-Efficiency indicators (V/I)**.
5. Use **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycles for continuous improvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Lean-Green):** Students often list lean and green wastes separately, missing the **mapping** between them. KTU specifically tests the **integration logic** — losing the integration table costs **3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Concept Models and Various Approaches"**

- ✅ **Concept Model** = abstract framework that *translates sustainability vision into actionable engineering practice*.
- ✅ **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** — Planet, Profit, People — *foundational model, by Elkington (1994)*.
- ✅ **6R Framework** — Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Redesign, Remanufacture — *most operationally used model in KTU exams*.
- ✅ **Cradle-to-Cradle (C2C)** — *Waste = Food*, biological and technical cycles, by Braungart & McDonough (2002).
- ✅ **Industrial Ecology** — *Ecosystem analogy*, **Kalundborg (Denmark)** is the must-know case study.
- ✅ **The Natural Step (TNS)** — Four System Conditions, by Karl-Henrik Robèrt.
- ✅ **Eco-Efficiency (V/I)** — *Doing more with less*; Eco-Effectiveness — *Doing the right things*.
- ✅ **Factor 4 / Factor 10** — Quantitative targets for resource productivity improvement.
- ✅ **Green Manufacturing** focuses on environment; **Lean Manufacturing** focuses on cost/waste; **Lean-Green** integrates both.
- ✅ **Agile Manufacturing** supports sustainability via mass customization and reduced overproduction.
- ✅ **Cloud Manufacturing** & **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** enable real-time resource optimization.
- ✅ **Key metrics to memorize:** Carbon Footprint, Material Efficiency (ME), Energy Efficiency (η), Recycling Rate (RR), Material Circularity Indicator (MCI), E-Factor, PMI.
- ✅ **MCI formula:** $\vert MCI = 1 - F_{L} \vert$, with $0 \leq MCI \leq 1$.
- ✅ **In C2C diagrams** — never mix biological and technical cycles; they are **separate loops**.
- ✅ **Order of preference in 6R:** Redesign > Reduce > Reuse > Remanufacture > Recycle > Recover.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question, structure the answer as: (1) **Definition** (1 mark), (2) **Key features / components** (3 marks), (3) **Diagram or schematic** (3 marks), (4) **Industrial example or case study** (3 marks), (5) **Comparison or advantages** (2 marks), (6) **Conclusion** (2 marks). This distribution maximizes marks consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lifecycle of Products/Services from Development to End of Life Stages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT612 - SUSTAINABLE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lifecycle of Products/Services from Development to End of Life Stages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Product Life Cycle (PLC)

The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is the sequence of stages a product passes through from its initial conception and development, through market introduction, growth, maturity, and decline, to its ultimate end-of-life (EOL) disposition. In the context of **Sustainable Manufacturing**, the PLC is examined holistically — from the extraction of raw materials (**cradle**) through manufacturing, use, and final disposition (**grave** or **next cradle** in a circular economy).

According to the **ISO 14040 / ISO 14044** standards, the life cycle of a product is defined as the "consecutive and interlinked stages of a product system, from raw material acquisition or generation from natural resources to final disposal."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course OESFT612 treats the product life cycle not as a commercial/sales concept, but as a **sustainability engineering framework**. The emphasis is on the **engineering decisions** at each stage that influence the environmental, economic, and social footprint of a product.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Service Life Cycle (SLC)

A **Service Life Cycle** describes the equivalent sequence of stages for a service offering, from concept and design of the service, through its delivery and operation, to its eventual discontinuation or redesign. The SLC in sustainable manufacturing includes the **Product-Service System (PSS)** approach, where tangible products are bundled with services to **decouple economic growth from resource consumption**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "A Product is Like a Living Organism"

Think of a product as a **human life**:

- **Conception (Development):** Like parents planning a child — designers conceive the product, choose materials, simulate performance.
- **Birth (Introduction):** The product is "born" into the market — small volumes, high costs.
- **Childhood (Growth):** Sales rise rapidly, the product gains acceptance.
- **Adulthood (Maturity):** The product is in its prime — stable sales, intense competition.
- **Old Age (Decline):** Sales fall, technology becomes obsolete.
- **Death (End of Life):** The product is retired — but unlike a human, it can be **reincarnated** through recycling, remanufacturing, or reuse.

> [!NOTE]
> The key insight from a sustainability perspective: A product's **environmental impact is largely "locked in" during the design/development stage**, even though most of the energy and material consumption occurs later. Sustainable manufacturing therefore places enormous weight on **Design for Environment (DfE)**.

## 1.4 Scope of This Module Topic

This topic covers the following life cycle stages in a sustainable manufacturing context:

1. **Development / Pre-production stage**
2. **Material extraction & processing**
3. **Manufacturing / Production**
4. **Distribution & Packaging**
5. **Use / Service stage**
6. **End-of-Life (EOL) stage** — Reuse, Repair, Refurbish, Remanufacture, Recycle, Recover, Dispose

Standard **bold metrics** used across this topic:

- **EOL Recovery Rate (R):** Fraction of product mass recovered at end of life (dimensionless, expressed as **%**)
- **Recyclability Index (RI):** Measure of how easily a product can be recycled (**0 to 1**)
- **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED):** Total primary energy consumed over the life cycle (**MJ** or **GJ**)
- **Global Warming Potential (GWP):** Equivalent CO₂ emissions (**kg CO₂-eq**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Product/Service Life Cycle

Each stage is described below with its **engineering activities**, **sustainability levers**, and **typical metrics**.

### Stage 1 — Product/Service Development (Conception & Design)

This is the **most influential stage** from a sustainability standpoint. Decisions made here propagate across the entire life cycle.

**Key activities:**
- Concept generation, QFD (Quality Function Deployment)
- **Design for Environment (DfE)** — Design for Disassembly, Design for Recycling, Design for Remanufacturing
- **Design for X (DfX):** A family of methodologies — DfAssembly, DfDisassembly, DfRecycling, DfRemanufacturing, DfReuse
- Material selection using **eco-indicator 99** or **CML 2001** impact assessment
- Prototyping, Computer-Aided Design (CAD), virtual simulation

**Sustainability levers:**
- Selection of low-impact materials (bio-based, recycled-content)
- Minimisation of material diversity (mono-material designs ease recycling)
- Modular design for easy upgrade, repair, and disassembly
- Lightweighting to reduce transport emissions

### Stage 2 — Material Extraction & Processing

**Key activities:**
- Mining, refining of ores, harvesting of biomass
- Pre-processing of raw materials (smelting, polymerisation, etc.)

**Sustainability concerns:**
- **Embodied energy** of materials (e.g., aluminium ≈ **170 MJ/kg** primary, vs. **8 MJ/kg** recycled)
- Habitat destruction, water use, toxic emissions
- Conflict minerals, child labour, supply chain ethics

### Stage 3 — Manufacturing / Production

**Key activities:**
- Forming, machining, joining, assembly, surface treatment

**Sustainability levers:**
- **Lean manufacturing** (waste minimisation — muda)
- **Green manufacturing** processes (dry machining, cryogenic cooling)
- **Additive manufacturing** (lower material wastage — up to **90%** material efficiency)
- Renewable energy in production
- Closed-loop coolant and lubricant systems

### Stage 4 — Distribution & Packaging

**Key activities:**
- Warehousing, transport, retail packaging

**Sustainability levers:**
- Optimised logistics, modal shift (rail/sea vs. air)
- Minimal/reusable/compostable packaging
- Reverse logistics for take-back

### Stage 5 — Use / Service Stage

**Key activities:**
- Product operation, maintenance, repair, upgrades

**Sustainability levers:**
- **Product-Service Systems (PSS):** Sell function, not product (e.g., "light-as-a-service" by Philips, "car-sharing")
- Energy-efficient operation (e.g., **EcoDesign Directive 2009/125/EC**)
- Maintenance for life extension
- Software updates to avoid premature obsolescence

### Stage 6 — End-of-Life (EOL)

The EOL stage offers the highest **circular-economy value** and is the focus of intense KTU examination.

The EOL hierarchy, in order of **descending sustainability preference**, is often called the **"9 R's" framework**:

1. **Refuse** — Don't make it
2. **Rethink** — Make it a service
3. **Reduce** — Use less material/energy
4. **Reuse** — Use again for the same function
5. **Repair** — Fix and continue use
6. **Refurbish** — Restore to good working condition
7. **Remanufacture** — Rebuild to as-new specification
8. **Repurpose** — Use in a different application
9. **Recycle** — Reprocess into raw materials
10. **Recover** — Energy recovery (incineration with energy capture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Most-Tested Concept:** The "**9 R's**" (or the 3R / 4R / 5R / 9R variants) is a guaranteed high-yield question. Students must list, rank, and justify the hierarchy.

### EOL Disposition Options

| EOL Option | Definition | Sustainability Profile |
|------------|------------|------------------------|
| Reuse | Product used again in same function | **Highest** value retention |
| Repair | Restore defective product to working order | High |
| Refurbish | Cosmetic/functional restoration | Medium-high |
| Remanufacture | Disassemble, inspect, rebuild to spec | Medium-high |
| Recycle | Reprocess into secondary raw materials | Medium |
| Incinerate (w/ energy recovery) | Burn with energy capture | Low-medium |
| Landfill | Disposal in engineered landfill | **Lowest** (last resort) |

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage

**Why** the life cycle must be considered as a system:
- **Burden shifting:** A "green" decision in one stage can shift impact to another (e.g., lightweight aluminium car body → lower use-phase fuel → higher production energy).
- **Lock-in effect:** 70–80% of life cycle cost and 50–80% of environmental impact are determined at the design stage (typical industry heuristic).

**How** engineers manage the life cycle:
- **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** — ISO 14040/14044
- **Life Cycle Costing (LCC)** — economic perspective
- **Social Life Cycle Assessment (S-LCA)** — UNEP/SETAC guidelines
- **Design for X (DfX)** family of tools
- **Material Flow Analysis (MFA)** and **Substance Flow Analysis (SFA)**
- **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** — regulatory mechanism

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|------------------|----------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Bass Diffusion Model (cumulative adoptions) | $N(t) = m \cdot \dfrac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p}e^{-(p+q)t}}$ | adopters | $m$ = market potential, $p$ = innovation coeff., $q$ = imitation coeff. |
| 2 | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | dimensionless | $\eta$ = scale (characteristic life), $\beta$ = shape |
| 3 | Hazard Rate (Bathtub Curve) | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | per unit time | $\beta < 1$: infant mortality, $\beta = 1$: random, $\beta > 1$: wear-out |
| 4 | EOL Recovery Rate | $R = \dfrac{m_{\text{recovered}}}{m_{\text{produced}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Mass of material recovered ÷ mass originally produced |
| 5 | Recycled Content Fraction | $RC = \dfrac{m_{\text{recycled input}}}{m_{\text{total input}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Used in LCA inventory |
| 6 | Recyclability Index | $RI = \sum_{i} \dfrac{m_i \cdot r_i}{m_{\text{total}}}$ | dimensionless | $r_i$ = recyclability factor of material $i$ (0 to 1) |
| 7 | Cumulative Energy Demand | $CED = \sum_{j} E_{j,\text{primary}}$ | **MJ** or **GJ** | Sum across all life cycle stages |
| 8 | Material Circularity Indicator (MCI) | $MCI = 1 - \dfrac{F_{\text{in}} + F_{\text{out}} - V_{\text{used}}}{2M + F_{\text{recycled}} - F_{\text{recycled,in}}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Ellen MacArthur Foundation / Granta Design |
| 9 | Product Life Cycle Cost (LCC) | $LCC = C_{\text{acq}} + \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{C_{\text{op}}(t) + C_{\text{maint}}(t)}{(1+r)^{t}} - \dfrac{S(T)}{(1+r)^{T}}$ | currency | $r$ = discount rate, $S$ = salvage value |
| 10 | Embodied Energy Savings (Recycling) | $\Delta E = E_{\text{virgin}} - E_{\text{recycled}}$ | **MJ/kg** | Compare virgin vs. recycled material |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU rule on tables:** When absolute value is needed (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` rather than the raw vertical bar to prevent breaking the markdown table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** End-of-Life Vehicles (ELV) Directive 2000/53/EC mandates **95%** reuse/recovery and **85%** recycling by mass.
- **Electronics:** Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) Directive 2012/19/EU sets collection and recovery targets.
- **Packaging:** EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Directive — recovery ≥ **60%**, recycling ≥ **55%**.
- **Buildings:** Whole-Life Carbon Assessment (WLCA) per RICS 2023 guidance.
- **Medical devices:** ISO 18562 series on bio-compatibility across life cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bass Diffusion Model (Product Adoption in PLC)

The Bass model describes how a new product is adopted in a market over time, predicting the **S-curve** of cumulative adoption that is central to understanding the **growth and maturity** stages of the PLC.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption

At any time $t$, the rate of new adoptions $n(t)$ is proportional to two factors:
- The remaining non-adopters $(m - N(t))$, where $m$ is the eventual market potential and $N(t)$ is the cumulative number of adopters.
- A constant innovation coefficient $p$ plus an imitation effect proportional to the existing adopters $N(t)$.

This yields the differential equation:

$$\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left(p + q \cdot \dfrac{N(t)}{m}\right)\left(m - N(t)\right)$$

### 3.1.2 Solving the Differential Equation

Step 1: Expand the right-hand side.

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = p(m - N) + \dfrac{q}{m}N(m - N)$$

Step 2: Expand and separate.

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = pm - pN + qN - \dfrac{qN^{2}}{m} = pm - (p-q)N - \dfrac{qN^{2}}{m}$$

Step 3: This is a Bernoulli / Riccati-type ODE. Introduce substitution and use standard integration. The closed-form solution is:

$$N(t) = m \cdot \dfrac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p}e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

Step 4: The instantaneous (non-cumulative) adoption rate is obtained by differentiating:

$$n(t) = \dfrac{dN}{dt} = m \cdot \dfrac{(p+q)^{2}}{p} \cdot \dfrac{e^{-(p+q)t}}{\left[1 + \dfrac{q}{p}e^{-(p+q)t}\right]^{2}}$$

Step 5: Time of peak adoption (inflection point of the cumulative S-curve):

$$t^{\star} = \dfrac{1}{p+q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)$$

**Interpretation:** This $t^{\star}$ marks the transition from the **growth** stage to the **maturity** stage in the product life cycle.

### 3.1.3 Worked Example

A new electric vehicle has $m = 1{,}000{,}000$ potential buyers, $p = 0.01$, $q = 0.40$.

$$t^{\star} = \dfrac{1}{0.41}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.40}{0.01}\right) = \dfrac{1}{0.41}\ln(40) = \dfrac{3.689}{0.41} \approx 9.0 \text{ years}$$

So the product enters maturity at ~**9 years** after launch.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bathtub Curve and Weibull Reliability

The **bathtub curve** plots hazard rate $h(t)$ vs. time. It has three regions:
- **Infant mortality** ($\beta < 1$): defects, manufacturing flaws.
- **Useful life** ($\beta = 1$): random failures (constant $h$).
- **Wear-out** ($\beta > 1$): aging, fatigue, corrosion.

### 3.2.1 Weibull Reliability Function

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

Step 1: Probability density function:

$$f(t) = -\dfrac{dR}{dt} = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

Step 2: Hazard rate:

$$h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

Step 3: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

where $\Gamma$ is the gamma function.

### 3.2.2 Worked Reliability Example

A ball bearing has $\eta = 8000$ hours, $\beta = 2.5$.

Reliability at $t = 5000$ hours:

$$R(5000) = e^{-(5000/8000)^{2.5}} = e^{-(0.625)^{2.5}}$$

$$(0.625)^{2.5} = 0.625^{2} \cdot 0.625^{0.5} = 0.3906 \cdot 0.7906 = 0.3087$$

$$R(5000) = e^{-0.3087} \approx 0.7345 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 73.45\%$$

So **73.45%** of bearings survive 5000 hours.

## 3.3 End-of-Life Material Recovery Calculation

### 3.3.1 The Recovery Rate Formula

$$R = \dfrac{m_{\text{recovered}}}{m_{\text{produced}}} \times 100$$

For a product with multiple material streams:

$$R = \dfrac{\sum_{i} m_{i,\text{recovered}}}{\sum_{i} m_{i,\text{produced}}} \times 100$$

### 3.3.2 Worked Example

A refrigerator contains:
- Steel: 35 kg (recovery factor = **0.92**)
- Plastics: 12 kg (recovery factor = **0.55**)
- Aluminium: 4 kg (recovery factor = **0.85**)
- Copper: 2 kg (recovery factor = **0.90**)
- Hazardous refrigerant: 0.5 kg (recovery factor = **0.95**)

Step 1: Total recovered mass.

$$m_{\text{recovered}} = (35)(0.92) + (12)(0.55) + (4)(0.85) + (2)(0.90) + (0.5)(0.95)$$

$$= 32.2 + 6.6 + 3.4 + 1.8 + 0.475 = 44.475 \text{ kg}$$

Step 2: Total produced mass.

$$m_{\text{produced}} = 35 + 12 + 4 + 2 + 0.5 = 53.5 \text{ kg}$$

Step 3: Recovery rate.

$$R = \dfrac{44.475}{53.5} \times 100 \approx 83.13\%$$

This **83.13%** falls short of the EU ELV Directive target of 95%, signalling a design improvement opportunity.

## 3.4 Material Circularity Indicator (MCI) — Step-by-Step

The **MCI** (Ellen MacArthur Foundation / Granta Design) measures how circular the material flows are.

$$\text{MCI} = 1 - \dfrac{F_{\text{in}} + F_{\text{out}} - V_{\text{used}}}{2M + F_{\text{recycled}} - F_{\text{recycled,in}}}$$

Where:
- $F_{\text{in}}$ = mass of virgin material input
- $F_{\text{out}}$ = mass of material leaving the system to landfill/incineration
- $V_{\text{used}}$ = mass of recycled/reused content added to product
- $M$ = mass of product
- $F_{\text{recycled}}$ = mass of output flow recycled
- $F_{\text{recycled,in}}$ = mass of input flow that is recycled

MCI = 0 → fully linear; MCI = 1 → fully circular.

**Worked Example (smartphone):**

Given:
- $F_{\text{in}} = 0.15$ kg virgin materials
- $F_{\text{out}} = 0.10$ kg to landfill
- $V_{\text{used}} = 0.05$ kg recycled content
- $M = 0.20$ kg
- $F_{\text{recycled}} = 0.10$ kg recycled
- $F_{\text{recycled,in}} = 0.05$ kg recycled input

$$\text{MCI} = 1 - \dfrac{0.15 + 0.10 - 0.05}{2(0.20) + 0.10 - 0.05} = 1 - \dfrac{0.20}{0.45} = 1 - 0.444 = 0.556$$

The smartphone has an **MCI of 0.556**, indicating moderate circularity — substantial room for improvement (e.g., modular design, take-back schemes).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Stage-wise Sustainability Levers

| Stage | Primary Environmental Concern | Sustainability Levers | Key Metric |
|-------|-------------------------------|----------------------|------------|
| Development | Lock-in of all downstream impacts | DfE, DfX, eco-design | Eco-indicator score |
| Material Extraction | Resource depletion, habitat loss | Recycled content, bio-based | CED (MJ/kg) |
| Manufacturing | Energy use, emissions, waste | Lean, green processes | kg CO₂-eq / unit |
| Distribution | Transport emissions | Modal shift, optimisation | kg CO₂-eq / t·km |
| Use | Energy/water consumption | Eco-design, PSS | kWh / year |
| End-of-Life | Landfill, pollution | 9R hierarchy, EPR | Recovery rate (%) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Define the **Product Life Cycle (PLC)**. List the **six stages** of a product life cycle as understood in sustainable manufacturing.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is the complete sequence of stages a product passes through from the extraction of raw materials to its final end-of-life disposition, encompassing environmental, economic, and social impacts. In the context of sustainable manufacturing, the PLC is examined as a **holistic systems framework** rather than a mere marketing/sales concept.
>
> The six stages are:
>
> 1. **Product Development & Design** — concept, eco-design, DfX.
> 2. **Material Extraction & Processing** — mining, refining, harvesting.
> 3. **Manufacturing / Production** — forming, machining, assembly.
> 4. **Distribution & Packaging** — logistics, retail.
> 5. **Use / Service Stage** — operation, maintenance, repair.
> 6. **End-of-Life** — recovery, recycling, reuse, disposal.
>
> The engineering significance is that **most of the life cycle impact is "locked in" at the design stage**, making stage 1 the highest-leverage intervention point.
>
> *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for correct six-stage listing.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** What is the **9 R's framework**? List and rank the hierarchy from most to least preferred.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The 9 R's framework is a **waste hierarchy** used in circular economy and sustainable manufacturing to rank end-of-life strategies from most to least preferred based on value retention and environmental benefit.
>
> Ranking (most → least preferred):
>
> 1. **Refuse** — Do not produce.
> 2. **Rethink** — Replace with a service.
> 3. **Reduce** — Use less material/energy.
> 4. **Reuse** — Same function, second use.
> 5. **Repair** — Restore to working order.
> 6. **Refurbish** — Cosmetic/functional restoration.
> 7. **Remanufacture** — Rebuild to as-new spec.
> 8. **Repurpose** — Use in a different application.
> 9. **Recycle** — Reprocess into raw material.
> 10. **Recover** — Energy recovery (incineration with energy capture).
>
> Landfill is the absolute last resort, falling outside the 9 R's.
>
> *(3 marks — 1.5 for list, 1.5 for justification of hierarchy.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the **six stages of the Product Life Cycle (PLC)** in detail, with emphasis on the **sustainability interventions** available at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A. (b)** Discuss the **9 R's / End-of-Life hierarchy** with a comparative table. For a **mobile phone**, justify which 2–3 strategies are most applicable and why. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A.(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Product Life Cycle, in the sustainable manufacturing context, is a **systems-engineering framework** that maps every input, output, and impact from "cradle" (raw material) to "grave" (end-of-life) — or, ideally, to the next "cradle" in a circular economy.

**Stage 1 — Development & Design (1.5 marks):**
- Concept generation, **Design for Environment (DfE)**, **Design for X (DfX)** family (DfDisassembly, DfRecycling, DfRemanufacturing).
- **70–80%** of life cycle cost and **50–80%** of environmental impact are determined here.
- Sustainability levers: material selection (recycled content), modular design, mono-material design, lightweighting.

**Stage 2 — Material Extraction (1 mark):**
- Mining, refining, harvesting. **Embodied energy** concerns (e.g., primary aluminium ≈ 170 MJ/kg).
- Sustainability levers: recycled feedstock, bio-based materials, certified sustainable sourcing (e.g., FSC wood, conflict-free minerals).

**Stage 3 — Manufacturing (1 mark):**
- Forming, machining, joining, assembly. Energy and waste are highest here.
- Sustainability levers: **Lean manufacturing**, **green processes** (dry machining, cryogenics), **additive manufacturing** (up to 90% material efficiency), renewable energy.

**Stage 4 — Distribution & Packaging (0.5 marks):**
- Logistics and retail. Sustainability levers: modal shift (rail/sea vs. air), reusable/compostable packaging, route optimisation.

**Stage 5 — Use Stage (1 mark):**
- Operation, maintenance, repair, upgrade. Often dominates life cycle impact for energy-using products (e.g., cars, refrigerators).
- Sustainability levers: **Product-Service Systems (PSS)**, software updates, preventive maintenance, design for upgrade.

**Stage 6 — End-of-Life (1 mark):**
- Disposition options ranked by 9 R's. Sustainability levers: **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)**, take-back schemes, design for disassembly, recyclability Index optimisation.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
Sustainable manufacturing requires a **stage-aware, interventionist** approach, with maximum leverage at the design stage.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A.(b)

**Comparative Table (3 marks):**

| EOL Option | Description | Value Retention | Energy Demand | Applicability to Mobile Phone |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|---------------|------------------------------|
| Reuse | Same function, second user | Highest | Very low | **High** (refurbished phone market) |
| Repair | Fix defect | High | Low | **High** (modular repair is feasible) |
| Refurbish | Cosmetic + functional restoration | High | Low-medium | **High** (screen, battery, housing) |
| Remanufacture | Rebuild to as-new | High | Medium | Low (mobile phones rarely remanufactured) |
| Recycle | Material recovery | Medium | Medium | **High** (Au, Cu, Co, Pd, Li) |
| Recover (Energy) | Incineration w/ energy capture | Low | N/A | Low (illegal in many regions; WEEE) |
| Landfill | Disposal | Lowest | N/A | Avoid (WEEE Directive) |

**Justification for a Mobile Phone (4 marks):**

For a mobile phone, the most applicable 9 R's strategies are:

1. **Reuse (Refurbishment for Resale):** The refurbished phone market is robust (e.g., Apple Refurbished, Cashify). Reuse is highest value retention since the embodied energy and rare-earth content are preserved.

2. **Repair (Modular Repair):** Phones with modular designs (e.g., Fairphone) can be repaired — battery, screen, camera modules replaced. Repair extends useful life by **2–3 years** on average.

3. **Recycle (Material Recovery):** Urban mining of mobile phones recovers gold (~**30 mg/phone**), copper, cobalt, palladium, lithium. Recycling addresses the **critical raw materials** risk flagged by the EU.

**Why not remanufacture?** Mobile phones have very short technology refresh cycles (12–24 months) and rapidly evolving specs, making remanufacture economically unviable.

**Conclusion:** A combined **Reuse → Repair → Recycle** strategy is the optimal pathway for mobile phones, supported by **WEEE Directive** compliance and producer take-back schemes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. (a)** With the help of the **Bass Diffusion Model**, explain how a new sustainable product (e.g., electric vehicle) progresses through the **growth and maturity** stages of its life cycle. Derive the time of peak adoption. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B. (b)** A washing machine contains the following material stream: **Steel 40 kg, Plastics 18 kg, Aluminium 6 kg, Copper 3 kg, Hazardous electronics 1.5 kg.** The EOL recovery factors are: Steel 0.92, Plastics 0.50, Aluminium 0.85, Copper 0.88, Electronics 0.80. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Calculate the **overall EOL recovery rate**.
- (ii) Comment on whether this complies with the **EU WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU** target of **80% recovery by mass** for large household appliances.
- (iii) Suggest **two design modifications** to improve the recovery rate.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B.(a)

**Introduction to Bass Model (2 marks):**

The Bass Diffusion Model (Frank Bass, 1969) describes the timing and magnitude of new product adoption. It splits adopters into:
- **Innovators** (driven by innovation coefficient $p$)
- **Imitators** (driven by word-of-mouth, imitation coefficient $q$)

The governing ODE is:

$$\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left(p + q \cdot \dfrac{N(t)}{m}\right)\left(m - N(t)\right)$$

**Solution & S-Curve (2 marks):**

The closed-form solution is:

$$N(t) = m \cdot \dfrac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p}e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

This produces a classic **S-shaped cumulative adoption curve** that maps directly onto the PLC's growth and maturity stages:
- **Growth stage:** Imitation dominates, $N(t)$ rises steeply.
- **Maturity stage:** Market saturates as $N(t) \to m$.

**Derivation of Peak Adoption Time (2 marks):**

Setting $\dfrac{d^{2}N}{dt^{2}} = 0$ at the inflection point yields:

$$t^{\star} = \dfrac{1}{p+q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)$$

**Numerical Example (1 mark):** For an electric vehicle with $p = 0.01$, $q = 0.40$:

$$t^{\star} = \dfrac{1}{0.41}\ln(40) = \dfrac{3.689}{0.41} \approx 9.0 \text{ years}$$

Hence the EV enters maturity roughly **9 years** after launch.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B.(b)

**(i) Recovery Rate Calculation (3 marks):**

| Material | Mass (kg) | Recovery Factor | Mass Recovered (kg) |
|----------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Steel | 40 | 0.92 | 36.80 |
| Plastics | 18 | 0.50 | 9.00 |
| Aluminium | 6 | 0.85 | 5.10 |
| Copper | 3 | 0.88 | 2.64 |
| Electronics | 1.5 | 0.80 | 1.20 |
| **Total** | **68.5** | — | **54.74** |

$$R = \dfrac{54.74}{68.5} \times 100 \approx 79.91\%$$

**(ii) Compliance Comment (2 marks):**

The achieved recovery rate of **79.91%** falls just **below** the EU WEEE Directive target of **80%** for large household appliances. The design is **non-compliant** by a narrow margin (0.09 percentage points). A small design improvement — for example, increasing the plastics recovery factor from 0.50 to 0.55 — would push it above the threshold.

**(iii) Design Modifications (2 marks):**

1. **Material substitution and labelling:** Replace multi-layer plastic housings with **mono-material polypropylene (PP)** that is easily sorted and recycled. Recovery factor of PP could rise to 0.80+.
2. **Design for Disassembly (DfD):** Replace glued/adhesive joints with **snap-fit or screw connections** so that hazardous electronic modules can be separated cleanly, raising the electronics recovery factor from 0.80 to ~0.95.

**Additional options** (for bonus credit): Use of **digital product passports** to track material composition; integration of **reverse-logistics** take-back schemes; substitution of copper with aluminium in less-critical parts to consolidate the metal stream.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make**
>
> 1. **Confusing PLC (sustainability) with PLC (marketing):** Many students write about "Introduction-Growth-Maturity-Decline" as a sales concept rather than a **sustainability engineering** framework. **Lose 2–3 marks** if engineering interventions (DfE, DfX, LCA) are absent.
>
> 2. **Mixing up the 9 R's order:** Ranks frequently reversed (e.g., "Recycle" listed before "Reuse"). Hierarchy must be **value-retention-based** in descending order.
>
> 3. **In recovery-rate problems, students forget to multiply each material mass by its individual recovery factor** before summing. This is a **3-mark deduction** on a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 4. **In Bass model derivations,** students often **omit the derivation of $t^{\star}$**, presenting the formula without justification. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for "no derivation shown."
>
> 5. **Confusing Recycled Content (RC) with Recovery Rate (R):** RC is a property of the **input** stream; R is a property of the **output** stream. These are **not the same** and must not be used interchangeably.
>
> 6. **In Mermaid/diagram questions,** students sometimes draw the cradle-to-grave but omit the **cradle-to-cradle circular feedback** — missing the modern sustainable-manufacturing emphasis.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorise for KTU 2024 Exam**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **PLC = Cradle-to-Grave** sequence of all life stages of a product.
- **Service Life Cycle (SLC)** = Parallel framework for services, often integrated via **Product-Service Systems (PSS)**.
- **Design for X (DfX)** = Family of design methodologies (DfAssembly, DfDisassembly, DfRecycling, DfRemanufacturing).

### The Six Stages
- ☐ Development → Material Extraction → Manufacturing → Distribution → Use → End-of-Life.
- **~70–80%** of life cycle cost and **~50–80%** of environmental impact are determined at the **Design** stage.

### The 9 R's (Descending Sustainability Value)
- ☐ Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Refurbish → Remanufacture → Repurpose → Recycle → Recover. **Landfill = last resort.**

### Critical Formulae (Memorise with Units)
- ☐ **Recovery rate:** $R = \dfrac{m_{\text{recovered}}}{m_{\text{produced}}} \times 100$ (%)
- ☐ **Weibull reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$
- ☐ **Hazard rate:** $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$
- ☐ **Bass diffusion inflection:** $t^{\star} = \dfrac{1}{p+q}\ln(q/p)$
- ☐ **MCI:** $0 \to 1$ measure of circularity.

### Key Regulatory / Standards Framework
- ☐ **ISO 14040 / 14044** — LCA methodology.
- ☐ **EU ELV Directive 2000/53/EC** — 95% recovery, 85% recycling for vehicles.
- ☐ **EU WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU** — 80% recovery for large appliances.
- ☐ **EU Packaging Directive** — 60% recovery, 55% recycling.
- ☐ **EU EcoDesign Directive 2009/125/EC** — energy-using products.

### Sustainable Manufacturing Tools
- ☐ **LCA** (Life Cycle Assessment)
- ☐ **LCC** (Life Cycle Costing)
- ☐ **S-LCA** (Social LCA)
- ☐ **MFA / SFA** (Material / Substance Flow Analysis)
- ☐ **EPR** (Extended Producer Responsibility)

### Frequently Confused Pairs
- ☐ **Recycled Content (input)** ≠ **Recovery Rate (output)**.
- ☐ **Remanufacture** (rebuild to spec) ≠ **Refurbish** (cosmetic restoration).
- ☐ **Reuse** (same function) ≠ **Repurpose** (different function).
- ☐ **Cradle-to-Grave** (linear) ≠ **Cradle-to-Cradle** (circular).

### Real-World Numerical Benchmarks (Memorise for Comparative Answers)
- ☐ Primary aluminium embodied energy: **~170 MJ/kg** vs. recycled: **~8 MJ/kg**.
- ☐ Additive manufacturing material efficiency: **up to 90%**.
- ☐ Modular phone life extension via repair: **+2–3 years**.
- ☐ Gold in mobile phones: **~30 mg/phone** (urban mining).

### Diagram Essentials
